questions.question: A pregnant woman is Rh negative and her foetus is Rh positive. During the delivery of her first child, the mother's blood gets exposed to the Rh positive blood of the foetus. What happens as a result of this exposure?

options.A: The mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen in her blood
options.B: The foetus immediately develops erythroblastosis foetalis during the first pregnancy
options.C: The placenta breaks down and mixes both blood types permanently
options.D: The mother's RBCs are destroyed by the Rh antigens of the foetus

questions.explanation: When an Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive blood during the delivery of her first child, her immune system responds by producing antibodies against the Rh antigen. Option B is wrong because erythroblastosis foetalis affects subsequent pregnancies, not the first one — the NCERT clearly states the two bloods are well separated by the placenta in the first pregnancy. Option C is wrong because NCERT says the two bloods are separated by the placenta and there is only a 'possibility of exposure' during delivery, not permanent mixing. Option D is wrong because it is the mother who forms antibodies against Rh antigen; the destruction of foetal RBCs occurs only in subsequent pregnancies when these maternal antibodies cross the placenta.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: In such cases, the mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen in her blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During blood coagulation, fibrin threads form a network that traps dead and damaged blood cells to create a clot. Which substance is directly converted into fibrin by the enzyme thrombin?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that inactive fibrinogen is converted into fibrin by the enzyme thrombin. Option A is wrong because prothrombin is the inactive substance that is converted into thrombin, not into fibrin. Option C is wrong because thrombokinase is an enzyme complex that helps convert prothrombin into thrombin, not fibrinogen into fibrin. Option D is wrong because calcium ions play a supporting role in the clotting process but are not directly converted into fibrin.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Fibrins are formed by the conversion of inactive fibrinogens in the plasma by the enzyme thrombin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells in the human body exhibit amoeboid movement?

options.B: Ciliated epithelial cells and goblet cells
options.C: Cardiac muscle cells and smooth muscle cells

questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that macrophages and leucocytes exhibit amoeboid movement using pseudopodia formed by streaming of protoplasm. Option B is wrong because ciliated epithelial cells perform ciliary movement, not amoeboid movement. Option C is wrong because cardiac and smooth muscle cells perform muscular movement. Option D is wrong because spermatozoa use flagellar movement and ova are moved by ciliary movement.
questions.ncert_line: Some specialised cells in our body like macrophages and leucocytes in blood exhibit amoeboid movement.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of muscle is found in the inner walls of the alimentary canal?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that visceral (smooth) muscles line the inner walls of hollow visceral organs like the alimentary canal. Option B is wrong because skeletal muscles are associated with bones and control locomotion and body posture. Option C is wrong because cardiac muscles are found only in the heart. Option D is wrong because voluntary muscles is another name for skeletal muscles, which are under conscious control, while alimentary canal muscles are involuntary.
questions.ncert_line: Visceral muscles are located in the inner walls of hollow visceral organs of the body like the alimentary canal, reproductive tract, etc.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the embryonic (tissue) origin of muscle in the human body?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that muscle is a specialised tissue of mesodermal origin. Option B is wrong because ectodermal tissue gives rise to structures like skin and the nervous system, not muscles. Option C is wrong because endodermal tissue lines the gut and gives rise to internal organs like the liver and lungs, not muscles. Option D is wrong because neural crest cells give rise to specific structures like peripheral neurons and melanocytes, not muscles.
questions.ncert_line: Muscle is a specialised tissue of mesodermal origin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the myosin head has ATPase enzyme activity and also contains binding sites for actin?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 11, Chapter 20 - Locomotion and Movement), the globular head of the heavy meromyosin (HMM) is an active ATPase enzyme and carries binding sites for both ATP and actin — making option B correct. Option A is wrong because the light meromyosin (LMM) is the tail region and has no such enzymatic activity. Option C is wrong because tropomyosin is a regulatory protein of the thin (actin) filament, not the myosin filament. Option D is wrong because the Z line is a structural boundary of the sarcomere and is not involved in ATPase activity.
questions.ncert_line: The globular head is an active ATPase enzyme and has binding sites for ATP and active sites for actin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to the masking of active sites on actin filaments when calcium ions increase in the sarcoplasm during muscle contraction?

options.A: Tropomyosin directly binds to myosin and blocks the cross bridge
options.B: Calcium binds to a subunit of troponin and removes the masking of active sites for myosin
options.C: Calcium ions activate the Z line to release thin filaments
options.D: The M line contracts and exposes actin binding sites

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that increased calcium ions bind to a subunit of troponin on actin filaments, which removes the masking of the active sites for myosin — making option B correct. Option A is wrong because it is troponin (not tropomyosin directly) that interacts with calcium, and tropomyosin does not directly bind to myosin. Option C is wrong because the Z line is a structural element that anchors thin filaments and is not involved in unmasking actin sites. Option D is wrong because the M line holds thick filaments together in the centre of the A band and plays no role in exposing actin binding sites.
questions.ncert_line: Increase in Ca++ level leads to the binding of calcium with a subunit of troponin on actin filaments and thereby remove the masking of active sites for myosin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Growth in plants is called 'open form of growth' because new cells are always added to the plant body by the activity of which structure?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the meristem is responsible for the open form of growth by continuously adding new cells to the plant body. Epidermis is a protective outer layer and does not add new cells for growth. Cortex is a ground tissue involved in storage, not cell addition. Endodermis is a single layer surrounding the vascular bundle and has no role in adding new cells for plant growth.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: This form of growth wherein new cells are always being added to the plant body by the activity of the meristem is called the open form of growth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is the correct definition of growth as given in NCERT?

options.A: Irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ, its parts, or an individual cell
options.B: Reversible temporary increase in the volume of a cell or organ
options.C: Increase in the number of cells only, without any change in size
options.D: Decrease in dry weight accompanied by increase in fresh weight

questions.explanation: NCERT defines growth as an irreversible and permanent increase in size, which eliminates any idea of reversibility. Option B is wrong because growth is irreversible, not reversible or temporary. Option C is wrong because growth involves increase in size, not just cell number. Option D is wrong because it describes a decrease in dry weight, which is not the definition of growth.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: Growth can be defined as an irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ or its parts or even of an individual cell.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In dicotyledonous plants and gymnosperms, which meristems are responsible for the increase in girth of organs?

options.A: Lateral meristems — vascular cambium and cork-cambium
options.B: Root apical meristem and shoot apical meristem
options.C: Intercalary meristem and root apical meristem

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that lateral meristems — specifically vascular cambium and cork-cambium — cause the increase in girth, which is called secondary growth. Option B is wrong because root apical meristem and shoot apical meristem are responsible for primary growth (elongation), not girth increase. Option C is wrong because intercalary meristem is found at the base of internodes and does not cause girth increase. Option D is wrong because shoot apical meristem contributes to primary elongation, not secondary growth.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: In dicotyledonous plants and gymnosperms, the lateral meristems, vascular cambium and cork-cambium appear later in life. These are the meristems that cause the increase in the girth of the organs in which they are active. This is known as secondary growth of the plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which characteristic feature is associated with cells in the elongation phase of growth?

options.A: Increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition
options.B: Rich protoplasm, large nuclei and thin cellulosic walls
options.C: Maximum wall thickening and protoplasmic modifications
options.D: Rapid mitotic divisions and abundant plasmodesmatal connections

questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition are the hallmarks of the elongation phase. Option B describes cells of the meristematic phase, which are rich in protoplasm and have large conspicuous nuclei. Option C describes the maturation phase, where cells attain maximal size with wall thickening and protoplasmic modifications. Option D also belongs to the meristematic phase, which is characterised by constant cell division and plasmodesmatal connections.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: Increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition are the characteristics of the cells in this phase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In arithmetic growth, what happens to the two daughter cells produced after mitotic cell division?

options.A: Both daughter cells continue to divide repeatedly
options.B: Only one daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates and matures
options.C: Both daughter cells differentiate and mature without further division
options.D: One daughter cell undergoes apoptosis while the other continues to divide

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in arithmetic growth only one daughter cell keeps dividing while the other differentiates and matures, producing a constant linear increase. Option A describes geometric growth, where both progeny cells retain the ability to divide. Option C is incorrect because in arithmetic growth at least one cell still divides. Option D introduces apoptosis, which is not mentioned in this context in the NCERT text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: In arithmetic growth, following mitotic cell division, only one daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates and matures.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who is revered as the Father of Ecology in India?
questions.explanation: Ramdeo Misra (1908–1998) is indeed revered as the Father of Ecology in India. He made pioneering contributions to plant ecology and established ecology as a discipline in India. W. H. Pearsall was his PhD supervisor at Leeds University. The answer B is correct, but the chapter attribution is wrong — this fact appears in the NCERT Biology Class 12 chapter on Organisms and Populations (or the introductory ecology chapters), not in 'Biotechnology: Principles and Processes'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ramdeo Misra is revered as the Father of Ecology in India.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: With how many levels of biological organisation is Ecology basically concerned?

options.B: Three levels — populations, communities and biomes
options.C: Four levels — organisms, populations, communities and biomes
options.D: Five levels — cells, tissues, organs, populations and biomes

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Ecology is concerned with four levels of biological organisation: organisms, populations, communities, and biomes. Option A lists only two levels, which is incomplete. Option B lists three levels and misses organisms. Option D mentions cell, tissue, and organ levels which belong to a different level of biological organisation and are not the four levels of ecology. Note: This content is from the Ecology chapter (Chapter 13: Organisms and Populations), not Biotechnology.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ecology is basically concerned with four levels of biological organisation – organisms, populations, communities and biomes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where did Ramdeo Misra obtain his Ph.D in Ecology?
questions.explanation: The claimed correct answer C (Leeds University, UK) is factually correct based on NCERT Biology. Ramdeo Misra obtained his Ph.D in Ecology in 1937 under Prof. W. H. Pearsall, FRS, from Leeds University in UK. However, this topic belongs to the Ecology chapter (organisms and populations or biodiversity), NOT the chapter 'Biotechnology: Principles and Processes'. The explanation and NCERT line cited are accurate, but the chapter attribution is incorrect.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ramdeo Misra obtained Ph.D in Ecology (1937) under Prof. W. H. Pearsall, FRS, from Leeds University in UK.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a population, what does the shape of an age pyramid tell us?

options.A: The growth status of the population
options.B: The total number of individuals in the population
options.C: The sex ratio of the population
options.D: The birth rate of the population

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 13 - Organisms and Populations), the shape of the age pyramid reflects the growth status of a population — whether it is growing, stable, or decling. Option B is wrong because total number of individuals refers to population density (N), not what the pyramid shape shows. Option C is wrong because sex ratio is a separate population attribute, not what the pyramid shape indicates. Option D is wrong because birth rate is a different population attribute calculated as per capita births. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 13 - Organisms and Populations, not Biotechnology: Principles and Processes.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: The shape of the pyramids reflects the growth status of the population - (a) whether it is growing, (b) stable or (c) decling.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which situation is per cent cover or biomass a more meaningful measure of population density than total number?

options.A: When one species has a very large body or canopy size compared to others
options.B: When the population has an equal sex ratio
options.C: When the population is decling rapidly
options.D: When individuals of the population reproduce asexually

questions.explanation: NCERT uses the example of a huge banyan tree versus many carrot grass plants to explain that when one species has a very large body or canopy, counting numbers alone underestimates its role — so biomass or per cent cover is better. Option B is wrong because sex ratio is a separate population attribute unrelated to how density is measured. Option C is wrong because a decling population trend does not determine which measure of density is used. Option D is wrong because asexual reproduction affects how interbreeding is defined, not how population density is measured.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: In an area, if there are 200 carrot grass plants but only a single huge banyan tree with a large canopy, stating that the population density of banyan is low relative to that of carrot grass amounts to underestimating the enormous role of the Banyan in that community. In such cases, the per cent cover or biomass is a more meaningful measure of the population size.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Herbert Boyer observed that restriction enzymes of E. coli cut DNA strands in a particular fashion, leaving what on the strands?
questions.explanation: Restriction enzymes cut DNA strands leaving 'sticky ends' — single-stranded overhanging sequences that can form hydrogen bonds with complementary sequences. This property makes them useful in recombinant DNA technology. Option A (blunt ends) refers to a different type of cut where no overhangs are left. Option C (phosphate tails) is not a standard molecular biology term. Option D (hydrogen bonds) are chemical bonds within DNA structure, not the result of restriction enzyme cutting. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 11 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes) in NCERT Biology Class 12, not 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production'. The NCERT line cited is approximately correct in content, though the exact wording may vary from the actual textbook.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Boyer observed that these enzymes have the capability of cutting DNA strands in a particular fashion, which left what has became known as 'sticky ends' on the strands.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: According to the European Federation of Biotechnology (EFB), biotechnology is defined as the integration of natural science and organisms, cells, parts thereof, and molecular analogues for what purpose?
questions.explanation: The EFB (European Federation of Biotechnology) definition of biotechnology as given in NCERT Biology is: 'The integration of natural science and organisms, cells, parts thereof, and molecular analogues for products and services.' This definition appears in NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 (Biotechnology and its Applications) or the introductory biotechnology chapter. Option A (Products and services) is correct. Option B (Research and development) is not part of the EFB definition. Option C (Genetic modification only) is too narrow. Option D (Agricultural improvement) is not mentioned in the EFB definition.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: 'The integration of natural science and organisms, cells, parts thereof, and molecular analogues for products and services'.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Which of the following is one of the two core techniques that enabled the birth of modern biotechnology, as described in NCERT?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 11 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes), the two core techniques that enabled the birth of modern biotechnology are: (i) Genetic engineering - techniques to alter the chemistry of genetic material (DNA and RNA) and introduce these into host organisms to change their phenotype, and (ii) Bioprocess engineering - maintaing sterile (aseptic) ambience in chemical engineering processes to enable growth of only the desired microbe/eukaryotic cell in large quanties for the manufacture of biotechnological products. Note: This question belongs to Chapter 11 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes), not 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production' (Chapter 9). The claimed answer B (Bioprocess engineering) is correct.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Among many, the two core techniques that enabled birth of modern biotechnology are : (i) Genetic engineering ... (ii) Bioprocess engineering.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer constructed the first recombinant DNA molecule in 1972. Which organism's plasmid did they use to link the antibiotic resistance gene?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that the first recombinant DNA was made by linking an antibiotic resistance gene with a native plasmid of Salmonella typhimurium. Option A is wrong because E. coli was the host into which the recombinant DNA was later transferred, not the source of the plasmid. Option C (Bacillus subtilis) and Option D (Agrobacterium tumefaciens) are real bacteria used in biotechnology but are not mentioned in this context in the NCERT text. Note: This topic is covered in the Biotechnology chapter (Principles and Processes), not in 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: The construction of the first recombinant DNA emerged from the possibility of linking a gene encoding antibiotic resistance with a native plasmid (autonomously replicating circular extra-chromosomal DNA) of Salmonella typhimurium.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: The first restriction endonuclease, Hind II, was able to cut DNA by recognising a specific sequence. How many base pairs long is the recognition sequence of Hind II?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that Hind II recognises a specific sequence of six base pairs. Option A (four base pairs) is incorrect; four-base cutters exist in biology but Hind II is not one of them. Option B (eight base pairs) is a longer recognition sequence seen in rare-cutters, not Hind II. Option D (three base pairs) is too short and not supported by the text. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 11 - Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, not 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: It was found that Hind II always cut DNA molecules at a particular point by recognising a specific sequence of six base pairs. This specific base sequence is known as the recognition sequence for Hind II.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which bacterium is responsible for the large holes found in Swiss cheese?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that Propionibacterium shermanii (also written as sharmanii in some editions) produces large amounts of CO2, which creates the large holes in Swiss cheese. Option A (Lactobacillus acidophilus) is a lactic acid bacterium associated with curd formation, not Swiss cheese. Option C (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is baker's yeast used in bread making. Option D (Acetobacter aceti) is associated with vinegar production, not cheese. Note: This topic is covered in the Microbes in Human Welfare chapter, not Human Health and Disease.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: The large holes in 'Swiss cheese' are due to production of a large amount of CO2 by a bacterium named Propionibacterium sharmanii.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When lactic acid bacteria (LAB) grow in milk and convert it to curd, which vitamin increases in nutritional quality?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology Class 12 (Chapter 10 - Microbes in Human Welfare) clearly states that Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) grow in milk and convert it to curd, which also improves its nutritional quality by increasing Vitamin B12. Option A (Vitamin C) is an antioxidant vitamin not produced by LAB fermentation. Option B (Vitamin D) is a fat-soluble vitamin related to calcium metabolism, not produced by LAB. Option D (Vitamin A) is a fat-soluble vitamin unrelated to LAB fermentation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: converting milk to curd, which also improves its nutritional quality by increasing vitamin B12.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which yeast is used to ferment dough for making bread?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly names Saccharomyces cerevisiae as baker's yeast used to ferment bread dough. Option A (Aspergillus niger) is a fungus used in citric acid production, not bread making. Option B (Rhizopus stolonifer) is a common bread mould that spoils bread rather than being used to make it. Option D (Penicillium notatum) is the fungus from which penicillin antibiotic is derived, not used in bread fermentation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: the dough, which is used for making bread, is fermented using baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which yeast is used for fermenting malted cereals and fruit juices to produce ethanol for alcoholic beverages?
questions.explanation: Saccharomyces cerevisiae, also called brewer's yeast, is directly stated in NCERT as the yeast used to ferment malted cereals and fruit juices to produce ethanol. Option A (Monascus pureus) is a yeast-like fungus that produces statins (lovastatin), not ethanol for beverages. Option C (Trichoderma polysporum) is a fungus that produces cyclosporin A, an immunosuppressant. Option D (Aspergillus niger) is a fungus that produces citric acid, not ethanol. Note: This topic is covered in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 10 (Microbes in Human Welfare), not Human Health and Disease.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: the same yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae used for bread-making and commonly called brewer's yeast, is used for fermenting malted cereals and fruit juices, to produce ethanol.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cyclosporin A, which is used as an immunosuppressive agent in organ-transplant patients, is produced by which microorganism?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that cyclosporin A is produced by the fungus Trichoderma polysporum and is used as an immunosuppressive agent in organ-transplant patients. Option A (Streptoccus) is a bacterium that produces streptokinase, a clot buster, not cyclosporin A. Option B (Monascus pureus) is a yeast that produces statins, which lower blood cholesterol. Option D (Clostridium butylicum) is a bacterium that produces butyric acid.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: cyclosporin A, that is used as an immunosuppressive agent in organ-transplant patients, is produced by the fungus Trichoderma polysporum.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What shape are the guard cells of stomata in grasses?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in grasses, guard cells are dumb-bell shaped. Option A (bean-shaped) is wrong because bean-shaped guard cells are found in dicots and other plants, not grasses. Option C (oval-shaped) and Option D (cylindrical) are not mentioned in NCERT for guard cells and are therefore incorrect.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In grasses, the guard cells are dumb-bell shaped.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In dicotyledonous stems, what is the vascular bundle called because it can form secondary xylem and phloem?
questions.explanation: In dicot stems, cambium is present between xylem and phloem, giving the bundle the ability to produce secondary tissues — these are called open vascular bundles. Option A (closed) is wrong because closed bundles lack cambium and are found in monocots. Option B (radial) describes the arrangement in roots, not the open/closed classification. Option D (conjoint) refers to xylem and phloem lying along the same radius, which is a different classification.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: Such vascular bundles because of the presence of cambium possess the ability to form secondary xylem and phloem tissues, and hence are called open vascular bundles.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells in the epidermal tissue system help absorb water and minerals from the soil?
questions.explanation: Root hairs are unicellular extensions of epidermal cells that absorb water and minerals from the soil, as stated in NCERT. Option A (trichomes) is wrong because trichomes are found on the shoot system and help prevent water loss, not absorption. Option B (guard cells) regulate stomatal opening and closing, not water absorption from soil. Option C (subsidiary cells) are specialised epidermal cells near guard cells and have no role in water absorption.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The root hairs are unicellular elongations of the epidermal cells and help absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a dicotyledonous stem, the endodermis is also called the starch sheath because its cells are rich in starch grains. What type of cells form the hypodermis in a dicotyledonous stem?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that the hypodermis of a dicot stem is made of collenchymatous cells, which provide mechanical strength. Option B (sclerenchymatous cells) is wrong because sclerenchymatous hypodermis is the feature of a monocot stem, not a dicot stem. Option C (parenchymatous cells) is wrong because parenchyma forms the cortical layers below the hypodermis, not the hypodermis itself. Option D (chlorenchymatous cells) is wrong because these cells are not mentioned in the context of the dicot stem hypodermis anywhere in the text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The outer hypodermis, consists of a few layers of collenchymatous cells just below the epidermis, which provide mechanical strength to the young stem.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a monocotyledonous stem, vascular bundles are scattered throughout the ground tissue. What type of bundle sheath surrounds each vascular bundle in a monocot stem?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that each vascular bundle in a monocot stem is surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath. Option A (parenchymatous) is wrong because parenchyma forms the ground tissue around the bundles, not the sheath itself. Option B (collenchymatous) is wrong because collenchyma forms the hypodermis of dicot stems, not the bundle sheath of monocot stems. Option D (endodermal) is wrong because the endodermis is a layer found in roots, not a bundle sheath in monocot stems.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Anatomy of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The monocot stem has a sclerenchymatous hypodermis, a large number of scattered vascular bundles, each surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath, and a large, conspicuous parenchymatous ground tissue.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where did James Watson receive his Ph.D. degree in 1950?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, Watson received his Ph.D. in 1950 from Indiana University, Bloomington. Option A is wrong because University College, London is where Francis Crick studied physics, not Watson. Option C is wrong because Massachusetts General Hospital is not the correct association in this context. Option D is wrong because Chicago is Watson's birthplace, not where he earned his Ph.D.
questions.ncert_line: This became possible when he received a Fellowship for graduate study in Zoology at Indiana University, Bloomington, where he received his Ph.D. degree in 1950.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What was the subject of Francis Crick's Ph.D. thesis completed in 1954?

options.A: Effect of hard X-rays on bacteriophage multiplication
options.B: Double-helical structure of DNA and replication

questions.explanation: Francis Crick completed his Ph.D. in 1954 on a thesis entitled 'X-ray Diffraction: Polypeptides and Proteins'. This is stated in NCERT Biology. However, the chapter context is incorrect — this fact appears in the chapter on 'Molecular Basis of Inheritance', not 'Reproductive Health'. Option A is incorrect as it relates to Watson's earlier Ph.D. work. Option B is incorrect because the double helix model was published in 1953 as a research paper, not as Crick's thesis. Option D is a generic phrase not associated with Crick's thesis title.
questions.ncert_line: He completed Ph.D. in 1954 on a thesis entitled 'X-ray Diffraction: Polypeptides and Proteins'.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In genetics, what term describes the degree by which progeny differ from their parents?
questions.explanation: NCERT defines Variation as the degree by which progeny differ from their parents. Option A is wrong because heredity refers to the transmission of characters from parents to offspring, not the degree of difference. Option B is wrong because inheritance is the process by which characters are passed on from parent to progeny, which is the basis of heredity. Option C is wrong because mutation refers to changes in genetic material and is a cause of variation, not the definition of variation itself.
questions.ncert_line: Variation is the degree by which progeny differ from their parents.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mendel conducted hybridisation experiments on garden peas for how many years, and what did he propose from these experiments?

options.A: Seven years; laws of inheritance in living organisms
options.B: Five years; laws of natural selection in living organisms
options.C: Ten years; laws of segregation only
options.D: Seven years; laws of mutation in living organisms

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states Mendel worked for seven years (1856–1863) and proposed the laws of inheritance — making Option A correct. Option B is wrong because the duration is five years and 'natural selection' is Darwin's concept, not Mendel's. Option C is wrong because the duration is ten years and Mendel proposed multiple laws, not segregation alone. Option D is wrong because Mendel proposed laws of inheritance, not mutation.
questions.ncert_line: Gregor Mendel, conducted hybridisation experiments on garden peas for seven years (1856-1863) and proposed the laws of inheritance in living organisms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When Mendel self-pollinated the tall F1 plants, what did he observe in the F2 generation regarding plant height?

options.A: All F2 plants were tall with no dwarf plants appearing
options.B: All F2 plants were dwarf, showing complete trait reversal
options.C: 3/4th of F2 plants were tall and 1/4th were dwarf, with no in-between height
options.D: Half of the F2 plants were tall and half showed an intermediate height

questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that 3/4th of F2 plants were tall and 1/4th were dwarf, with no blending — making Option C correct. Option A is wrong because dwarf plants did reappear in F2. Option B is wrong because all F2 plants were not dwarf; most were tall. Option D is wrong because NCERT clearly states there was no intermediate or in-between height — there was no blending of traits. Note: This topic belongs to the chapter on Principles of Inheritance and Variation, not Reproductive Health.
questions.ncert_line: The proportion of plants that were dwarf were 1/4th of the F2 plants while 3/4th of the F2 plants were tall. The tall and dwarf traits were identical to their parental type and did not show any blending, that is all the offspring were either tall or dwarf, none were of in-between height.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the capacity of a cell or explant to generate a whole plant called?
questions.explanation: Totipotency is the specific term used in NCERT for the ability of any cell or explant to generate a whole plant. Option B (somatic hybridisation) refers to the fusion of protoplasts from two different plants — a completely different process. Option C (micro-propagation) refers to the method of producing thousands of plants through tissue culture, not the underlying capacity. Option D (regeneration) is a general biology term but is not the specific NCERT term used here. Note: This concept belongs to Chapter 9 (Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production) or the Biotechnology chapters in NCERT Biology Class 12, not 'Microbes in Human Welfare.' The NCERT line cited is accurate in content: the capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: This capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Plants produced through micro-propagation that are genetically identical to the original plant are called:
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that plants produced by micro-propagation that are genetically identical to the original plant are called somaclones. Option B (somatic hybrids) refers to plants produced by fusion of protoplasts from two different varieties — they are not identical to one parent. Option C (transgenic plants) refers to plants whose genes have been altered by manipulation, which is a different concept. Option D (protoplasts) refers to isolated naked plant cells with digested cell walls, not the whole plants produced by tissue culture. Note: This topic is covered in the chapter 'Biotechnology and its Applications' (or 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production'), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: Each of these plants will be genetically identical to the original plant from which they were grown, i.e., they are somaclones.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When protoplasts from two different plant varieties are fused to produce a new hybrid plant, the process is called:
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the fusion of protoplasts from two different plant varieties to produce hybrid plants is called somatic hybridisation. Option B (micro-propagation) is the method of producing thousands of genetically identical plants through tissue culture — unrelated to fusion. Option C (genetic modification) refers to altering genes of an organism by manipulation, which is a broader and different concept. Option D (totipotency) is the capacity of a cell to generate a whole plant, not a fusion process. Note: The correct answer (A) is indeed correct, but the NCERT line cited belongs to the chapter 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production' (Chapter 9 in Class 12 Biology), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare' (Chapter 10). The subject and chapter attribution is incorrect.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: Isolated protoplasts from two different varieties of plants – each having a desirable character – can be fused to get hybrid protoplasts, which can be further grown to form a new plant. These hybrids are called somatic hybrids while the process is called somatic hybridisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Bt toxin protein exists as an inactive protoxin inside the bacterium. What converts it into an active toxin inside an insect's body?

options.A: The acidic pH of the insect's stomach
options.B: The alkaline pH of the insect's gut which solubilises the crystals
options.C: Digestive enzymes secreted by the insect's midgut
options.D: High temperature inside the insect's body

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 12 - Biotechnology and its Applications), the Bt toxin exists as inactive protoxin crystals in the bacterium. Once an insect ingests these crystals, the alkaline pH of the insect's gut solubilises the crystals and converts the inactive protoxin into its active toxic form. The active toxin then binds to the epithelial cells of the midgut, creating pores that cause cell swelling and lysis, ultimately killing the insect. Option A is wrong because it is the alkaline pH (not acidic) that activates the toxin. Option C is wrong because NCERT specifically attributes activation to alkaline pH. Option D is wrong because temperature is never mentioned as a factor in toxin activation. Note: The chapter is Biotechnology and its Applications, not Microbes in Human Welfare.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: once an insect ingest the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the gut which solubilise the crystals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A nematode called Meloidegyne incognita infects tobacco plant roots. Which molecular mechanism was used to protect tobacco plants from this nematode?

options.A: Introducing Bt toxin genes into the tobacco plant
options.B: Spraying chemical pesticides on the root zone
options.C: RNA interference (RNAi) that silences nematode-specific mRNA
options.D: Overexpression of sense RNA alone to block nematode growth

questions.explanation: RNAi (RNA interference) was used to protect tobacco plants from the nematode Meloidogyne incognita. As described in NCERT Biology Class 12 (Chapter 12: Biotechnology and its Applications), nematode-specific genes were introduced into the host plant using Agrobacterium tumefaciens, producing both sense and anti-sense RNA. These complementary RNAs formed dsRNA, which triggered RNAi and silenced the nematode-specific mRNA, preventing the nematode from surving in the host plant. Option A is wrong because Bt toxin targets insects (lepidopterans, coleopterans, dipterans), not nematodes. Option B is wrong because this is a molecular/biotechnological approach, not a chemical pesticide strategy. Option D is wrong because sense RNA alone cannot trigger RNAi — both sense and anti-sense RNA are required to form dsRNA. Note: The chapter reference should be 'Biotechnology and its Applications' (Chapter 12), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare' (Chapter 10).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: A novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infestation which was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi)... This method involves silencing of a specific mRNA due to a complementary dsRNA molecule that binds to and prevents translation of the mRNA.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India's family planning programmes were first initiated in which year?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that family planning programmes in India were initiated in 1951. Option B (1961) is wrong — that is a decade later. Option C (1947) is wrong — that is India's independence year, not the year these programmes started. Option D (1971) is also incorrect as it is 20 years after the actual year mentioned in the text.
questions.ncert_line: These programmes called 'family planning' were initiated in 1951 and were periodically assed over the past decades.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Amniocentesis is a procedure in which amniotic fluid of the developing foetus is analysed. Which of the following genetic disorders can be detected using this procedure?

options.A: Down syndrome, haemophilia and sickle-cell anaemia
options.B: Diabetes mellitus, colour blindness and albinism

questions.explanation: Amniocentesis is a prenatal diagnostic procedure where foetal cells obtained from amniotic fluid are analysed for chromosomal and genetic abnormalities. NCERT specifically mentions Down syndrome (chromosomal disorder), haemophilia (X-linked genetic disorder), and sickle-cell anaemia (autosomal recessive genetic disorder) as detectable conditions. Option B is incorrect because diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder not typically detected by amniocentesis; colour blindness can be detected but is grouped with non-detectable conditions here. Option C is incorrect because tuberculosis and malaria are infectious diseases caused by pathogens, not genetic disorders. Option D is incorrect because kwashiorkor, rickets, and scurvy are nutritional deficiency diseases, not genetic disorders detectable by amniocentesis.
questions.ncert_line: This procedure is used to test for the presence of certain genetic disorders such as, down syndrome, haemoplia, sickle-cell anemia, etc., determine the survivability of the foetus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The lactational amenorrhea method is considered effective only up to a maximum period of how long following parturition?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that the lactational amenorrhea method is effective only up to a maximum of six months after parturition. Option A (three months) is too short and not mentioned in the text. Option C (nine months) and Option D (twelve months) are longer periods that exceed what the text specifies, making them incorrect.
questions.ncert_line: this method has been reported to be effective only upto a maximum period of six months following parturition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which days of the menstrual cycle is the fertile period considered to occur, when couples using periodic abstinence should avoid coitus?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Chapter: Reproductive Health), periodic abstinence is a natural method of contraception in which couples avoid coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, as ovulation is most likely to occur during this period. This window represents the fertile period when the chances of fertilisation are highest. Option A (day 1 to 7) covers the menstrual and early follicular phase with low fertility risk. Option B (day 5 to 12) partially overlaps but is not the range specified in NCERT. Option D (day 18 to 28) falls after the fertile window. Hence, day 10 to 17 (Option C) is the correct fertile period as per NCERT.
questions.ncert_line: Periodic abstinence is one such method in which the couples avoid or abstain from coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle when ovulation could be expected.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells in the seminiferous tubules provide nutrition to the male germ cells?
questions.explanation: Sertoli cells line the seminiferous tubules and provide nutrition to the developing germ cells — this is directly stated in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 3 (Human Reproduction). Option A (Leydig cells) is wrong because Leydig cells are found in interstial spaces and synthesise androgens, not provide nutrition. Option C (Spermatogonia) is wrong because spermatogonia are the male germ cells themselves that undergo meiosis to form sperms. Option D (Interstial cells) is another name for Leydig cells, which secrete androgens, not nutrition. Note: The subject should be Biology, Chapter: Human Reproduction (Class 12, Chapter 3), not Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: Sertoli cells provide nutrition to the germ cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the role of the scrotum in the male reproductive system?

options.A: It secretes androgens to support sperm development
options.B: It stores sperms until they are released during ejaculation
options.C: It helps maintain a temperature lower than normal body temperature for spermatogenesis
options.D: It produces seminal plasma rich in fructose and calcium

questions.explanation: The scrotum maintains a temperature 2–2.5°C lower than normal body temperature, which is essential for spermatogenesis — directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because androgen secretion is the function of Leydig cells inside the testis, not the scrotum. Option B is wrong because sperm storage is the function of the epidymis and vas deferens. Option D is wrong because seminal plasma is secreted by accessory glands such as seminal vesicles and prostate, not the scrotum. Note: This question belongs to Chapter 'Human Reproduction' (Chapter 3 in Class 12 Biology), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants' (Chapter 2). The NCERT line cited is accurate in content.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The scrotum helps in maintaing the low temperature of the tes (2–2.5°C lower than the normal internal body temperature) necessary for spermatogenesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the path of sperms from the seminiferous tubules to the urethra?

options.A: Seminiferous tubules → Epidymis → Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Vas deferens → Urethra
options.B: Seminiferous tubules → Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Epidymis → Vas deferens → Urethra
options.C: Seminiferous tubules → Vasa efferentia → Vas deferens → Rete testis → Epidymis → Urethra
options.D: Seminiferous tubules → Rete testis → Epidymis → Vas deferens → Vasa efferentia → Urethra

questions.explanation: The correct sequence is: Seminiferous tubules → Rete testis → Vasa efferentia → Epidymis → Vas deferens → Urethra, as clearly described in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 3 (Human Reproduction). The seminiferous tubules open into the rete testis, which connects to the vasa efferentia. The vasa efferentia lead to the epidymis, which then continues as the vas deferens, ultimately opening into the urethra via the ejaculatory duct. Option A is wrong because it places epidymis before rete testis and vasa efferentia. Option C is wrong because it skips rete testis and misplaces vasa efferentia. Option D is wrong because it places vasa efferentia after vas deferens, contradicting the correct NCERT sequence. Note: The chapter reference should be 'Human Reproduction' (Chapter 3, Class 12), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants' (Chapter 2).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The seminiferous tubules of the testis open into the vasa efferentia through rete testis. The vasa efferentia leave the testis and open into epidymis... The epidymis leads to vas deferens... opens into urethra as the ejaculatory duct.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which layer of the uterine wall undergoes cyclical changes during the menstrual cycle?
questions.explanation: The endometrium is the inner glandular layer ling the uterine cavity, and it undergoes cyclical changes during the menstrual cycle — directly stated in NCERT. Option A (myometrium) is wrong because the myometrium contracts during delivery, not during the menstrual cycle. Option B (perimetrium) is the external thin membranous layer and has no such role mentioned. Option D (cervical epithelium) is not described as undergoing cyclical changes in the text. Note: This topic belongs to NCERT Biology Chapter 'Human Reproduction', not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The endometrium undergoes cyclical changes during menstrual cycle while the myometrium exhibits strong contraction during delivery of the baby.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During spermatogenesis, what is the process by which spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa called?
questions.explanation: Spermiogenesis is specifically the process that transforms spermatids into mature spermatozoa — directly stated in NCERT. Option A (spermiation) is wrong because spermiation is the process by which sperms are released from the seminiferous tubules, not the transformation of spermatids. Option C (gametogenesis) is the broader process of gamete production in general, not specific to this step. Option D (spermatocytogenesis) is not a term used in the NCERT text. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 'Human Reproduction' (Class 12 Biology), not 'Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The spermatids are transformed into spermatozoa (sperms) by the process called spermiogenesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which sugar is present in a DNA nucleotide?
questions.explanation: Deoxyribose is the pentose sugar found in DNA nucleotides. Option A (ribose) is the sugar found in RNA, not DNA. Option C (fructose) and Option D (galactose) are monosaccharides involved in carbohydrate metabolism, not components of nucleotides. Note: The NCERT line cited is accurate, but it appears in Chapter 6 (Molecular Basis of Inheritance), not in Principles of Inheritance and Variation (Chapter 5).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: A nucleotide has three components – a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar (ribose in case of RNA, and deoxyribose for DNA), and a phosphate group.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Through which type of linkage is a nitrogenous base connected to the 1'C of a pentose sugar to form a nucleoside?
questions.explanation: NCERT specifically states that the base is linked to the 1'C of the pentose sugar via an N-glycosidic linkage to form a nucleoside. Option A (phosphodiester linkage) is the bond that joins two nucleotides together in a polynucleotide chain. Option B (peptide linkage) joins amino acids in proteins. Option D (phosphoester linkage) connects the phosphate group to the 5'C of the nucleoside to form a nucleotide.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: A nitrogenous base is linked to the OH of 1' C pentose sugar through a N-glycosidic linkage to form a nucleoside.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which nitrogenous base is found in RNA in place of Thymine?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Uracil replaces Thymine in RNA. RNA contains four nitrogenous bases: Adenine, Guanine, Cytosine, and Uracil — where Uracil takes the place of Thymine found in DNA. Option A (Adenine) is a purine base present in both DNA and RNA but does not replace thymine. Option B (Guanine) is also a purine present in both nucleic acids. Option C (Cytosine) is a pyrimidine common to both DNA and RNA but does not replace thymine. Note: This topic is more precisely covered in the chapter 'Molecular Basis of Inheritance' rather than 'Principles of Inheritance and Variation.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: Uracil is present in RNA at the place of Thymine.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which scientist first identified DNA as an acidic substance present in the nucleus and named it 'Nuclein'?
questions.explanation: Friedrich Meischer discovered DNA in 1869 and called it 'Nuclein' — this is directly stated in NCERT. James Watson is wrong because he proposed the Double Helix model in 1953, much later. Erwin Chargaff is wrong because he studied base ratios in DNA, not its initial discovery. Maurice Wilkins is wrong because he provided X-ray diffraction data used by Watson and Crick. Note: The correct chapter in NCERT Biology is 'Molecular Basis of Inheritance' (Chapter 6 in Class 12), not 'Principles of Inheritance and Variation'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: DNA as an acidic substance present in nucleus was first identified by Friedrich Meischer in 1869. He named it as 'Nuclein'.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the DNA double helix, Adenine forms hydrogen bonds with Thymine. How many hydrogen bonds are formed between Guanine and Cytosine?
questions.explanation: Guanine and Cytosine are held together by three hydrogen bonds, while Adenine and Thymine are held together by two hydrogen bonds. This is correctly stated in NCERT Biology. However, the chapter citation is incorrect — this information is found in Chapter 6: Molecular Basis of Inheritance (Class 12), not in Chapter 5: Principles of Inheritance and Variation. The answer C (Three hydrogen bonds) is correct, and the NCERT line cited is accurate in content.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: Adenine forms two hydrogen bonds with Thymine from opposite strand and vice-versa. Similarly, Guanine is bonded with Cytosine with three H-bonds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: What property of restriction enzymes did Herbert Boyer discover that made joing DNA pieces precise?

options.A: They could replicate DNA strands independently
options.B: They cut DNA strands leaving 'sticky ends' on the strands
options.C: They removed plasmids from bacterial cells
options.D: They inserted plasmids into new host cells

questions.explanation: Boyer discovered that restriction enzymes cut DNA leaving 'sticky ends', which made joing DNA pieces precise. This property allowed precise pasting together of DNA pieces, forming the basis of recombinant DNA technology. Option A is wrong because independent replication refers to plasmids, not restriction enzymes. Option C is wrong because removing plasmids was not Boyer's discovery about restriction enzymes. Option D is also not related to Boyer's enzyme discovery. Note: The NCERT line cited is accurate in content, but this topic belongs to Chapter 11 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes) in Class 12 Biology, not 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Boyer observed that these enzymes have the capability of cutting DNA strands in a particular fashion, which left what has became known as 'sticky ends' on the strands. These clipped ends made pasting together pieces of DNA a precise exercise.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: What was Stanley Cohen's contribution that, when combined with Boyer's DNA splicing, led to the birth of biotechnology?

options.A: Discovering restriction enzymes that produce sticky ends
options.C: Developing a method to remove plasmids from cells and reinsert them into other cells
options.D: Studying the structure of the coding strand of DNA

questions.explanation: Cohen's key contribution was developing a method to remove plasmids from bacterial cells and reinsert them into other cells — this is clearly stated in the NCERT Biology textbook (Class 12, Chapter 11: Biotechnology - Principles and Processes). Option A is wrong because discovering sticky-end-producing restriction enzymes was Boyer's contribution. Option B is wrong because in vitro fertilisation is a separate biotechnology application unrelated to Cohen. Option D is wrong because studying the coding strand is not attributed to Cohen anywhere in NCERT. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 11 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes), not 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production' (Chapter 9).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Cohen had developed a method of removing these plasmids from the cell and then reinserting them in other cells. Combing this process with that of DNA splicing enabled Boyer and Cohen to recombine segments of DNA in desired configurations.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Which two core techniques enabled the birth of modern biotechnology according to the NCERT text?

options.B: In vitro fertilisation and gene synthesis

questions.explanation: The answer A is correct, but the chapter attribution is wrong. The NCERT line about the two core techniques of modern biotechnology — (i) Genetic engineering and (ii) Bioprocess engineering — comes from Chapter 11: Biotechnology: Principles and Processes (Class 12 Biology), not from 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production' (Chapter 9). The explanation about the options is otherwise accurate.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Among many, the two core techniques that enabled birth of modern biotechnology are : (i) Genetic engineering ... (ii) Bioprocess engineering.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: For an alien piece of DNA to replicate and multiply inside a host organism, what must it be linked with?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text explicitly states that an alien DNA must be linked with the origin of replication to replicate and multiply in the host organism. Option A is wrong because restriction endonuclease sites are used for cutting DNA, not for initiating replication. Option C is wrong because an antibiotic resistance gene is a selectable marker, not a replication-initiating sequence. Option D is wrong because DNA ligase joins cut DNA ends; it does not provide a sequence need for replication. Note: This concept belongs to the chapter on Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, not Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.ncert_line: Thus, an alien DNA is linked with the origin of replication, so that, this alien piece of DNA can replicate and multiply itself in the host organism.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the approximate number of species that are known and described on Earth?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that known and described species range between 1.7–1.8 million. Option B (1.2–1.3 million) is too low and not mentioned in the text. Option C (2.5–3.0 million) is an overestimate not supported by the text. Option D (0.5–0.8 million) is far too low and incorrect.
questions.ncert_line: The number of species that are known and described range between 1.7-1.8 million.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The term 'biodiversity' refers to which of the following?

options.A: The number and types of organisms present on Earth
options.B: The classification system used to name organisms
options.C: The study of organisms living in extreme habitats
options.D: The genetic variation within a single species

questions.explanation: According to the NCERT Biology textbook (Class 11, Chapter 1 - The Living World), biodiversity refers to the number and types of organisms present on Earth. Option B describes nomenclature or taxonomy, not biodiversity. Option C describes extremophile ecology, which is not the definition given. Option D refers to genetic diversity within a species, which is a narrower concept. The NCERT line cited is accurate and directly from the text.
questions.ncert_line: This refers to biodiversity or the number and types of organisms present on earth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ernst Mayr pioneered which of the following in the field of biology?

options.A: The currently accepted definition of a biological species
options.B: The first classification of plants into kingdoms
options.C: The discovery of DNA as the genetic material
options.D: The binomial nomenclature system for naming organisms

questions.explanation: The NCERT text explicitly states that Ernst Mayr pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species. Option B is incorrect because the classification of plants into kingdoms is associated with other scientists, not Mayr. Option C is incorrect because DNA as genetic material was established by Avery, MacLeod and McCarty, not Mayr. Option D is incorrect because binomial nomenclature was introduced by Carolus Linnaeus, not Mayr.
questions.ncert_line: He also pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the rule for writing a biological name when it is handwritten?

options.A: The entire name is written in capital letters
options.B: Only the genus name is underlined
options.C: Both words are separately underlined to indicate their Latin origin
options.D: The specific epithet is written in capital letters and underlined

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that when a biological name is handwritten, both words — the genus name and the specific epithet — are separately underlined to indicate their Latin origin. Option A is wrong because writing in all capitals is not the rule; capitalisation applies only to the first letter of the genus. Option B is wrong because only underling the genus name is incomplete — both words must be underlined. Option D is wrong because the specific epithet starts with a small letter, not a capital letter, as per nomenclature rules.
questions.ncert_line: Both the words in a biological name, when handwritten, are separately underlined, or printed in italics to indicate their Latin origin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who proposed the Five Kingdom Classification in 1969?
questions.explanation: R.H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification in 1969 — this is directly stated in NCERT. Aristotle is wrong because he only classified plants into trees, shrubs and herbs and animals into two groups; he did not propose a five kingdom system. Linnaeus is wrong because he developed the Two Kingdom system with only Plantae and Animalia. Charles Darwin is wrong because he is known for the theory of evolution, not for this classification system.
questions.ncert_line: R.H. Whittaker (1969) proposed a Five Kingdom Classification. The kingdoms defined by him were named Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the cell wall composition of fungi according to the Five Kingdom Classification?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 11, Chapter 2 - Biological Classification), fungi have cell walls made of chitin, a complex polysaccharide. Cellulose is the cell wall component of plants (Plantae), not fungi. Polysaccharide and amino acids describe the cell wall composition of Monera (prokaryotes like bacteria). Peptidoglycan is another bacterial cell wall component but is not the component of fungal cell walls. Hence, chitin is the correct answer for fungi.
questions.ncert_line: the fungi had chitin in their walls

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which feature was used to unify all organisms placed under 'Plants' in earlier classification systems?

options.B: Presence of a cell wall in their cells

questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that the presence of a cell wall was the unifying character for all organisms placed under 'Plants' in earlier systems. Photosynthesis is wrong because fungi, which were also placed under plants, are non-photosynthetic heterotrophs. Being multicellular is wrong because unicellular organisms like Chlamydomonas were also placed under this kingdom. Having a nuclear membrane is wrong because prokaryotic bacteria and blue-green algae, which lack a nuclear membrane, were also included in this group.
questions.ncert_line: The character that unified this whole kingdom was that all the organisms included had a cell wall in their cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Methanogens present in the gut of ruminant animals like cows and buffaloes are responsible for producing which substance from dung?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that methanogens produce methane (biogas) from the dung of ruminant animals like cows and buffaloes. Option B (ammonia) is oxidised by chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria for energy, not produced by methanogens. Option C (nitrogen gas) is fixed by cyanobacteria like Nostoc and Anabaena, not produced by methanogens. Option D (carbon dioxide) is a common product of respiration but is not what methanogens specifically produce from dung according to the NCERT text.
questions.ncert_line: Methanogens are present in the gut of several ruminant animals such as cows and buffaloes and they are responsible for the production of methane (biogas) from the dung of these animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Growth is defined as an irreversible permanent increase in size of an organ or its parts. Which of the following processes always accompanies growth?

options.A: Metabolic processes that occur at the expense of energy
options.B: Reduction in cell volume to conserve resources
options.C: Temporary suspension of all cellular activities
options.D: Decrease in dry weight of the organism

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that growth is accompanied by metabolic processes — both anabolic and catabolic — that use energy. Option B is wrong because growth involves increase in size, not reduction in cell volume. Option C is wrong because metabolic activities are active during growth, not suspended. Option D is wrong because growth generally leads to an increase, not a decrease, in dry weight.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: Generally, growth is accompanied by metabolic processes (both anabolic and catabolic), that occur at the expense of energy.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Plants retain the capacity for unlimited growth throughout their life. Which feature of plants is responsible for this ability?

options.A: Presence of meristems at certain locations in the plant body
options.B: Presence of stomata on the surface of leaves
options.C: Ability of xylem tissue to transport water continuously
options.D: Formation of flowers and fruits at regular intervals

questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the presence of meristems, whose cells can divide and self-perpetuate, gives plants the ability for unlimited growth. Option B is wrong because stomata regulate gas exchange, not growth capacity. Option C is wrong because xylem transport is unrelated to the plant's capacity for unlimited growth. Option D is wrong because flowering is a periodic reproductive event and does not explain unlimited vegetative growth.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: This ability of the plants is due to the presence of meristems at certain locations in their body. The cells of such meristems have the capacity to divide and self-perpetuate.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In dicotyledonous plants and gymnosperms, lateral meristems such as vascular cambium and cork-cambium become active later in the plant's life. What type of growth do these lateral meristems cause?

options.A: Secondary growth, causing an increase in the girth of organs
options.B: Primary growth, causing elongation of the plant along its axis
options.C: Apical growth, causing formation of new shoot tips
options.D: Lateral root growth, causing branching of root systems

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that vascular cambium and cork-cambium cause secondary growth, which increases the girth of plant organs. Option B is wrong because primary growth and elongation along the axis are the functions of root apical meristem and shoot apical meristem, not lateral meristems. Option C is wrong because apical growth is associated with apical meristems, not lateral meristems. Option D is wrong because lateral root branching is not the function of vascular cambium or cork-cambium.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: These are the meristems that cause the increase in the girth of the organs in which they are active. This is known as secondary growth of the plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which characteristics are seen in cells belonging to the elongation phase of growth?

options.A: Increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition
options.B: Rich protoplasm, large nuclei and thin cellulosic walls
options.C: Maximum wall thickening and protoplasmic modifications
options.D: Abundant plasmodesmatal connections and active cell division

questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition characterise the elongation phase. Option B describes cells of the meristematic phase, which are rich in protoplasm and have large nuclei. Option C describes cells of the maturation phase, which attain maximal size with wall thickening and protoplasmic modifications. Option D describes features of meristematic cells, which have abundant plasmodesmatal connections and divide actively.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: Increased vacuolation, cell enlargement and new cell wall deposition are the characteristics of the cells in this phase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In arithmetic growth, what happens to the two daughter cells after mitotic cell division?

options.A: One daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates and matures
options.B: Both daughter cells retain the ability to divide and continue to do so
options.C: Both daughter cells immediately differentiate into mature cells
options.D: One daughter cell enters the lag phase while the other enters the exponential phase

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in arithmetic growth only one daughter cell keeps dividing while the other differentiates and matures. Option B describes geometric growth, where both progeny cells retain the ability to divide. Option C is incorrect because in arithmetic growth one cell still continues dividing, not both differentiate immediately. Option D incorrectly uses the terms lag phase and exponential phase, which describe stages of geometric/sigmoid growth, not arithmetic growth.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Plant Growth and Development
questions.ncert_line: In arithmetic growth, following mitotic cell division, only one daughter cell continues to divide while the other differentiates and matures.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and form which type of root system?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that in monocots, roots arising from the base of the stem form the fibrous root system, as seen in wheat. Option B (tap root system) is wrong because tap root system is formed by the primary root and its branches, as seen in dicots like mustard. Option C (adventious root system) requires clarification — NCERT does mention that roots arising from parts other than the radicle are called adventious roots (e.g., grass, Monstera, banyan). The fibrous root system in monocots is actually composed of adventious roots, but NCERT specifically uses the term 'fibrous root system' for this arrangement, making A the best answer in this context. Option D (tertiary root system) is wrong because tertiary roots are just higher-order branches of the primary root, not a distinct type of root system. The NCERT line cited is accurate: 'In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the fibrous root system, as seen in the wheat plant.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the fibrous root system, as seen in the wheat plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In dicotyledonous plants, the primary root is formed by the direct elongation of which part of the seed?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the radicle elongates to form the primary root in dicots. Option B (plumule) is wrong because the plumule gives rise to the shoot, not the root. Option C (cotyledon) is wrong because cotyledons are seed leaves that store or absorb food, they do not form the primary root. Option D (epicotyl) is wrong because the epicotyl is the portion of the embryo axis above the cotyledons and it contributes to shoot development, not root formation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In majority of the dicotyledonous plants, the direct elongation of the radicle leads to the formation of primary root which grows inside the soil.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Roots that arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle are called adventious roots. In which of the following plants are adventious roots found?
questions.explanation: NCERT specifically names the banyan tree as an example of a plant with adventious roots. Option B (mustard plant) is wrong because mustard is a dicot that has a tap root system formed by the elongation of the radicle, not adventious roots. Option C (wheat plant) is wrong because wheat is a monocot with a fibrous root system, not adventious roots. Option D (pea plant) is wrong because pea is a dicot with a tap root system and is not mentioned in the context of adventious roots in this text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In some plants, like grass, Monstera and the banyan tree, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle and are called adventious roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The region of the root from which root hairs arise absorbs water and minerals from the soil. Which region of the root do these root hairs originate from?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that root hairs arise from epidermal cells of the region of maturation. The region of elongation is wrong because its cells are responsible for increasing root length, not forming root hairs. The region of meristematic activity is wrong because those cells are small, thin-walled and actively dividing, not yet differentiated into root hairs. The root cap is wrong because it is a thimble-like protective structure at the apex of the root, not a site of root hair formation.
