questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: From this region some of the epidermal cells form very fine and delicate, thread-like structures called root hairs. These root hairs absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the human neural system includes the brain and the spinal cord?
questions.explanation: The Central Neural System (CNS) includes the brain and spinal cord — directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because the peripheral neural system comprises all nerves of the body associated with the CNS, not the brain or spinal cord. Option C is wrong because the autonomic neural system is a division of the PNS that transmits impulses to involuntary organs. Option D is wrong because the somatic neural system relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles only.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Neural Control and Coordination
questions.ncert_line: The CNS includes the brain and the spinal cord and is the site of information processing and control.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of nerve fibres transmit impulses from tissues and organs to the CNS?
questions.explanation: Afferent fibres carry impulses from tissues/organs to the CNS — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because efferent fibres carry impulses in the opposite direction, from CNS to peripheral tissues. Option B is wrong because motor fibres is another name for efferent fibres that carry signals away from CNS. Option D is wrong because autonomic fibres refer to a division of PNS dealing with involuntary organs, not the direction of impulse transmission.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Neural Control and Coordination
questions.ncert_line: The afferent nerve fibres transmit impulses from tissues/organs to the CNS and the efferent fibres transmit regulatory impulses from the CNS to the concerned peripheral tissues/organs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the role of the autonomic neural system?

options.A: It relays impulses from the CNS to skeletal muscles
options.B: It transmits impulses from sensory organs to the CNS
options.C: It transmits impulses from the CNS to involuntary organs and smooth muscles
options.D: It connects the brain directly to the somatic nervous system

questions.explanation: The autonomic neural system specifically transmits impulses from the CNS to involuntary organs and smooth muscles — directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because it describes the somatic neural system, which relays impulses to skeletal muscles. Option B is wrong because transmitting impulses from sensory organs to CNS is the function of afferent fibres. Option D is wrong because the text does not describe any such direct connection between the brain and somatic nervous system through the autonomic system.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Neural Control and Coordination
questions.ncert_line: The autonomic neural system transmits impulses from the CNS to the involuntary organs and smooth muscles of the body.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where are bipolar neurons found in the human body?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that bipolar neurons, which have one axon and one dendrite, are found in the retina of the eye. Option A is wrong because the cerebral cortex contains multipolar neurons. Option C is wrong because unipolar neurons (not bipolar) are found in the embryonic stage. Option D is wrong because spinal nerves contain myelinated nerve fibres, not a specific neuron type described here.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Neural Control and Coordination
questions.ncert_line: bipolar (with one axon and one dendrite, found in the retina of eye)

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to the outer surface of the axonal membrane when a stimulus causes rapid influx of sodium ions at a site?

options.A: It remains positively charged as in the resting state
options.B: It becomes negatively charged, reversing the original polarity
options.C: It becomes impermeable to all ions immediately
options.D: It releases potassium ions to the outside rapidly

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that when Na+ rapidly flows into the axon at the stimulated site, the outer surface becomes negatively charged and the inner surface becomes positively charged — this is called depolarisation. Option A is wrong because the outer surface was positively charged only during the resting state, not after stimulation. Option C is wrong because the membrane actually becomes freely permeable to Na+ upon stimulation, not impermeable. Option D is wrong because potassium diffuses outward only after the depolarisation phase, to restore the resting potential.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Neural Control and Coordination
questions.ncert_line: the membrane at the site A becomes freely permeable to Na+. This leads to a rapid influx of Na+ followed by the reversal of the polarity at that site, i.e., the outer surface of the membrane becomes negatively charged and the inner side becomes positively charged.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who first saw and described a live cell?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 11, Chapter 8 - Cell: The Unit of Life), Antonie Von Leeuwenhoek first saw and described a live cell. Robert Brown discovered the nucleus in 1831. Matthias Schleiden studied plant tissues and contributed to cell theory, while Theodore Schwann studied animal cells and proposed that the cell is the basic unit of life for animals. The NCERT line cited is accurate and consistent with the textbook content.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: Antonie Von Leeuwenhoek first saw and described a live cell. Robert Brown later discovered the nucleus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which scientist proposed that the bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Theodore Schwann proposed the hypothesis that bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells. Option A is wrong because Schleiden studied plants and observed that all plants are composed of cells, but it was Schwann who proposed this broader hypothesis encompassing both animals and plants. Option C is wrong because Leeuwenhoek first saw and described a live cell. Option D is wrong because Robert Brown discovered the nucleus.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: Schwann proposed the hypothesis that the bodies of animals and plants are composed of cells and products of cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What did Theodore Schwann conclude is a unique character of plant cells?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that Schwann concluded that the presence of cell wall is a unique character of plant cells. Option A is wrong because the plasma membrane was reported by Schwann as a thin outer layer present in animal cells, not as a unique plant character. Option B is wrong because the nucleus was discovered by Robert Brown and is present in both plant and animal cells. Option D is wrong because mitochondria are not mentioned in this context in the given text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: He also concluded, based on his studies on plant tissues, that the presence of cell wall is a unique character of the plant cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which scientist first explained that new cells arise from pre-existing cells?
questions.explanation: Rudolf Virchow in 1855 gave the concept 'Omnis cellula-e cellula', meaning new cells come from pre-existing cells. Schleiden and Schwann together formulated the original cell theory but did NOT explain how new cells are formed. Robert Hooke discovered cells but had no role in this concept.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: Rudolf Virchow (1855) first explained that cells divided and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula-e cellula).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following organelles is found in ALL cells — both prokaryotic and eukaryotic?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are non-membrane bound organelles present in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, making them universal to all cells. Mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi complex are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotic cells. The NCERT text clearly states that prokaryotic cells lack such membrane-bound organelles.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: Ribosomes are non-membrane bound organelles found in all cells – both eukaryotic as well as prokaryotic.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which plasma protein helps in maintaing osmotic balance in the blood?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, albumins help in osmotic balance. Option A is wrong because fibrinogen is need for clotting of blood, not osmotic balance. Option B is wrong because globulins are primarily involved in defense mechanisms of the body. Option D is wrong because haemoglobin is found in RBCs, not in plasma, and it transports respiratory gases.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: the albumins help in osmotic balance.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of white blood cell secretes histamine, serotonin, and heparin and is involved in inflammatory reactions?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, and heparin and are involved in inflammatory reactions. Option A is wrong because neutrophils are phagocytic cells that destroy foreign organisms. Option B is wrong because eosinophils resist infections and are associated with allergic reactions. Option C is wrong because lymphocytes are responsible for immune responses of the body.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, heparin, etc., and are involved in inflammatory reactions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where are red blood cells (RBCs) formed in adults?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that RBCs are formed in the red bone marrow in adults. Option A is wrong because the spleen is described as the graveyard of RBCs, where they are destroyed after their lifespan, not where they are formed. Option B is wrong because the liver is not mentioned in NCERT as the site of RBC formation in adults (though it serves this role in the fetus). Option D is wrong because yellow bone marrow contains mostly fat cells and is not the site of RBC production.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: RBCs are formed in the red bone marrow in the adults.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A pregnant woman is Rh negative and her foetus is Rh positive. During the delivery of her first child, the mother's blood gets exposed to Rh positive blood of the foetus. What will happen in her second Rh positive pregnancy?

options.A: Rh antibodies from the mother will leak into the foetal blood and destroy foetal RBCs
options.B: The placenta will completely block all Rh antigens from reaching the mother
options.C: The mother will produce anti-A and anti-B antibodies to protect the foetus
options.D: The foetus will produce its own Rh antibodies to counter the mother's Rh antigens

questions.explanation: After exposure during first delivery, the Rh-negative mother forms antibodies against Rh antigen. In the second Rh-positive pregnancy, these maternal Rh antibodies leak into the foetal blood and destroy foetal RBCs, causing erythroblastosis foetalis. Option B is wrong because during delivery the placental separation allows some mixing of blood — it does not block everything. Option C is wrong because anti-A and anti-B are ABO system antibodies, not related to Rh incompatibility. Option D is wrong because the destruction is caused by the mother's antibodies acting on the foetus, not the other way around.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: In case of her subsequent pregnancies, the Rh antibodies from the mother (Rh-ve) can leak into the blood of the foetus (Rh+ve) and destroy the foetal RBCs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During blood coagulation, fibrin threads are formed at the site of injury. Which substance is directly converted into fibrin by the enzyme thrombin?
questions.explanation: Thrombin directly converts inactive fibrinogen into fibrin threads that form the clot. Option B is wrong because prothrombin is the inactive substance that is converted INTO thrombin, not into fibrin. Option C is wrong because thrombokinase is an enzyme complex that helps convert prothrombin to thrombin — it does not get converted into fibrin. Option D is wrong because prothrombin activator is essentially another name for thrombokinase, which activates the prothrombin-to-thrombin step, and is not converted into fibrin.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Fibrins are formed by the conversion of inactive fibrinogens in the plasma by the enzyme thrombin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In algae, fusion between one large non-motile female gamete and a smaller motile male gamete is called:
questions.explanation: Oogamy is defined as fusion of a large non-motile female gamete with a smaller motile male gamete — this is directly stated in NCERT with examples Volvox and Fucus. Isogamy is wrong because in isogamy the two gametes are similar in size. Anisogamy is wrong because anisogamy involves two motile gametes dissimilar in size but does not specify one being non-motile and large as in oogamy. Fragmentation is a method of vegetative reproduction, not sexual reproduction.
questions.ncert_line: Fusion between one large, non-motile (static) female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete is termed oogamous, e.g., Volvox, Fucus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following algae shows isogamous sexual reproduction?
questions.explanation: Spirogyra shows isogamy where gametes are non-flagellated (non-motile) but similar in size — NCERT directly states this as an example of isogamous reproduction. Volvox is wrong because Volvox is an example of oogamy (large non-motile female gamete and small motile male gamete). Fucus is also wrong because Fucus is listed as an example of oogamy. Eudorina is wrong because species of Eudorina show anisogamy, which involves gametes dissimilar in size (both motile but different in size).
questions.ncert_line: non-flagellated (non-motile) but similar in size (as in Spirogyra). Such reproduction is called isogamous.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The natural classification system for flowering plants, based on both external and internal features, was given by:

options.B: George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the natural classification for flowering plants, which considered internal features like anatomy, embryology and phytochemistry along with external features, was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker. Linnaeus is wrong because Linnaeus gave an artificial classification system based on androecium structure. Aristotle and Theophrastus were ancient naturalists not mentioned in this context. Darwin and Wallace are associated with the theory of evolution, not plant classification systems.
questions.ncert_line: Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which pigment is responsible for the brown colour seen in members of Phaeophyceae?
questions.explanation: Fucoxanthin is a xanthophyll pigment present in brown algae (Phaeophyceae) and is directly responsible for their characteristic brown colour — this is clearly stated in NCERT. Option A (r-phycoerythrin) is wrong because it is the red pigment found in Rhodophyceae, not brown algae. Option B (Chlorophyll b) is wrong because it is the major accessory pigment of green algae (Chlorophyceae) and gives a green colour. Option D (Carotene) is a carotenoid present in brown algae but it is NOT the pigment responsible for the brown colour — fucoxanthin is.
questions.ncert_line: They vary in colour from olive green to various shades of brown depending upon the amount of the xanthophyll pigment, fucoxanthin present in them.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The commercial product 'agar', used to grow microbes and in preparation of ice-creams and jellies, is obtained from which organisms?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that agar is obtained from Gelidium and Gracilaria, both of which are red algae (Rhodophyceae). Option A is wrong because Laminaria and Sargassum are brown algae that produce algin, not agar. Option C is wrong because Chlorella is a unicellular green alga used as a food supplement, and Porphyra is a marine alga used as food — neither produces agar. Option D is wrong because Ectocarpus and Fucus are brown algae, not the source of agar.
questions.ncert_line: Agar, one of the commercial products obtained from Gelidium and Gracilaria are used to grow microbes and in preparations of ice-creams and jellies.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which organelle is the site of photosynthesis in eukaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, photosynthesis takes place within the chloroplasts in eukaryotes. Option A (mitochondria) is wrong — mitochondria is the site of breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy, not photosynthesis. Option C (cytoplasm) is wrong — cytoplasm is involved in breakdown of food molecules, not photosynthesis. Option D (nucleus) has no role in photosynthesis.
questions.ncert_line: Photosynthesis, of course, takes place within the chloroplasts (in the eukaryotes), whereas the breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy takes place in the cytoplasm and in the mitochondria (also only in eukaryotes).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which form is used by the cell to store and utilise energy released during respiration?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that energy released during respiration is trapped as ATP, which acts as the energy currency of the cell. Option A (glucose) is wrong — glucose is a respiratory substrate, not the energy storage form used directly by cells. Option B (ADP) is wrong — ADP is the product after ATP is broken down to release energy, not the stored form. Option D (NADH) is wrong — NADH is an electron carrier, not the direct energy currency mentioned in this context.
questions.ncert_line: It is released in a series of slow step-wise reactions controlled by enzymes, and it is trapped as chemical energy in the form of ATP. Hence, ATP acts as the energy currency of the cell.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How do plants fulfil their need for gaseous exchange in the absence of specialised respiratory organs?

options.A: Through roots that absorb gases from the soil
options.C: Through chloroplasts present in all cells
options.D: Through transport of gases between plant parts

questions.explanation: NCERT states that plants use stomata and lenticels for gaseous exchange in the absence of specialised respiratory organs. Option A is wrong — roots absorb water and minerals, and the text does not mention roots absorbing gases from soil for respiration. Option C is wrong — chloroplasts are not present in all cells; NCERT mentions only cells in superficial layers contain chloroplasts. Option D is wrong — NCERT specifically states there is very little transport of gases from one plant part to another.
questions.ncert_line: Plants, unlike animals, have no specialised organs for gaseous exchange but they have stomata and lenticels for this purpose.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In glycolysis, at which step is NADH + H⁺ formed from NAD⁺?

options.A: When glucose is converted to glucose-6-phosphate
options.B: When fructose-6-phosphate is converted to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate
options.D: When phosphoenolpyruvate is converted to pyruvic acid

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that NADH + H⁺ is produced during the conversion of PGAL (3-phosphoglyceraldehyde) to BPGA (1,3-bisphoglycerate), when two hydrogen atoms are removed from PGAL and transferred to NAD⁺. This is an oxidation step catalyzed by glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase. Option A is wrong because the conversion of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate is a phosphorylation step that uses ATP, not one that produces NADH. Option B is wrong because the conversion of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate also uses ATP and does not produce NADH. Option D is wrong because the conversion of phosphoenolpyruvate to pyruvic acid is an ATP-synthesing (substrate-level phosphorylation) step, not an NADH-producing step.
questions.ncert_line: There is one step where NADH + H⁺ is formed from NAD⁺; this is when 3-phosphoglyceraldehyde (PGAL) is converted to 1,3-bisphoglycerate (BPGA).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In plants, what is the source of glucose that enters the glycolytic pathway?

options.A: Starch broken down directly by hexokinase
options.B: Sucrose converted into glucose and fructose by the enzyme invertase
options.C: Fructose-6-phosphate produced from the Calvin cycle
options.D: Glucose-6-phosphate absorbed directly from the soil

questions.explanation: According to the NCERT text, sucrose — the end product of photosynthesis — is broken down by the enzyme invertase into glucose and fructose, which then enter glycolysis. Option A is wrong because hexokinase phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate; it does not break down starch. Option C is wrong because fructose-6-phosphate is an intermediate within glycolysis, not the starting source of glucose entering the pathway. Option D is wrong because glucose-6-phosphate is not absorbed from soil; it is produced inside the cell after hexokinase acts on glucose.
questions.ncert_line: In plants, this glucose is derived from sucrose, which is the end product of photosynthesis, or from storage carbohydrates. Sucrose is converted into glucose and fructose by the enzyme, invertase, and these two monosaccharides readily enter the glycolytic pathway.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vertical distribution of different species occupying different levels in an ecosystem is called:
questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that vertical distribution of species at different levels is called stratification. Option B (succession) refers to change in community over time, not vertical distribution. Option C (zonation) refers to horizontal distribution in different geographical areas. Option D (species composition) refers to identification and enumeration of plant and animal species, not their vertical arrangement. Note: This concept is from the Ecosystems chapter (Chapter 14), not Biotechnology and its Applications.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Vertical distribution of different species occupying different levels is called stratification.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Net Primary Productivity (NPP) of an ecosystem is best defined as:

options.A: Gross Primary Productivity minus respiration losses
options.B: Gross Primary Productivity plus respiration losses
options.C: The total organic matter produced during photosynthesis
options.D: The rate of formation of new organic matter by consumers

questions.explanation: The answer A is correct. NPP = GPP - R (respiration losses). However, the chapter cited is wrong. This concept is from NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 14: Ecosystem, not 'Biotechnology and its Applications' (Chapter 12). The NCERT line is accurate: 'Gross primary productivity minus respiration losses (R), is the net primary productivity (NPP). GPP – R = NPP.' Option C describes GPP (total organic matter produced during photosynthesis), and Option D describes Secondary Productivity.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Gross primary productivity minus respiration losses (R), is the net primary productivity (NPP). GPP – R = NPP

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a pond ecosystem, which organisms are especially abundant at the bottom and act as decomposers?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology explicitly states that fungi, bacteria and flagellates are the decomposers that are especially abundant at the bottom of the pond ecosystem. Option B (phytoplankton and algae) are autotrophic producers, not decomposers. Option C (zooplankton and free-swimming forms) are consumers in the pond ecosystem. Option D (submerged and marginal plants) are also part of the autotrophic component, not decomposers. Note: This topic is covered in the Ecosystem chapter (Chapter 14 in Class 12 Biology), not in Biotechnology and its Applications.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: The decomposers are the fungi, bacteria and flagellates especially abundant in the bottom of the pond.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Humus is described as colloidal in nature. What role does this property help it serve in the soil?

options.A: It acts as a reservoir of nutrients

questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that because humus is colloidal in nature, it serves as a reservoir of nutrients. Option B is wrong because humus is described as highly resistant to microbial action, not something that speeds it up. Option C is wrong because humus has no stated role in increasing soil temperature. Option D is wrong because leaching is a separate process where water-soluble nutrients move down the soil horizon — humus actually retains nutrients rather than promoting their loss.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Being colloidal in nature it serves as a reservoir of nutrients.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which climatic conditions favour a slower rate of decomposition according to the NCERT text?
questions.explanation: The NCERT Biology textbook (Class 12, Chapter 14 - Ecosystem) clearly states that low temperature and anaerobiosis (absence of oxygen) inhibit decomposition, resulting in the build-up of organic materials. This means decomposition is slower under these conditions. Option B is incorrect because warm temperature and high moisture actually favour (speed up) decomposition. Option C is incorrect because high temperature generally promotes microbial activity. Option D is incorrect because high oxygen availability supports aerobic decomposition, speeding it up. Note: This topic belongs to the Ecosystem chapter, not Biotechnology and its Applications.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: low temperature and anaerobiosis inhibit decomposition resulting in build up of organic materials.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates. What is this level of organisation called?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that sponges exhibit cellular level of organisation because their cells are arranged as loose aggregates. Option A (tissue level) belongs to coelenterates, where cells performing the same function are grouped into tissues. Option B (organ level) is seen in Platyhelminthes and higher phyla. Option D (organ system level) is seen in Annelids, Arthropods, Molluscs, Echinoderms and Chordates.
questions.ncert_line: in sponges, the cells are arranged as loose cell aggregates, i.e., they exhibit cellular level of organisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a closed type of circulatory system, how does blood move through the body?

options.A: Blood is pumped out of the heart and directly bathes the cells and tissues
options.B: Blood is circulated through a series of vessels of varying diameters
options.C: Blood flows freely in the body cavity without any vessels
options.D: Blood moves only through the heart and does not reach the tissues

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that in the closed type of circulatory system, blood is circulated through vessels of varying diameters — arteries, veins and capillaries. Option A describes the open type of circulatory system, where blood is pumped out of the heart and directly bathes cells and tissues. Option C is a wrong description not supported by NCERT. Option D is completely incorrect and has no basis in the text.
questions.ncert_line: closed type in which the blood is circulated through a series of vessels of varying diameters (arteries, veins and capillaries).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Phylum Porifera, water enters the body through minute pores called ostia and exits through which opening?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, water enters through minute pores (ostia) in the body wall into a central cavity, spongocoel, from where it goes out through the osculum. Option B (hypostome) is wrong — hypostome is found in Cnidarians (it is the elevated area bearing the mouth in Hydra), not sponges. Option C (spongocoel) is wrong — spongocoel is the central cavity through which water passes, not the exit opening. Option D (nematocyst) is wrong — nematocysts are sting capsules found in Cnidarians, unrelated to water flow in sponges.
questions.ncert_line: Water enters through minute pores (ostia) in the body wall into a central cavity, spongocoel, from where it goes out through the osculum.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which phenomenon is seen in Cnidarians like Obelia, where polyps produce medusae asexually and medusae form polyps sexually?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that alternation of generations in cnidarians is called Metagenesis, seen in Obelia. Option B (bioluminescence) is wrong — it is the property of emitting light, which is well-marked in Ctenophores, not related to this alternation. Option C (fragmentation) is wrong — fragmentation is asexual reproduction seen in sponges. Option D (regeneration) is wrong — regeneration is the ability to regrow lost parts, as seen in Planaria of Platyhelminthes.
questions.ncert_line: Those cnidarians which exist in both forms exhibit alternation of generations (Metagenesis), i.e., polyps produce medusae asexually and medusae form the polyps sexually (e.g., Obelia).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following organisms uses its moist cuticle for exchange of gases?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that earthworms use their moist cuticle for gas exchange. Insects are wrong because they use tracheal tubes, not cuticle. Sponges are wrong because they exchange gases by simple diffusion over their entire body surface. Fish are wrong because they use gills for respiration.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Earthworms use their moist cuticle and insects have a network of tubes (tracheal tubes) to transport atmospheric air within the body.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process of exchange of oxygen from the atmosphere with carbon dioxide produced by the cells is called:
questions.explanation: NCERT directly defines breathing as the exchange of O2 from atmosphere with CO2 produced by cells. Digestion is wrong because it refers to breakdown of food. Circulation is wrong because it refers to transport of blood through the body. Excretion is wrong because it refers to removal of metabolic waste products.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: This process of exchange of O2 from the atmosphere with CO2 produced by the cells is called breathing, commonly known as respiration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of respiration is used by amphibians like frogs in addition to lungs?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that amphibians like frogs can perform cutaneous respiration through their moist skin. Branchial respiration through gills is wrong because that is used by aquatic arthropods and molluscs, as well as fishes. Tracheal respiration is wrong because tracheal tubes are used by insects. Pulmonary respiration through air sacs is wrong because lungs, not air sacs, are the breathing organs for terrestrial vertebrates.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Amphibians like frogs can respire through their moist skin (cutaneous respiration) also.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where does actual diffusion of oxygen and carbon dioxide between blood and atmospheric air take place?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 11, Chapter 17 - Breathing and Exchange of Gases), the respiratory system is divided into two parts: the conducting part (external nostrils to terminal bronchioles) and the respiratory/exchange part. The alveoli and their ducts (alveolar sacs, alveolar ducts, and alveoli) form the exchange part, which is the actual site of diffusion of O2 and CO2 between blood and atmospheric air. Terminal bronchioles belong to the conducting zone and only transport air without performing gas exchange. Primary bronchi are large airways that carry air toward the lungs and do not exchange gases. The nasal chamber is part of the conducting zone responsible for filtering, humidifying, and warming incoming air, but does not participate in gas exchange with blood.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Exchange part is the site of actual diffusion of O2 and CO2 between blood and atmospheric air.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of sexual reproduction involves fusion of one large, non-motile female gamete with a smaller, motile male gamete?
questions.explanation: Oogamous reproduction is defined in NCERT as fusion between a large, non-motile female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete — seen in Volvox and Fucus. Isogamous reproduction involves flagellated or non-flagellated gametes that are similar in size. Anisogamous reproduction involves fusion of two gametes dissimilar in size, but both gametes are motile — this distinguishes it from oogamy where the female gamete is non-motile. Fragmentation is a vegetative method of reproduction, not a sexual one.
questions.ncert_line: Fusion between one large, non-motile (static) female gamete and a smaller, motile male gamete is termed oogamous, e.g., Volvox, Fucus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In algae, which classification system was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker for flowering plants?
questions.explanation: George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker gave the Natural classification system for flowering plants. The NCERT Biology textbook (Class 11, Chapter 2 - Biological Classification / Plant Kingdom) states that natural classification systems are based on natural affinities among organisms and include morphological, anatomical, physiological, and other characters. The system given by Bentham and Hooker is indeed a Natural classification system. Note: The question's preamble mentions 'In algae' which is irrelevant and likely an error in question framing, as Bentham and Hooker's classification pertains to flowering plants (Angiosperms), not algae. Artificial systems used only one or few morphological characters; Phylogenetic systems are based on evolutionary relationships; Numerical taxonomy uses computers and codes — none of these are attributed to Bentham and Hooker. The NCERT line cited is accurate: 'Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.'
questions.ncert_line: Such a classification for flowering plants was given by George Bentham and Joseph Dalton Hooker.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In algae, sexual reproduction in Spirogyra is called isogamous because the gametes are:

options.C: Dissimilar in size and both motile
options.D: Large and non-motile female gametes fusing with small motile male gametes

questions.explanation: NCERT states that in Spirogyra, isogamous reproduction involves non-flagellated (non-motile) but similar-sized gametes. Option A describes isogamy in Ulothrix, where gametes are flagellated and similar in size — this is correct for Ulothrix, not Spirogyra. Option C describes anisogamy, where gametes are dissimilar in size, as seen in Eudorina. Option D describes oogamy, seen in Volvox and Fucus, involving a large non-motile female gamete and a small motile male gamete.
questions.ncert_line: These gametes can be flagellated and similar in size (as in Ulothrix) or non-flagellated (non-motile) but similar in size (as in Spirogyra). Such reproduction is called isogamous.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which unicellular alga is rich in proteins and is used as a food supplement by space travellers?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that Chlorella is the unicellular alga rich in proteins used by space travellers. Chlamydomonas is also a unicellular green alga but is not mentioned in this context. Gelidium and Gracilaria are red algae used to obtain agar for growing microbes and preparing ice-creams and jellies — not as protein supplements for space travellers.
questions.ncert_line: Chlorella, a unicellular alga rich in proteins, is used as food supplement even by space travellers.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In brown algae (Phaeophyceae), the colour varies from olive green to various shades of brown depending on the amount of which pigment?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the xanthophyll pigment fucoxanthin is responsible for the colour variation in brown algae. r-phycoerythrin is the red pigment found in Rhodophyceae (red algae), not brown algae. Chlorophyll b is the dominant pigment in green algae (Chlorophyceae). Carotene is present in brown algae but it is not the pigment responsible for the colour variation described in the text.
questions.ncert_line: They vary in colour from olive green to various shades of brown depending upon the amount of the xanthophyll pigment, fucoxanthin present in them.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Watson and Crick proposed the double-helical structure of DNA in which year?
questions.explanation: Watson and Crick proposed the double-helical structure of DNA in 1953. This is clearly stated in NCERT Biology. The NCERT line cited ('resulted, early in March 1953, in the proposal of the complementary double-helical configuration') is accurate and comes from the chapter on Molecular Basis of Inheritance, not Reproductive Health. The distractors provided in the explanation (Option A being Watson's PhD year, Option B being a prize year, Option D being Crick's B.Sc. year) are not verified NCERT facts and appear to be fabricated details used to justify wrong options.
questions.ncert_line: resulted, early in March 1953, in the proposal of the complementary double-helical configuration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Genetics is a branch of biology that deals with which of the following?

options.A: Only the variation of characters in organisms
options.B: Only the inheritance of characters from parents to offspring
options.C: The inheritance as well as the variation of characters from parents to offspring
options.D: The artificial selection and domestication of wild animals

questions.explanation: Genetics deals with BOTH inheritance AND variation — as directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because it mentions only variation and excludes inheritance. Option B is wrong because it mentions only inheritance and excludes variation. Option D is wrong because artificial selection and domestication are examples of applying genetic knowledge, not the definition of genetics itself. Note: This definition appears in the NCERT Biology Chapter on Principles of Inheritance and Variation, not Reproductive Health.
questions.ncert_line: This subject deals with the inheritance, as well as the variation of characters from parents to offspring.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mendel observed that all F1 progeny plants from a cross between tall and dwarf pea plants were tall. What happened when these F1 tall plants were self-pollinated?

options.A: All F2 plants were tall, just like the F1 generation
options.B: F2 plants showed blending, producing plants of intermediate height
options.C: Both tall and dwarf plants appeared in F2 in the proportion 3:1
options.D: All F2 plants were dwarf, showing reversal of the F1 trait

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology, when F1 tall plants were self-pollinated, the F2 generation showed both tall and dwarf plants in a 3:1 ratio (3/4th tall and 1/4th dwarf). Option A is wrong because dwarf plants did reappear in F2. Option B is wrong because NCERT clearly states there was no blending — offspring were either tall or dwarf, none were of intermediate height. Option D is wrong because most F2 plants (3/4th) were tall, not dwarf. The correct answer is C. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 'Principles of Inheritance and Variation', not 'Reproductive Health'.
questions.ncert_line: Mendel then self-pollinated the tall F1 plants and to his surprise found that in the Filial 2 generation some of the offspring were 'dwarf'; the proportion of plants that were dwarf were 1/4th of the F2 plants while 3/4th of the F2 plants were tall.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mendel called the units of inheritance 'factors'. What do scientists call these 'factors' today, and what is the term used for slightly different forms of the same gene?

options.A: Genes; the different forms are called chromosomes
options.B: Genes; the different forms are called alles
options.C: Gametes; the different forms are called alles
options.D: Proteins; the different forms are called loci

questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that Mendel's 'factors' are now called genes, and slightly different forms of the same gene are called alles. Option A is wrong because different forms of a gene are called alles, not chromosomes. Option C is wrong because the modern term for Mendel's factors is genes, not gametes (gametes are reproductive cells). Option D is wrong because the units of inheritance are genes, not proteins, and different forms are alles, not loci.
questions.ncert_line: He called these things as 'factors'. Now we call them as genes. Genes which code for a pair of contrasting traits are known as alles, i.e., they are slightly different forms of the same gene.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What unique character of plant cells was concluded by Theodore Schwann based on his studies?
questions.explanation: Theodore Schwann concluded that the presence of cell wall is a unique character of plant cells — this is directly stated in NCERT. Nucleus is wrong because its discovery is credited to Robert Brown, not Schwann. Plasma membrane is wrong because Schwann reported that animal cells had a thin outer layer known as plasma membrane, which is common to all cells, not unique to plant cells. Chloroplasts are not mentioned in this NCERT passage at all.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: He also concluded, based on his studies on plant tissues, that the presence of cell wall is a unique character of the plant cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Rudolf Virchow modified the cell theory by explaing that new cells are formed from:
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, Rudolf Virchow stated that new cells arise from pre-existing cells, summarised by the Latin phrase 'Omnis cellula-e cellula'. Option B is wrong because Virchow rejected the idea of cells forming from non-living matter. Option C refers to the older discredited idea of spontaneous generation, which Virchow's work contradicted. Option D is incorrect because nuclear fragments were never described as the origin of new cells in this context.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell: The Unit of Life
questions.ncert_line: Rudolf Virchow (1855) first explained that cells divided and new cells are formed from pre-existing cells (Omnis cellula-e cellula).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes hormones according to their new scientific definition?

options.A: Non-nutrient chemicals that act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts
options.B: Nutrient chemicals secreted by duct glands directly into the target organ
options.C: Chemicals produced in large amounts by nerve fibres to regulate body functions
options.D: Proteins secreted by exocrine glands and transported through lymph

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly defines hormones as non-nutrient chemicals that act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts. Option B is wrong because hormones are produced by ductless (endocrine) glands, not duct glands. Option C is wrong because hormones are produced in trace amounts, not large amounts, and nerve fibres do not produce hormones. Option D is wrong because endocrine glands are ductless and hormones are transported through blood, not lymph.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals which act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the pituitary gland stores and releases oxytocin and vasopressin?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that neurohypophysis (posterior pituitary) stores and releases oxytocin and vasopressin. Option B is wrong because pars distalis (anterior pituitary) produces GH, PRL, TSH, ACTH, LH, and FSH — not oxytocin or vasopressin. Option C is wrong because pars intermedia secretes only MSH. Option D is wrong because adenohypophysis includes pars distalis and pars intermedia, neither of which releases oxytocin or vasopressin.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Neurohypophysis (pars nervosa) also known as posterior pituitary, stores and releases two hormones called oxytocin and vasopressin, which are actually synthesised by the hypothalamus and are transported axonally to neurohypophysis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the role of somatostatin secreted by the hypothalamus?

options.A: It inhibits the release of growth hormone from the pituitary
options.B: It stimulates the pituitary to release gonadotrophins
options.C: It stimulates the synthesis of thyroid stimulating hormone
options.D: It promotes the release of melanocyte stimulating hormone

questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that somatostatin inhibits the release of growth hormone from the pituitary. Option B is wrong because it is GnRH (Gonadotrophin releasing hormone), not somatostatin, that stimulates release of gonadotrophins. Option C is wrong because TSH synthesis is not linked to somatostatin; somatostatin is an inhibiting hormone. Option D is wrong because MSH is secreted by pars intermedia and is not regulated by somatostatin according to the text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Somatostatin from the hypothalamus inhibits the release of growth hormone from the pituitary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which hormone stimulates the resorption of water and electrolytes by the distal tubules of the kidney, thereby reducing water loss through urine?
questions.explanation: Vasopressin (ADH) acts on the distal tubules of the kidney to reabsorb water and electrolytes, reducing water loss — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option A (Oxytocin) is wrong because oxytocin acts on smooth muscles and stimulates uterine contraction and milk ejection, not kidney water resorption. Option C (Melatonin) is wrong because melatonin is secreted by the pineal gland and regulates the sleep-wake cycle and diurnal rhythms, not kidney function. Option D (Prolactin) is wrong because prolactin regulates growth of mammary glands and milk formation, not water balance.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: Vasopressin acts mainly at the kidney and stimulates resorption of water and electrolytes by the distal tubules and thereby reduces loss of water through urine (diuresis). Hence, it is also called as anti-diuretic hormone (ADH).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which condition results from impaired synthesis or release of ADH, leading to water loss and dehydration due to the kidney's diminished ability to conserve water?
questions.explanation: Diabetes Insipidus is directly caused by impaired ADH synthesis or release, reducing the kidney's ability to conserve water — exactly as stated in NCERT. Option A (Diabetes Mellitus) is wrong because it is related to insulin and blood glucose regulation, not ADH or water conservation. Option B (Cretinism) is wrong because it results from hypothyroidism during pregnancy, causing stunted growth and mental retardation in the baby. Option D (Acromegaly) is wrong because it is caused by excess secretion of growth hormone in adults, leading to disfigurement of facial features.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Chemical Coordination and Integration
questions.ncert_line: An impairment affecting synthesis or release of ADH results in a diminished ability of the kidney to conserve water leading to water loss and dehydration. This condition is known as Diabetes Insipidus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In animal cells, which organelle duplicates in the cytoplasm during the S phase of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: During the S phase (synthesis phase) of the cell cycle in animal cells, DNA replication occurs in the nucleus, and the centriole duplicates in the cytoplasm. This is consistent with NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter 10 (Cell Cycle and Cell Division), which states that during the S phase, the centriole duplicates in the cytoplasm while DNA replication takes place in the nucleus. Mitochondria replicate by binary fission independently of the cell cycle, and Golgi apparatus and ribosomes are not specifically duplicated during S phase.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: In animal cells, during the S phase, DNA replication begins in the nucleus, and the centriole duplicates in the cytoplasm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During anaphase of mitosis, which part of the chromosome remains directed towards the pole as it moves?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that during anaphase the centromere leads the movement towards the pole, with chromosome arms trailing behind. Chromatid arms are wrong because they trail behind, not lead. Kinetochore is wrong because although it attaches spindle fibres, the centromere (of which kinetochore is a surface structure) is described as the leading edge. Spindle fibre is wrong because it is not a part of the chromosome itself.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: As each chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate, the centromere of each chromosome remains directed towards the pole and hence at the leading edge, with the arms of the chromosome trailing behind.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which structures serve as the sites of attachment of spindle fibres to chromosomes during metaphase?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that kinetochores are small disc-shaped structures on the centromere surface that act as attachment sites for spindle fibres. Centrosomes are wrong because they are the organelles that organise spindle fibres, not the attachment sites on chromosomes. Asters are wrong because they are microtubules radiating from centrosomes, not sites on chromosomes. Chromatid arms are wrong because they are the lateral portions of chromosomes and do not attach to spindle fibres.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: Small disc-shaped structures at the surface of the centromeres are called kinetochores. These structures serve as the sites of attachment of spindle fibres to the chromosomes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Lactic acid bacteria (LAB) improve the nutritional quality of curd by increasing the content of which vitamin?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 10 - Microbes in Human Welfare) states that Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) improve the nutritional quality of curd by increasing Vitamin B12 content. LAB produce lactic acid that coagulates and partially digests milk proteins, converting milk to curd. This fermentation process results in increased Vitamin B12, making curd more nutritious than milk. Option A (Vitamin C) is an antioxidant not produced by LAB. Option B (Vitamin D) is synthesized in the skin upon sunlight exposure and not by bacterial fermentation. Option D (Vitamin A) is a fat-soluble vitamin unrelated to LAB fermentation activity.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: LAB produce acids that coagulate and partially digest the milk proteins... thus converting milk to curd, which also improves its nutritional quality by increasing vitamin B12.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Streptokinase, used as a 'clot buster' to remove clots from blood vessels of heart attack patients, is produced by which microorganism?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Streptokinase is produced by the bacterium Streptoccus and is used as a clot buster in heart attack patients. Option A (Lactobacillus) is a bacterium that produces lactic acid, not Streptokinase. Option B (Clostridium butylicum) is a bacterium that produces butyric acid. Option D (Acetobacter aceti) is a bacterium that produces acetic acid, not Streptokinase.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Human Health and Disease
questions.ncert_line: Streptokinase produced by the bacterium Streptoccus and modified by genetic engineering is used as a 'clot buster' for removing clots from the blood vessels of patients who have undergone myocardial infarction leading to heart attack.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Amniocentesis is used to analyse foetal cells and dissolved substances obtained from amniotic fluid. Which of the following genetic disorders can be detected by this procedure?

options.A: Down syndrome, haemophilia and sickle-cell anaemia
options.D: Colour blindness, night blindness and rickets

questions.explanation: NCERT specifically mentions Down syndrome, haemophilia and sickle-cell anaemia as examples of genetic disorders detected by amniocentesis. Option B lists infectious diseases caused by pathogens (bacteria, parasites, viruses), not genetic disorders detectable by foetal cell analysis. Option C lists lifestyle/metabolic diseases which are not diagnosed by amniocentesis. Option D lists conditions like colour blindness (which is hereditary) and night blindness and rickets (largely nutritional/deficiency-based), but none of these are mentioned in NCERT in the context of amniocentesis. The NCERT line cited is essentially accurate in content, though 'haemophilia' is misspelled as 'haemoplia' in the cited line — the actual NCERT text refers to haemophilia.
questions.ncert_line: This procedure is used to test for the presence of certain genetic disorders such as, down syndrome, haemoplia, sickle-cell anemia, etc., determine the survivability of the foetus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The lactational amenorrhea method of contraception is effective for a maximum period of how long following parturition?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the lactational amenorrhea method is effective only up to a maximum of six months after parturition. Option A (three months) is too short and not mentioned in the text. Option C (nine months) and Option D (twelve months) are longer durations that exceed what NCERT states, making them incorrect.
questions.ncert_line: this method has been reported to be effective only upto a maximum period of six months following parturition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the periodic abstinence method of contraception, couples avoid coitus during which days of the menstrual cycle?
questions.explanation: NCERT specifically states that couples abstain from coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, as ovulation is expected during this fertile period. Option A (day 1 to 7) corresponds to the menstrual phase, not the fertile period. Option B (day 5 to 12) is partially overlapping but incorrect as it does not match the NCERT text. Option D (day 18 to 28) is after the expected ovulation window and is not the fertile period described in NCERT.
questions.ncert_line: Periodic abstinence is one such method in which the couples avoid or abstain from coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle when ovulation could be expected.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Panchan Maheshwari was born in which city of Rajasthan?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that Panchan Maheshwari was born in Jaipur, Rajasthan. Option B (Jodhpur), Option C (Udaipur), and Option D (Ajmer) are all cities in Rajasthan but are not mentioned as his birthplace in the text.
questions.ncert_line: Born in November 1904 in Jaipur (Rajasthan) Panchan Maheshwari rose to become one of the most distinguished botanists not only of India but of the entire world.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is the end product of sexual reproduction in flowering plants?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text explicitly states that fruits and seeds are the end products of sexual reproduction in flowering plants. Option B is wrong because flowers and pollen are involved in the process, not the end products. Option C is wrong because roots and leaves are vegetative organs. Option D is wrong because spores and conidia are associated with asexual reproduction in lower organisms.
questions.ncert_line: All flowering plants show sexual reproduction. A look at the diversity of structures of the inflorescences, flowers and floral parts, shows an amazing range of adaptations to ensure formation of the end products of sexual reproduction, the fruits and seeds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Panchan Maheshwari established the Department of Botany as an important centre of research in embryology and tissue culture at which university?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that Maheshwari established the Department of Botany at the University of Delhi as a research centre for embryology and tissue culture. Option B is wrong because Allahabad is where he went for higher education, not where he established this department. Options C and D (Mumbai and Calcutta) are not mentioned in the text at all in this context.
questions.ncert_line: He established the Department of Botany, University of Delhi as an important centre of research in embryology and tissue culture.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which wall layer of the microsporangium nourishes the developing pollen grains?
questions.explanation: The tapetum is the innermost wall layer of the microsporangium and its primary function is to nourish the developing pollen grains — directly stated in NCERT. Endothecium is the second wall layer and is involved in protection and dehiscence, not nourishment. Epidermis is the outermost layer and performs protection. Middle layers also perform protection and help in dehiscence, not nourishment.
questions.ncert_line: The innermost wall layer is the tapetum. It nourishes the developing pollen grains.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process of formation of microspores from a pollen mother cell through meiosis is called:
questions.explanation: Microsporogenesis is the exact term used in NCERT for the formation of microspores from a pollen mother cell (PMC) through meiosis. Megasporogenesis refers to the formation of megaspores in the female reproductive organ, not microspores. Microgametogenesis refers to the development of the male gametophyte from the microspore, which is a later stage. Sporulation is a term used for asexual reproduction in lower organisms and is not relevant here.
questions.ncert_line: The process of formation of microspores from a pollen mother cell (PMC) through meiosis is called microsporogenesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which part of the polynucleotide chain forms the backbone of the molecule?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that the backbone is made of sugar and phosphates. Nitrogenous bases and phosphate groups is wrong because nitrogenous bases project outward from the backbone and are not part of it. Sugar and nitrogenous bases is wrong because nitrogenous bases project away from the backbone. Purines and pyrimidines refer only to the bases which project outward, not the backbone.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: The backbone of a polynucleotide chain is formed due to sugar and phosphates. The nitrogenous bases linked to sugar moiety project from the backbone.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who first identified DNA as an acidic substance present in the nucleus and named it 'Nuclein'?
questions.explanation: Friedrich Meischer first identified DNA as an acidic substance in the nucleus in 1869 and called it 'Nuclein' — directly stated in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 6 (Molecular Basis of Inheritance), not Chapter 5 (Principles of Inheritance and Variation). James Watson proposed the Double Helix model of DNA along with Francis Crick in 1953. Erwin Chargaff is known for his base pairing rules/base ratio observations. Maurice Wilkins (along with Rosalind Franklin) produced X-ray diffraction data used by Watson and Crick, but did not name DNA as Nuclein. The answer A is correct, but the chapter attribution in the subject line is incorrect — this fact belongs to Chapter 6: Molecular Basis of Inheritance.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: DNA as an acidic substance present in nucleus was first identified by Friedrich Meischer in 1869. He named it as 'Nuclein'.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the DNA double helix, which base pair is held together by three hydrogen bonds?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, Guanine pairs with Cytosine through three hydrogen bonds, while Adenine pairs with Thymine through only two hydrogen bonds. The correct answer is A. However, the NCERT line cited is from Chapter 6 (Molecular Basis of Inheritance), not Chapter 5 (Principles of Inheritance and Variation). The NCERT line itself is accurate: 'Adenine forms two hydrogen bonds with Thymine from opposite strand and vice-versa. Similarly, Guanine is bonded with Cytosine with three H-bonds.' Options C and D are incorrect because Adenine-Cytosine and Guanine-Thymine are not valid Watson-Crick base pairs in normal DNA.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: Adenine forms two hydrogen bonds with Thymine from opposite strand and vice-versa. Similarly, Guanine is bonded with Cytosine with three H-bonds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of white blood cells are responsible for secreting histamine, serotonin, and heparin?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, and heparin and are involved in inflammatory reactions. Option A is wrong because neutrophils are phagocytic cells that destroy foreign organisms. Option B is wrong because eosinophils resist infections and are associated with allergic reactions, but do not secrete histamine or heparin. Option D is wrong because monocytes are phagocytic cells, not secretors of histamine or heparin.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Basophils secrete histamine, serotonin, heparin, etc., and are involved in inflammatory reactions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A pregnant woman is Rh negative and her foetus is Rh positive. During the delivery of her first child, small amounts of Rh positive blood enter the mother's blood. What does the mother's body do in response?

options.A: The mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen
options.B: The mother starts producing Rh positive RBCs
options.C: The mother's platelets destroy the foetal Rh antigens immediately
options.D: The mother develops anti-A and anti-B antibodies in her plasma

questions.explanation: When an Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive blood from her foetus during delivery, her immune system responds by producing antibodies against the Rh antigen — this is directly stated in the NCERT text. Option B is wrong because the mother does not produce Rh positive RBCs; blood group characteristics are fixed. Option C is wrong because platelets are involved in clotting, not in immune responses against blood group antigens. Option D is wrong because anti-A and anti-B antibodies relate to ABO blood grouping, not Rh grouping.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: In such cases, the mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen in her blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During blood coagulation, fibrin threads are formed at the site of injury. What is the immediate source from which fibrin is produced?

options.A: Fibrinogens are converted to fibrins by the enzyme thrombin
options.B: Prothrombin is directly converted to fibrins by calcium ions
options.C: Platelets dissolve into fibrin threads upon contact with the injury site
options.D: Thrombokinase directly polymerises plasma proteins into fibrin

questions.explanation: According to NCERT, inactive fibrinogens present in plasma are converted into fibrins by the enzyme thrombin — fibrin forms the network of threads that traps blood cells to make a clot. Option B is wrong because prothrombin is converted to thrombin, not directly to fibrins; calcium ions assist the overall process but do not directly produce fibrin from prothrombin. Option C is wrong because platelets release factors that activate coagulation, but platelets themselves do not dissolve into fibrin. Option D is wrong because thrombokinase activates the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin, not the direct polymerisation of plasma proteins into fibrin.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Fibrins are formed by the conversion of inactive fibrinogens in the plasma by the enzyme thrombin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the definition of health as given in NCERT?

options.A: Absence of disease and physical fitness
options.B: A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being
options.C: A balance of bodily humors as described by Hippocrates
options.D: Efficient functioning of all organs without any symptoms

questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly defines health as a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being. Option A is wrong because NCERT clearly states health does NOT simply mean absence of disease or physical fitness. Option C is wrong because the humor hypothesis is an old, disproved idea. Option D is not the definition given in NCERT — it is a partial and incorrect description. Note: This content appears in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 8 (Human Health and Disease), not in the Evolution chapter.
questions.ncert_line: Health does not simply mean 'absence of disease' or 'physical fitness'. It could be defined as a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Through which two systems does the mind influence the immune system, according to the NCERT text?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 8 - Human Health and Disease) clearly states that the mind influences the immune system through the neural system and endocrine system. This concept is part of the discussion on how mental/psychological states can affect immunity. Option A is wrong because digestive and circulatory systems are not mentioned in this context. Option C is wrong because the lymphatic system is not the correct pairing here. Option D is wrong because the reproductive system is not mentioned in relation to mind and immunity in the NCERT text. Note: This topic belongs to Human Health and Disease, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: Biology stated that mind influences, through neural system and endocrine system, our immune system and that our immune system maintains our health.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which toxic substance is released when RBCs rupture during malarial infection, causing chill and high fever?
questions.explanation: Haemozoin is the toxic substance released during rupture of RBCs by Plasmodium, directly causing chill and high fever. Haemoglobin is the normal oxygen-carrying protein in RBCs and is not a toxin. Histamine is a chemical involved in allergic reactions, not produced during malarial RBC rupture. Sporozoite is the infectious form of Plasmodium that enters the human body, not a toxic substance released from RBCs. Note: This topic belongs to the chapter on Human Health and Disease, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: The rupture of RBCs is associated with release of a toxic substance, haemozoin, which is responsible for the chill and high fever recurring every three to four days.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Through which route does Salmonella typhi enter the human body to cause typhoid fever?

options.A: Food and water contaminated with the pathogen
options.B: Bite of an infected female mosquito
options.C: Inhaling droplets from an infected person
options.D: Direct contact with skin of an infected person

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Salmonella typhi enters the small intestine through contaminated food and water. Bite of an infected female mosquito is the route for Plasmodium, the malarial parasite, not Salmonella typhi. Inhaling droplets is the route for diseases like pneumonia and common cold, not typhoid. Direct skin contact is not mentioned as a route for typhoid fever in the NCERT text. Note: This topic is covered in the chapter on Human Health and Disease, not Evolution.
questions.ncert_line: These pathogens generally enter the small intestine through food and water contaminated with them and migrate to other organs through blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where does the breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy take place in eukaryotic cells?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Respiration in Plants), the breakdown of complex molecules to release energy occurs in the cytoplasm and mitochondria in eukaryotes. Option A is wrong because chloroplasts are the site of photosynthesis, not energy breakdown. Option C is wrong because ribosomes are involved in protein synthesis and vacuoles in storage. Option D is wrong because endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi apparatus are involved in protein processing and transport, not energy release.
questions.ncert_line: the breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy takes place in the cytoplasm and in the mitochondria (also only in eukaryotes).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which molecules are most commonly used as respiratory substrates in plants?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that carbohydrates are the usual respiratory substrates that are oxidised to release energy. Option A is wrong because proteins and fats can be used only under certain conditions, not usually. Option B is wrong because nucleic acids and vitamins are not mentioned as respiratory substrates in NCERT. Option D is wrong because organic acids and lipids are only alternative substrates used under special conditions, not the primary ones.
questions.ncert_line: Usually carbohydrates are oxidised to release energy, but proteins, fats and even organic acids can be used as respiratory substances in some plants, under certain conditions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which structures do plants use for gaseous exchange in place of specialised respiratory organs?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that plants use stomata and lenticels for gaseous exchange since they lack specialised respiratory organs. Option B is wrong because xylem and phloem are vascular tissues responsible for transport of water and food, not gas exchange. Option C is wrong because root hairs absorb water and trichomes have protective functions, not gaseous exchange. Option D is wrong because guard cells regulate stomatal opening but are part of the stomatal apparatus, and epidermal cells are not primarily involved in gaseous exchange.
questions.ncert_line: Plants, unlike animals, have no specialised organs for gaseous exchange but they have stomata and lenticels for this purpose.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which part of the cell does glycolysis occur?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm. Option A is wrong because mitochondria is the site of the Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, not glycolysis. Option B is wrong because the nucleus is involved in genetic functions, not metabolic breakdown of glucose. Option D is wrong because the endoplasmic reticulum is involved in protein and lipid synthesis, not glycolysis.
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm of the cell and is present in all living organisms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During glycolysis, glucose is first phosphorylated to form glucose-6-phosphate. Which enzyme is responsible for this step?
questions.explanation: Hexokinase catalyses the phosphorylation of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate using ATP during glycolysis. This is correct. Option A is wrong because invertase converts sucrose into glucose and fructose. Option B is wrong because isomerase converts glucose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate (the next step in glycolysis). Option D is wrong because phosphorylase breaks down starch/glycogen, not involved in this step. However, the cited NCERT line is slightly inaccurate — NCERT (Respiration in Plants) states that glucose is phosphorylated to glucose-6-phosphate by hexokinase, and separately that fructose can also be phosphorylated. The phrasing 'Glucose and fructose are phosphorylated to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate by the activity of the enzyme hexokinase' is not precise, as fructose is phosphorylated to fructose-6-phosphate, not glucose-6-phosphate. The core answer (hexokinase phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate) is correct per NCERT.
questions.ncert_line: Glucose and fructose are phosphorylated to give rise to glucose-6-phosphate by the activity of the enzyme hexokinase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who popularised the term 'Biodiversity' to describe the combined diversity at all levels of biological organisation?
questions.explanation: Edward Wilson, a sociobiologist, popularised the term 'Biodiversity' — this is directly stated in NCERT. Robert May is wrong because he gave an estimate of global species diversity, not the term biodiversity. Charles Darwin is associated with the theory of evolution, not this term. Ernst Haeckel coined the term 'ecology', not biodiversity.
questions.ncert_line: Biodiversity is the term popularised by the sociobiologist Edward Wilson to describe the combined diversity at all the levels of biological organisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to Robert May, what is the scientifically sound estimate of global species diversity?
questions.explanation: Robert May's conservative and scientifically sound estimate is about 7 million species. 1.5 million is the number of species already described by IUCN (2004), not the total estimate. 20 million and 50 million are extreme estimates mentioned in the text, not Robert May's figure.
questions.ncert_line: A more conservative and scientifically sound estimate made by Robert May places the global species diversity at about 7 million.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Among all animals on Earth, which group is the most species-rich taxonomic group?
questions.explanation: Insects are the most species-rich group among animals, making up more than 70 per cent of all animal species — directly stated in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations). Mammals, Reptiles, and Fishes are far fewer in number of species compared to insects. The NCERT text clearly states that among animals, insects are the most species-rich taxonomic group, making up more than 70 per cent of the total.
questions.ncert_line: Among animals, insects are the most species-rich taxonomic group, making up more than 70 per cent of the total.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which region on Earth has the greatest biodiversity and is home to more than 40,000 species of plants?

options.A: The Amazonian rain forest in South America
options.C: The temperate forests of the Midwest USA
options.D: The forests of Colombia near the equator

questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that the largely tropical Amazonian rain forest in South America has the greatest biodiversity on Earth — it is home to more than 40,000 species of plants, 3,000 of fishes, 1,300 of birds, 427 of mammals, 427 of amphibians, 378 of reptiles and more than 1,25,000 invertebrates. Option B is wrong because India, though a mega-diversity country, is not described as having the greatest biodiversity on Earth. Option C is wrong because temperate forests have far fewer species than tropical ones. Option D is partially misleading — Colombia is indeed mentioned in NCERT for its bird species (1,400 species), but the greatest overall biodiversity on Earth is attributed to the Amazon, not Colombia. The NCERT line is from Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations) / Chapter 15 (Biodiversity and Conservation) depending on edition, and the fact is accurately cited.
questions.ncert_line: The largely tropical Amazonian rain forest in South America has the greatest biodiversity on earth- it is home to more than 40,000 species of plants, 3,000 of fishes, 1,300 of birds, 427 of mammals, 427 of amphibians, 378 of reptiles and of more than 1,25,000 invertebrates.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why do tropical regions have greater species diversity compared to temperate regions?

options.A: Tropics have remained relatively undisturbed for millions of years, allowing a long evolutionary time for species diversification
