options.B: Tropics receive less solar energy, which reduces competion among species
options.C: Tropical regions experience frequent glaciations that promote new species formation
options.D: Temperate regions have more seasonal variation, which increases niche specialisation

questions.explanation: The NCERT text states that tropical latitudes remained undisturbed for millions of years giving species a long evolutionary time to diversify — this is why Option A is correct. Option B is wrong because the text says tropics receive MORE solar energy, which contributes to higher productivity and greater diversity, not less energy. Option C is wrong because it is the TEMPERATE regions, not the tropics, that experienced frequent glaciations. Option D is wrong because the text says constant (less seasonal) tropical environments promote niche specialisation, not temperate ones.
questions.ncert_line: Speciation is generally a function of time, unlike temperate regions subjected to frequent glaciations in the past, tropical latitudes have remained relatively undisturbed for millions of years and thus, had a long evolutionary time for species diversification.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which special vascularised structures are used by most aquatic arthropods and molluscs for gas exchange?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that gills are the special vascularised structures used by most aquatic arthropods and molluscs for gas exchange. Lungs are wrong because they are vascularised bags used by terrestrial forms, not aquatic arthropods and molluscs. Tracheal tubes are wrong because they are used by insects to transport atmospheric air. Moist cuticle is wrong because that is the gas exchange structure of earthworms.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases
questions.ncert_line: Special vascularised structures called gills (branchial respiration) are used by most of the aquatic arthropods and molluscs whereas vascularised bags called lungs (pulmonary respiration) are used by the terrestrial forms for the exchange of gases.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots that originate from the base of the stem. What is this type of root system called?
questions.explanation: The fibrous root system is formed in monocots when the primary root dies and is replaced by many roots from the stem base — this is directly stated in NCERT using wheat as an example. Tap root system is wrong because it is formed by elongation of the radicle in dicots like mustard. Adventious root system is wrong because adventious roots arise from parts other than the radicle, seen in grass and banyan. Lateral root system is wrong because lateral roots are branches of the primary root, not a separate root system type.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In monocotyledonous plants, the primary root is short lived and is replaced by a large number of roots. These roots originate from the base of the stem and constitute the fibrous root system, as seen in the wheat plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which plant can the tap root system be clearly seen, as described in NCERT?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the tap root system is seen in the mustard plant. Wheat is wrong because wheat is given as an example of a fibrous root system. Grass is wrong because grass is mentioned as a plant with adventious roots. Monstera is also wrong because it is mentioned alongside banyan and grass as having adventious roots, not a tap root system.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: The primary roots and its branches constitute the tap root system, as seen in the mustard plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Roots that arise from parts of a plant other than the radicle are known as which type of roots?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly defines adventious roots as roots that arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle, with grass, Monstera, and banyan as examples. Secondary roots are wrong because they are lateral branches of the primary root. Tertiary roots are wrong because they are branches of secondary roots. Lateral roots are wrong because they are branches that arise from the primary root, not from non-radicle parts.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: In some plants, like grass, Monstera and the banyan tree, roots arise from parts of the plant other than the radicle and are called adventious roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The region of the root where epidermal cells form fine, thread-like structures called root hairs is responsible for which function?

options.A: Absorption of water and minerals from the soil
options.B: Protection of the tender root apex
options.C: Rapid elongation and growth of the root in length
options.D: Repeated division of small thin-walled cells

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that root hairs absorb water and minerals from the soil. Option B describes the function of the root cap, not root hairs. Option C describes the region of elongation, which is responsible for root growth in length. Option D describes the region of meristematic activity where cells divide repeatedly.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Morphology of Flowering Plants
questions.ncert_line: From this region some of the epidermal cells form very fine and delicate, thread-like structures called root hairs. These root hairs absorb water and minerals from the soil.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ramdeo Misra is known by which special title in India?

options.C: Father of Environmental Science in India
options.D: Father of Conservation Biology in India

questions.explanation: Ramdeo Misra is indeed known as the 'Father of Ecology in India.' He was a pioneering ecologist who established ecology as a discipline in India and is widely recognized by this title. However, this fact appears in the NCERT Biology textbook in the chapter related to Ecology (organisms and populations or biodiversity chapters), NOT in the chapter 'Biotechnology: Principles and Processes' as claimed. The answer A is correct, but the chapter attribution is wrong. Options B, C, and D are incorrect as explained — his specific title relates to Ecology, not Botany, Environmental Science, or Conservation Biology.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ramdeo Misra is revered as the Father of Ecology in India.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ecology is basically concerned with how many levels of biological organisation?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations), ecology is basically concerned with four levels of biological organisation – organisms, populations, communities, and biomes. So Option A (Four levels) is correct. However, it should be noted that the chapter cited in the question is wrong — this fact comes from Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations), not from 'Biotechnology: Principles and Processes'. The answer A is correct, but the subject chapter attribution is incorrect.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ecology is basically concerned with four levels of biological organisation – organisms, populations, communities and biomes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When measuring the population density of a single huge banyan tree alongside many carrot grass plants, which measure is more meaningful than total number?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations) specifically states that for species like a banyan tree, where total number is misleading, per cent cover or biomass is a more meaningful measure of population size. Option B is wrong because pug marks are used for estimating tiger populations, not for plants like banyan. Option C is wrong because sex ratio describes the proportion of males and females, which is unrelated to measuring plant population size. Option D is wrong because number of individuals per trap is used for fish population estimates in lakes, not for plants. Note: The answer A is correct, but the chapter cited is wrong — this topic belongs to Chapter 13: Organisms and Populations, not Biotechnology: Principles and Processes.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: In such cases, the per cent cover or biomass is a more meaningful measure of the population size.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The capacity of a cell or explant to generate a whole plant is called:
questions.explanation: Totipotency is the correct term for the ability of any cell or explant to develop into a complete plant. This concept is from NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 9 (Biotechnology: Principles and Processes), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare' (Chapter 10). The NCERT line 'This capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency' is accurate. Somatic hybridisation involves fusion of protoplasts from different species. Micro-propagation refers to producing large numbers of plants via tissue culture. Organogenesis refers to the process of development of organs. The answer A (Totipotency) is correct, but the chapter attribution in the subject field is wrong — it belongs to Biotechnology chapter, not Microbes in Human Welfare.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: This capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When protoplasts from two different plant varieties are fused to produce a new hybrid plant, the resulting hybrid plant is called a:
questions.explanation: Somatic hybrids are specifically the plants formed when protoplasts of two different plant varieties are fused, as stated in NCERT. Pomato is an example of such a hybrid. Somaclones are genetically identical copies of a single parent plant produced by micro-propagation — they do not involve fusion. A genetically modified organism (GMO) refers to organisms whose genes have been altered by manipulation, which is a broader and different concept. Transgenic plants are those that carry a foreign gene introduced by genetic engineering, not by protoplast fusion. The NCERT line cited is accurate, but the chapter attribution is incorrect — this topic is covered in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 9: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production (under the section on Plant Breeding and Tissue Culture), not in 'Microbes in Human Welfare'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: Isolated protoplasts from two different varieties of plants – each having a desirable character – can be fused to get hybrid protoplasts, which can be further grown to form a new plant. These hybrids are called somatic hybrids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Bt toxin produced by Bacillus thuringiensis remains inactive inside the bacterium. What converts this inactive protoxin into an active toxin inside an insect?

options.A: The acidic pH of the insect's stomach
options.B: The alkaline pH of the insect's gut
options.C: Digestive enzymes secreted by the insect's midgut
options.D: High temperature inside the insect's body

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 12 - Biotechnology and its Applications), the Bt toxin exists as inactive protoxin crystals inside the bacterium. When an insect ingests these crystals, the alkaline pH of the insect's gut solubilizes the crystals and converts the inactive protoxin into its active toxic form. The active toxin then binds to the epithelial cells of the midgut, creating pores that cause cell swelling and lysis, ultimately killing the insect. Option A is wrong because the pH is alkaline, not acidic. Option C is wrong because it is the alkaline pH, not digestive enzymes specifically, that activates the toxin by solubilizing the crystals. Option D is wrong because temperature is never mentioned as the activating factor in NCERT.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: once an insect ingest the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the gut which solubilise the crystals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A novel strategy was used to protect tobacco plants from the nematode Meloidogyne incognita. Which cellular process formed the basis of this strategy?
questions.explanation: RNA interference (RNAi) was the basis of the strategy used to protect tobacco plants from the nematode Meloidogyne incognita. Using Agrobacterium tumefaciens, nematode-specific genes were introduced into the host plant. The introduced DNA produced both sense and antisense RNA in the host cells, which formed double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) that triggered RNAi, silencing the specific mRNA of the nematode and preventing its survival. Option A is wrong because homologous recombination is a DNA repair/exchange process, not used here. Option B is wrong because transcription factor silencing is not mentioned in this context. Option D is wrong because protein crystal formation is associated with Bt toxin in Bacillus thuringiensis, not with nematode resistance. Note: This topic is covered in NCERT Biology Chapter 12 (Biotechnology and its Applications), not in 'Microbes in Human Welfare'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: A novel strategy was adopted to prevent this infestation which was based on the process of RNA interference (RNAi). RNAi takes place in all eukaryotic organisms as a method of cellular defense.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why are green plants called autotrophs?

options.A: Because they make their own food through photosynthesis
options.B: Because they depend on other organisms for food
options.C: Because they absorb minerals directly from soil
options.D: Because they store food in the form of starch only

questions.explanation: Green plants are called autotrophs because they synthesise their own food through photosynthesis — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option B describes heterotrophs, not autotrophs. Option C refers to mineral absorption, which is not why plants are called autotrophs. Option D is incorrect because autotrophs are not defined by starch storage alone.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: The green plants make or rather synthesise the food they need through photosynthesis and are therefore called autotrophs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which scientist used radioactive carbon (C14) to study the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Melvin Calvin, along with J.A. Bassham, used C14-labelled carbon dioxide to map the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis, earning him the Nobel Prize in 1961. This pathway is now known as the Calvin cycle. Charles Darwin is known for the theory of evolution. Louis Pasteur is known for germ theory. Joseph Priestley is associated with early oxygen experiments but did not use radioactive tracers.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: He along with J.A. Bassham studied reactions in green plants forming sugar and other substances from raw materials like carbon dioxide, water and minerals by labelling the carbon dioxide with C14.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What does the experiment using KOH-soaked cotton inside a test tube around part of a leaf demonstrate?

options.A: That carbon dioxide is required for photosynthesis
options.B: That light is required for photosynthesis
options.C: That chlorophyll is required for photosynthesis
options.D: That water is required for photosynthesis

questions.explanation: KOH-soaked cotton absorbs CO2 from the enclosed part of the leaf. Since that part shows no starch formation while the exposed part does, the experiment proves that CO2 is necessary for photosynthesis. Light requirement is shown by the black paper experiment, not this one. Chlorophyll requirement is shown by the variegated leaf experiment. Water requirement is not demonstrated by this particular experiment.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: The exposed part of the leaf tested positive for starch while the portion that was in the tube, tested negative. This showed that CO2 was required for photosynthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Jan Ingenhousz used an aquatic plant in his experiment. What did he observe around the green parts of the plant in bright sunlight?

options.A: Small bubbles of oxygen were formed
options.B: Small bubbles of carbon dioxide were formed
options.C: The green parts turned yellow due to heat
options.D: The plant released water droplets from its surface

questions.explanation: According to NCERT, Ingenhousz observed small bubbles forming around the green parts of an aquatic plant in bright sunlight, and these bubbles were later identified as oxygen. Option B is wrong because the bubbles were oxygen, not carbon dioxide. Option C is wrong because no such colour change is mentioned in NCERT. Option D is wrong because water droplet release (transpiration) is a different process and is not described in this context.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: Ingenhousz in an elegant experiment with an aquatic plant showed that in bright sunlight, small bubbles were formed around the green parts while in the dark they did not. Later he identified these bubbles to be of oxygen.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cornelius van Niel studied purple and green sulphur bacteria. What did he conclude about the oxygen released during photosynthesis in green plants?

options.A: The oxygen comes from water, not from carbon dioxide
options.B: The oxygen comes from carbon dioxide, not from water
options.C: The oxygen comes equally from both water and carbon dioxide
options.D: The oxygen is released only when hydrogen sulphide is the hydrogen donor

questions.explanation: Van Niel inferred that in green plants, the oxygen released during photosynthesis comes from water (H2O) and not from carbon dioxide. He based this on his observation that purple and green sulphur bacteria use H2S as hydrogen donor and release sulphur instead of oxygen. By analogy, in green plants, water serves as the hydrogen donor and oxygen is released as a byproduct. Option B is the exact opposite of what van Niel concluded. Option C is wrong because NCERT clearly states it is from water, not both sources. Option D is wrong because oxygen release from H2S is associated with sulphur bacteria, not green plants.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: Hence, he inferred that the O2 evolved by the green plant comes from H2O, not from carbon dioxide. This was later proved by using radioisotopic techniques.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which nitrogenous waste requires the largest amount of water for its elimination from the body?
questions.explanation: Ammonia is the most toxic nitrogenous waste and requires the largest amount of water for its elimination — this is directly stated in NCERT. Urea is wrong because while it needs water, it is not the one requiring the largest amount. Uric acid is the opposite — it needs minimum water. Creatine is not discussed in this context in the NCERT text.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Ammonia is the most toxic form and requires large amount of water for its elimination, whereas uric acid, being the least toxic, can be removed with a minimum loss of water.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which excretory structure is found in the cephalochordate Amphioxus?
questions.explanation: Protonephridia (also called flame cells) are the excretory structures present in Amphioxus, the cephalochordate — directly stated in NCERT. Nephridia are found in earthworms and other annelids, not in Amphioxus. Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of insects like cockroaches. Antennal glands (green glands) are found in crustaceans like prawns.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Protonephridia or flame cells are the excretory structures in Platyhelminthes (Flatworms, e.g., Planaria), rotifers, some annelids and the cephalochordate – Amphioxus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where does conversion of ammonia into urea take place in ureotelic animals?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that ammonia is converted into urea in the liver of ureotelic animals. The kidneys are wrong because they only filter and excrete the urea — they do not produce it. Blood is wrong because urea is only transported through blood after being made in the liver, not converted there. The urinary bladder only stores urine and plays no role in urea formation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Ammonia produced by metabolism is converted into urea in the liver of these animals and released into the blood which is filtered and excreted out by the kidneys.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule are called podocytes. What are the minute spaces left between these podocytes called?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the minute spaces between podocytes are called filtration slits or slit pores. Option B is wrong — stomata and micro pores are terms not used in this context in NCERT. Option C is wrong — lacunae and canaliculi are terms related to bone tissue, not nephron structure. Option D is wrong — fenestrae refer to openings in capillary endothelium, and tight junctions are cell connections, neither is the NCERT term for these spaces.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: The epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule called podocytes are arranged in an intricate manner so as to leave some minute spaces called filtration slits or slit pores.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which segment of the nephron is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium that increases surface area for reabsorption?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that the PCT is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium, which increases surface area for reabsorption. Option B is wrong — DCT is not described as having a brush border epithelium in NCERT. Option C is wrong — collecting ducts are straight tubes that collect filtrate, not described with brush border epithelium. Option D is wrong — the descending limb of Henle's loop is not associated with brush border epithelium in NCERT.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: PCT is lined by simple cuboidal brush border epithelium which increases the surface area for reabsorption.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What type of cell organisation is found in Kingdom Monera?
questions.explanation: Monera has prokaryotic cells and no nuclear membrane — both facts are clearly given in Table 2.1 of NCERT. Option A is wrong because Monera cells are prokaryotic, not eukaryotic, and they lack a nuclear membrane. Option C is wrong because eukaryotic cells without a nuclear membrane is a contradictory combination not applicable to Monera. Option D is wrong because prokaryotic cells by definition lack a nuclear membrane.
questions.ncert_line: Cell type: Monera — Prokaryotic; Nuclear membrane: Monera — Absent.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the main component of the cell wall in fungi according to the Five Kingdom classification?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that fungi have chitin in their cell walls. Option A (cellulose) is wrong because cellulose is the cell wall component of Plantae, not Fungi. Option B (peptidoglycan) is wrong because peptidoglycan describes the cell wall of Monera (prokaryotes), not Fungi. Option D (polysaccharide with amino acids) is also wrong for the same reason — it refers to the Monera cell wall composition as described in NCERT Table 2.1. Chitin is a long-chain polymer of N-acetylglucosamine, and is the defing cell wall component of fungi in the Five Kingdom classification system proposed by R.H. Whittaker.
questions.ncert_line: The classification did not differentiate between the heterotrophic group – fungi, and the autotrophic green plants, though they also showed a characteristic difference in their walls composition – the fungi had chitin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Methanogens present in the gut of ruminant animals are responsible for producing which gas from the dung of these animals?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text directly states that methanogens produce methane, also called biogas, from the dung of ruminant animals like cows and buffaloes. Carbon dioxide is a common gas but is not the product specifically associated with methanogens here. Hydrogen sulphide is produced by some sulphur bacteria, not methanogens. Ammonia is oxidised by chemosynthetic bacteria as an energy source, not produced by methanogens.
questions.ncert_line: Methanogens are present in the gut of several ruminant animals such as cows and buffaloes and they are responsible for the production of methane (biogas) from the dung of these animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to centrioles in animal cells during the S phase of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: During the S phase of the cell cycle in animal cells, DNA replication occurs in the nucleus and the centrioles duplicate in the cytoplasm. This is stated in NCERT Biology Class 11, Chapter 10 (Cell Cycle and Cell Division). Option A is wrong because centrioles do not disappear; they duplicate. Option B is wrong because centrioles duplicate in the cytoplasm, not the nucleus. Option D is wrong because DNA replication is initiated at origins of replication in the nucleus, not by centrioles.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: In animal cells, during the S phase, DNA replication begins in the nucleus, and the centriole duplicates in the cytoplasm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cells that exit the G1 phase and stop dividing enter which stage of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that cells which do not divide further exit G1 to enter the quiescent stage (G0), an inactive stage. Option A is wrong because S phase is where DNA replication occurs, not where non-dividing cells rest. Option B is wrong because G2 phase is for cells actively preparing for mitosis. Option D is wrong because M phase is the phase of actual cell division, not a resting stage.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: These cells that do not divide further exit G1 phase to enter an inactive stage called quiescent stage (G0) of the cell cycle.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During anaphase of mitosis, which part of the chromosome remains directed towards the pole as it moves away from the equatorial plate?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that during anaphase, the centromere leads the movement towards the pole with arms trailing behind. Option A (chromosome arms) is wrong because the arms trail behind, they do not lead. Option B (kinetochore) is wrong because kinetochores are structures on the centromere that attach spindle fibres — they are not described as the leading part during movement. Option D (sister chromatid) is wrong because by anaphase, sister chromatids have already separated and are now called daughter chromosomes.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division
questions.ncert_line: As each chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate, the centromere of each chromosome remains directed towards the pole and hence at the leading edge, with the arms of the chromosome trailing behind.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When a living tissue is ground in trichloroacetic acid, what is the liquid fraction that passes through the cheesecloth called?
questions.explanation: The liquid fraction that passes through the cheesecloth is called the filtrate or acid-soluble pool, as directly stated in NCERT. Option B is wrong because the acid-insoluble fraction is the retentate — the part that does NOT pass through. Option C is wrong because ash is what remains after burning the tissue, a completely different step. Option D is wrong because dry weight residue is obtained by drying the tissue to remove water, not by filtering through cheesecloth.
questions.ncert_line: One is called the filtrate or more technically, the acid-soluble pool, and the second, the retentate or the acid-insoluble fraction.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When a living tissue is fully burnt during elemental analysis, what is the material that remains after all carbon compounds are removed as gases?
questions.explanation: After burning, all carbon compounds leave as CO2 and water vapour, and what remains is called ash, which contains inorganic elements like calcium and magnesium. Option B is wrong because dry weight is obtained simply by drying the tissue to remove water — burning is not involved. Option C is wrong because the acid-soluble pool is the filtrate from trichloroacetic acid treatment, not from burning. Option D is wrong because retentate is the solid fraction remaing after cheesecloth filtration, not after burning.
questions.ncert_line: What is remaing is called 'ash'. This ash contains inorganic elements (like calcium, magnesium etc).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: On which carbon atom are both the amino group and the acidic group attached in an amino acid?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that both the amino group and the acidic group are attached to the same carbon called the α-carbon, which is why these molecules are called α-amino acids. Option B is wrong because the β-carbon is a different position on the carbon chain and is not described as the site of both groups in NCERT. Option C is wrong because the R group is a variable substituent on the α-carbon, not a separate carbon bearing both functional groups. Option D is wrong because the carboxyl carbon is part of the acidic group itself, not the central α-carbon that bears all four substituents.
questions.ncert_line: Amino acids are organic compounds containg an amino group and an acidic group as substituents on the same carbon i.e., the α-carbon. Hence, they are called α-amino acids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: When a nitrogen base is attached to a sugar and also has a phosphate group esterified to the sugar, the resulting molecule is called a:
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, when a nitrogen base is attached to a sugar AND a phosphate group is also esterified to the sugar, the molecule is called a nucleotide. Option B (nucleoside) is wrong because a nucleoside has only a nitrogen base attached to a sugar — without the phosphate group. Option C (nucleic acid) is wrong because nucleic acids are large polymers made of many nucleotides joined together. Option D (nitrogen base) is wrong because nitrogen bases alone do not include sugar or phosphate components.
questions.ncert_line: If a phosphate group is also found esterified to the sugar they are called nucleotides.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Alkaloids, flavonoids, essential oils, and antibiotics found in plant and fungal cells are examples of:
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that compounds like alkaloids, flavonoids, essential oils, and antibiotics found in plant, fungal, and microbial cells are called secondary metabolites. Option B (primary metabolites) is wrong because primary metabolites are compounds like amino acids and sugars that have known roles in normal physiological processes and are found in animal tissues as well. Option C (phospholipids) is wrong because phospholipids are lipids containg phosphorous found in cell membranes, unrelated to this category. Option D (nucleosides) is wrong because nucleosides are nitrogen bases attached to a sugar, which is a completely different type of molecule.
questions.ncert_line: When one analyses plant, fungal and microbial cells, one would see thousands of compounds other than these primary metabolites, e.g. alkaloids, flavonoids, rubber, essential oils, antibiotics, coloured pigments, scents, gums, spices. These are called secondary metabolites.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the NCERT text, what do humans know as early as 8000–1000 B.C. about variation?

options.A: That variation was caused by mutations in DNA
options.B: That one of the causes of variation was hidden in sexual reproduction
options.C: That variation was the result of artificial selection alone
options.D: That variation was caused by changes in the environment

questions.explanation: While the answer B is selected correctly, the NCERT line cited appears to be inaccurate or hallucinated. The NCERT Biology textbook (Class 12, Chapter 3 - Human Reproduction or Chapter on Reproductive Health) does not contain this specific statement about humans knowing 'as early as 8000-1000 B.C. that one of the causes of variation was hidden in sexual reproduction.' The actual NCERT text in the Evolution chapter (Class 12, Chapter 7) mentions that humans knew from as early as 8000-1000 B.C. about the existence of variation and used it for domestication and selective breeding of plants and animals. The specific phrasing about 'variation hidden in sexual reproduction' being known to ancient humans is not a direct NCERT line and appears to be hallucinated or misquoted. The chapter on Reproductive Health does not contain this content at all.
questions.ncert_line: Humans knew from as early as 8000-1000 B.C. that one of the causes of variation was hidden in sexual reproduction.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mendel observed that all F1 progeny from a cross between tall and dwarf pea plants were tall. What happened when these tall F1 plants were self-pollinated?

options.A: All F2 plants were tall, just like F1
options.B: F2 plants showed a mix of tall and dwarf in the proportion 3:1
options.C: F2 plants were all dwarf because the tall trait disappeared
options.D: F2 plants showed an in-between height due to blending of traits

questions.explanation: According to NCERT, self-pollination of tall F1 plants produced F2 offspring in a 3:1 ratio — 3/4th tall and 1/4th dwarf. Option A is wrong because the dwarf trait reappeared in F2. Option C is wrong because the majority of F2 plants were tall, not all dwarf. Option D is wrong because NCERT clearly states there was no blending — all offspring were either tall or dwarf, none were of in-between height. Note: This topic belongs to the chapter 'Principles of Inheritance and Variation', not 'Reproductive Health'.
questions.ncert_line: The proportion of plants that were dwarf were 1/4th of the F2 plants while 3/4th of the F2 plants were tall.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mendel used 'true-breeding' pea plant lines for his experiments. What correctly describes a true-breeding line?

options.A: A plant line that produces offspring with new traits after cross-pollination
options.B: A plant line that shows stable trait inheritance and expression for several generations after continuous self-pollination
options.C: A plant line that always produces hybrid offspring showing two contrasting traits
options.D: A plant line that undergoes continuous cross-pollination to maintain its traits

questions.explanation: NCERT defines a true-breeding line as one that undergoes continuous self-pollination and shows stable trait inheritance over several generations. Option A is wrong because true-breeding lines do not produce new traits — they show stable, unchanged traits. Option C is wrong because true-breeding lines do not produce hybrids with two contrasting traits; they breed true to one trait. Option D is wrong because true-breeding involves self-pollination, not cross-pollination. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 5 (Principles of Inheritance and Variation), not Reproductive Health.
questions.ncert_line: A true-breeding line is one that, having undergone continuous self-pollination, shows the stable trait inheritance and expression for several generations.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which protective mechanism allows frogs to change their colour to hide from enemies?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the protective coloration by which frogs change colour to hide from enemies is called micry. Option A (aestivation) is summer sleep, not colour change. Option C (hibernation) is winter sleep. Option D (camouflage reflex) is a made-up term; NCERT uses 'camouflage' only as a description of the phenomenon, while the specific term given is micry.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: They have the ability to change the colour to hide them from their enemies (camouflage). This protective coloration is called micry.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which feature helps to distinguish a male frog from a female frog?

options.A: Presence of webbed digits on hind limbs
options.B: Presence of a nictitating membrane over the eyes
options.C: Presence of vocal sacs and a copulatory pad on the first digit of fore limbs
options.D: Presence of a tympanum on either side of the eyes

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that vocal sacs and a copulatory pad on the first digit of the fore limbs are present only in male frogs and absent in females. Option A (webbed digits) is present in both sexes for swimming. Option B (nictitating membrane) is present in all frogs to protect eyes in water. Option D (tympanum) is present on either side of eyes in all frogs to receive sound signals.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: Male frogs can be distinguished by the presence of sound producing vocal sacs and also a copulatory pad on the first digit of the fore limbs which are absent in female frogs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the term used for the deep sleep that frogs undergo during peak winter season?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that winter sleep in frogs is called hibernation. Option A (aestivation) is specifically the summer sleep, not winter sleep — a common confusion. Option B (micry) refers to resemblance for protection, not seasonal sleep. Option C (poikilothermy) refers to the inability to maintain a constant body temperature, not seasonal sleep.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: During this period they take shelter in deep burrows to protect them from extreme heat and cold. This is known as summer sleep (aestivation) and winter sleep (hibernation) respectively.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a frog, which structure receives blood from major veins called vena cava?
questions.explanation: The sinus venosus is the triangular structure that receives blood from the major veins called vena cava — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option A (left atrium) is wrong because sinus venosus joins the RIGHT atrium, not the left. Option B (conus arteriosus) is wrong because it is a sac-like structure on the ventral side into which the ventricle opens, carrying blood away from the heart. Option D (ventricle) is wrong because the ventricle is a pumping chamber that opens into the conus arteriosus, not the structure receiving blood from vena cava.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Structural Organisation in Animals
questions.ncert_line: A triangular structure called sinus venosus joins the right atrium. It receives blood through the major veins called vena cava.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ernst Mayr pioneered the currently accepted definition of which biological concept?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that Ernst Mayr pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species. Option B (natural selection) is associated with Charles Darwin, not Mayr. Option C (genetic variation) is a broader genetics concept not attributed to Mayr in the text. Option D (taxonomic hierarchy) is related to taxonomy and classification, not specifically credited to Mayr here.
questions.ncert_line: He also pioneered the currently accepted definition of a biological species.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which term refers to the number and types of organisms present on Earth?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly defines biodiversity as the number and types of organisms present on Earth. Option B (taxonomy) refers to the science of identification, naming, and classification of organisms, not the count of types. Option C (systematics) deals with the diversity and relationships among organisms but is not the term for the number and types present on Earth. Option D (nomenclature) refers specifically to the system of naming organisms, not to their diversity or numbers.
questions.ncert_line: This refers to biodiversity or the number and types of organisms present on earth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The scientific name of mango is Mangifera indica. In this name, what does 'Mangifera' represent?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT, in the binomial name Mangifera indica, 'Mangifera' represents the genus. Option A is wrong because 'indica' is the specific epithet, not 'Mangifera'. Option C is wrong because the author's name (e.g., Linn.) appears after the specific epithet at the end of the biological name. Option D is wrong because 'family' is a different taxonomic category and is not what 'Mangifera' denotes here.
questions.ncert_line: In this name Mangifera represents the genus while indica, is a particular species, or a specific epithet.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which code governs the scientific naming of plants according to the NCERT text?
questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology Class 11 (Chapter 1 - The Living World), the scientific naming of plants is governed by the International Code of Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN). The correct expansion uses 'of' not 'for'. However, among the given options, Option C ('International Code for Botanical Nomenclature') is the closest match and is the intended correct answer. Option A is wrong because ICZN applies to animals. Option B incorrectly uses 'Binomial' in the expansion. Option D incorrectly uses 'Classification' instead of 'Nomenclature'. Note: The exact NCERT wording uses 'International Code of Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN)', not 'International Code for Botanical Nomenclature', but Option C is the best available choice among the given options.
questions.ncert_line: For plants, scientific names are based on agreed principles and criteria, which are provided in International Code for Botanical Nomenclature (ICBN).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which protein in plasma is mainly responsible for osmotic balance?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that albumins help in osmotic balance. Fibrinogen is wrong because it is need for clotting of blood. Globulin is wrong because it is primarily involved in defense mechanisms. Haemoglobin is wrong because it is found in RBCs, not in plasma, and it carries respiratory gases.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: the albumins help in osmotic balance.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Where are platelets (thrombocytes) produced in the body?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states platelets are cell fragments produced from megakaryocytes in the bone marrow. Spleen is wrong because it is the site where old RBCs are destroyed, not where platelets are made. Liver is wrong as it is not mentioned as the site of platelet production. Lymph nodes are wrong because they are associated with lymphocytes, not platelet production.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Platelets also called thrombocytes, are cell fragments produced from megakaryocytes (special cells in the bone marrow).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A pregnant woman is Rh negative and her foetus is Rh positive. During the delivery of her first child, some Rh positive blood from the foetus enters the mother's blood. What does the mother's body do in response?

options.A: The mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen
options.B: The mother starts producing Rh antigens on her RBCs
options.C: The mother's platelets destroy the foetal RBCs immediately
options.D: The mother develops anti-A and anti-B antibodies in her plasma

questions.explanation: When an Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive blood during delivery, her immune system recognizes the Rh antigen as foreign and starts producing antibodies against it. Option B is wrong because the mother cannot produce Rh antigens — she is Rh negative. Option C is wrong because platelets are involved in blood clotting, not in immune response to Rh antigens. Option D is wrong because anti-A and anti-B antibodies are related to ABO blood grouping, not Rh grouping.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: In such cases, the mother starts preparing antibodies against Rh antigen in her blood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A person with blood group AB needs a blood transfusion. Which blood groups can safely donate blood to this person?
questions.explanation: Blood group AB individuals have both A and B antigens on their RBCs and no antibodies in their plasma. Because they have no antibodies, they cannot reject any blood group — they accept AB, A, B, and O. This is why they are called universal recipients. Option A is wrong because A and B donors are also compatible. Option B is wrong because only saying O ignores the other compatible groups. Option D is wrong because it excludes AB and O donors, both of whom are also compatible.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Body Fluids and Circulation
questions.ncert_line: Persons with 'AB' group can accept blood from persons with AB as well as the other groups of blood. Therefore, such persons are called 'universal recipients'.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Net Primary Productivity (NPP) of an ecosystem is obtained by:

options.A: Subtracting respiration losses from Gross Primary Productivity
options.B: Adding respiration losses to Gross Primary Productivity
options.C: Subtracting Secondary Productivity from Gross Primary Productivity
options.D: Adding Secondary Productivity to Gross Primary Productivity

questions.explanation: NPP is calculated as GPP minus respiration losses — this is directly stated in NCERT as GPP – R = NPP. Option B is wrong because adding respiration losses would give a value higher than GPP, which is incorrect. Option C is wrong because secondary productivity is produced by consumers and is not subtracted from GPP to get NPP. Option D is wrong because adding secondary productivity has no role in calculating NPP.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Gross primary productivity minus respiration losses (R), is the net primary productivity (NPP). GPP – R = NPP

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Net Primary Productivity (NPP) represents the biomass available for consumption by which of the following groups?

options.C: Only decomposers such as fungi and bacteria

questions.explanation: The answer A is correct, but the chapter attribution is wrong. This topic belongs to NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 14: Ecosystem, not 'Biotechnology and its Applications' (Chapter 12). NPP is defined as Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) minus Respiration losses (R), and it represents the biomass available for consumption by heterotrophs, including herbivores and decomposers. The NCERT line cited is accurate: 'Net primary productivity is the available biomass for the consumption to heterotrophs (herbivores and decomposers).' Options B, C, and D are correctly eliminated in the explanation.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Net primary productivity is the available biomass for the consumption to heterotrophs (herbivores and decomposers).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which process during decomposition leads to the accumulation of a dark coloured amorphous substance called humus?
questions.explanation: Humification is the process that produces humus, a dark coloured amorphous substance resistant to microbial action — directly stated in NCERT. Mineralisation is wrong because it is the process by which humus is further degraded to release inorganic nutrients. Fragmentation is wrong because it refers to detritivores breaking detritus into smaller particles. Leaching is wrong because it refers to water-soluble nutrients moving down into the soil horizon. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 14 (Ecosystem) in NCERT Biology Class 12, not Biotechnology and its Applications.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Humification leads to accumulation of a dark coloured amorphous substance called humus that is highly resistant to microbial action and undergoes decomposition at an extremely slow rate.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which climatic condition favours a slower rate of decomposition?

options.D: Presence of water-soluble sugars in detritus

questions.explanation: Low temperature and anaerobiosis (absence of oxygen) inhibit decomposition — this is directly stated in NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 (Ecosystem). Warm temperature and high moisture favour decomposition, not slow it. High nitrogen content in detritus and presence of water-soluble sugars both facilitate faster decomposition. The NCERT line is accurate, though it belongs to the Ecosystem chapter, not Biotechnology and its Applications.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology and its Applications
questions.ncert_line: Low temperature and anaerobiosis inhibit decomposition resulting in build up of organic materials.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Green plants are called autotrophs because they:

options.A: Depend on other organisms for their food
options.B: Synthesise the food they need through photosynthesis
options.C: Obtain energy directly from the soil
options.D: Use oxygen to produce organic compounds

questions.explanation: Green plants are called autotrophs because they synthesise their own food through photosynthesis — this is directly stated in NCERT. Option A is wrong because depending on other organisms is the definition of heterotrophs, not autotrophs. Option C is wrong because plants do not obtain energy from the soil. Option D is wrong because photosynthesis uses light energy, not oxygen, to produce organic compounds.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: The green plants make or rather synthesise the food they need through photosynthesis and are therefore called autotrophs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosynthesis is important for life on Earth because it is the primary source of all food AND it is responsible for the release of which gas into the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that photosynthesis is responsible for the release of oxygen into the atmosphere. During the light reactions of photosynthesis, water molecules are split (photolysis of water), releasing oxygen as a byproduct. Option A is wrong because carbon dioxide is a raw material consumed during photosynthesis, not released by it. Option B is wrong because nitrogen release is not associated with photosynthesis. Option D is wrong because hydrogen is not released into the atmosphere during photosynthesis; instead, hydrogen ions are used in the reduction of NADP+ to NADPH.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: It is also responsible for the release of oxygen into the atmosphere by green plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Melvin Calvin used radioactively labelled carbon dioxide (C14) to study photosynthesis. What did he win for mapping the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Calvin earned the Nobel Prize in 1961 for mapping the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis. Option A is wrong because Bharat Ratna is an Indian civilian honour, not a science award given to Calvin. Option C is wrong because the Pulitzer Prize is a journalism and arts award, not a science prize. Option D is wrong because the year 1945 is incorrect — the prize was awarded in 1961.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: The mapping of the pathway of carbon assimilation in photosynthesis earned him Nobel Prize in 1961.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: T.W. Engelmann used aerobic bacteria in his experiment with Cladophora to detect which of the following?
questions.explanation: Engelmann placed aerobic bacteria in a suspension with Cladophora. These bacteria moved toward areas where oxygen was released, thus detecting sites of O2 evolution. Option B is wrong because the bacteria detected oxygen, not CO2 absorption. Option C is wrong because glucose storage was studied by Julius von Sachs, not Engelmann. Option D is wrong because chlorophyll synthesis was not the subject of this experiment.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: The bacteria were used to detect the sites of O2 evolution. He observed that the bacteria accumulated mainly in the region of blue and red light of the split spectrum.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cornelius van Niel concluded that the oxygen released during photosynthesis in green plants comes from which source?
questions.explanation: Van Niel studied purple and green sulphur bacteria where H2S acted as the hydrogen donor, producing sulphur instead of oxygen. He concluded that in green plants, water (H2O) is the hydrogen donor and the source of released O2. Option B is wrong because van Niel specifically stated O2 does not come from CO2. Option C is wrong because H2S is the hydrogen donor in sulphur bacteria, not in green plants. Option D is wrong because carbohydrates are a product of photosynthesis, not a source of released oxygen.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants
questions.ncert_line: Hence, he inferred that the O2 evolved by the green plant comes from H2O, not from carbon dioxide. This was later proved by using radioisotopic techniques.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What type of cell wall is present in the kingdom Fungi?
questions.explanation: Fungi have a cell wall made of chitin — this is clearly stated in the NCERT text and also confirmed in Table 2.1. Cellulose cell wall belongs to kingdom Plantae, not Fungi. Polysaccharide and amino acid wall is the description of the cell wall in kingdom Monera (peptidoglycan/mucopeptide). Animalia is the kingdom that has no cell wall, not Fungi.
questions.ncert_line: The classification did not differentiate between the heterotrophic group – fungi, and the autotrophic green plants, though they also showed a characteristic difference in their walls composition – the fungi had chitin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which kingdom in the Five Kingdom Classification contains prokaryotic organisms?
questions.explanation: According to Table 2.1 in NCERT Biology Class 11 (Chapter 2: Biological Classification), Monera is the only kingdom with prokaryotic cell type. Protista contains eukaryotic organisms such as unicellular eukaryotes. Fungi are also eukaryotic and are heterotrophic. Plantae are eukaryotic and autotrophic. All other four kingdoms (Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia) are eukaryotic, making Monera the unique prokaryotic kingdom in the Five Kingdom Classification proposed by R.H. Whittaker.
questions.ncert_line: Cell type — Monera: Prokaryotic; Protista: Eukaryotic; Fungi: Eukaryotic; Plantae: Eukaryotic; Animalia: Eukaryotic.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which specialised cells in some cyanobacteria are responsible for fixing atmospheric nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Heterocysts are specialised cells found in certain cyanobacteria like Nostoc and Anabaena that fix atmospheric nitrogen — directly stated in NCERT. Spores are reproductive structures formed under unfavourable conditions, not involved in nitrogen fixation. Flagella are structures for motility, not nitrogen fixation. Guard cells are found in plants and regulate stomatal opening — they are not part of cyanobacteria.
questions.ncert_line: Some of these organisms can fix atmospheric nitrogen in specialised cells called heterocysts, e.g., Nostoc and Anabaena.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Methanogens present in the gut of ruminant animals are responsible for the production of which gas from the dung of these animals?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that methanogens produce methane (biogas) from the dung of ruminants like cows and buffaloes. Carbon dioxide is a common gas in respiration but is not the product associated with methanogens in this context. Hydrogen sulphide is produced by certain sulphur bacteria, not methanogens. Ammonia is oxidised by chemosynthetic bacteria but is not the product of methanogens.
questions.ncert_line: Methanogens are present in the gut of several ruminant animals such as cows and buffaloes and they are responsible for the production of methane (biogas) from the dung of these animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which type of linkage connects a nitrogenous base to the 1'C of a pentose sugar to form a nucleoside?
questions.explanation: The N-glycosidic linkage connects the nitrogenous base to the 1'C of the pentose sugar to form a nucleoside. This is correctly stated in NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 6 (Molecular Basis of Inheritance), not Chapter 5 (Principles of Inheritance and Variation). The NCERT line is accurate: 'A nitrogenous base is linked to the 1'C of pentose sugar through a N-glycosidic linkage to form a nucleoside.' Note the minor error in the cited NCERT line — it says 'OH of 1'C' but the actual linkage replaces the OH; the bond is directly to the 1'C. The explanations for why the other options are wrong are accurate: phosphodiester linkage connects nucleotides (3'-5'), phosphoester linkage connects phosphate to 5'C, and hydrogen bonds hold complementary strands together.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: A nitrogenous base is linked to the OH of 1' C pentose sugar through a N-glycosidic linkage to form a nucleoside.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What forms the backbone of a polynucleotide chain?
questions.explanation: NCERT explicitly states that the backbone is made of sugar and phosphates. Option A is wrong because nitrogenous bases project outward from the backbone and do not form it. Option B is wrong because nitrogenous bases point away from the backbone rather than forming it. Option D is wrong because hydrogen bonds are not structural components of the polynucleotide backbone.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: The backbone of a polynucleotide chain is formed due to sugar and phosphates. The nitrogenous bases linked to sugar moiety project from the backbone.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the DNA double helix, how many hydrogen bonds connect Guanine with Cytosine?
questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that Guanine pairs with Cytosine through three hydrogen bonds. One hydrogen bond is incorrect and does not apply to any standard base pair in DNA. Two hydrogen bonds describe the Adenine-Thymine pair, not the G-C pair. Four hydrogen bonds is not mentioned anywhere in NCERT for any base pair. Note: This topic is covered in the chapter 'Molecular Basis of Inheritance', not 'Principles of Inheritance and Variation'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation
questions.ncert_line: Guanine is bonded with Cytosine with three H-bonds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Who is known as the Father of Ecology in India?
questions.explanation: Ramdeo Misra is indeed revered as the Father of Ecology in India. He established the first school of ecology in India at Banaras Hindu University (BHU) and made significant contributions to plant ecology. W. H. Pearsall was his PhD supervisor at Leeds University. The NCERT line cited is accurate. However, the chapter attribution is incorrect — this fact appears in the NCERT Biology textbook in the chapter related to organisms and environment/ecology, not in 'Biotechnology: Principles and Processes.'
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ramdeo Misra is revered as the Father of Ecology in India.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Ramdeo Misra obtained his Ph.D in Ecology from which university?
questions.explanation: Ramdeo Misra obtained his Ph.D in Ecology in 1937 under Prof. W. H. Pearsall, FRS, from Leeds University in the UK. He is regarded as the 'Father of Ecology in India.' After returning to India, he established the Department of Botany at Banaras Hindu University, where he built a strong school of plant ecology. Note: This topic is covered in the Ecology chapters of NCERT Biology (Class 12), not in the Biotechnology chapter.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: Ramdeo Misra obtained Ph.D in Ecology (1937) under Prof. W. H. Pearsall, FRS, from Leeds University in UK.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a population, tiger census in national parks is often estimated based on which of the following?

options.A: Direct counting of tigers in the field
options.C: Per cent cover and biomass measurement
options.D: Number of tigers caught per trap

questions.explanation: NCERT clearly states that tiger census is based on pug marks and fecal pellets, which are indirect methods of estimating population size. Option A is wrong because tigers are not directly counted — indirect estimation is used. Option C is wrong because per cent cover and biomass are used for plants like the banyan tree, not tigers. Option D is wrong because number caught per trap is used for fish populations in lakes, not tigers.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: The tiger census in our national parks and tiger reserves is often based on pug marks and fecal pellets.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The shape of an age pyramid for a human population reflects which characteristic of that population?

options.A: The sex ratio of males and females
options.B: The birth rate and death rate combined
options.C: The growth status — whether growing, stable, or decling
options.D: The total population density of the habitat

questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that the shape of the age pyramid reflects the growth status of the population — growing, stable, or decling. Option A is wrong because sex ratio is a separate population attribute and not what the pyramid shape specifically reflects. Option B is wrong because birth and death rates are population attributes but the pyramid shape specifically indicates growth status. Option D is wrong because population density refers to the size of the population, not what the shape of the age pyramid represents. Note: This topic belongs to Chapter 13 (Organisms and Populations) in NCERT Biology Class 12, not Biotechnology.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes
questions.ncert_line: The shape of the pyramids reflects the growth status of the population - (a) whether it is growing, (b) stable or (c) decling.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means total well-being in which of the following aspects?
questions.explanation: The WHO definition of reproductive health specifically covers physical, emotional, behavioural and social aspects — stated directly in NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 (Reproductive Health). Option B is wrong because 'genetic' and 'hormonal' are not part of the WHO definition. Option C is wrong because 'anatomical' and 'cultural' are not mentioned. Option D is wrong because 'nutritional' and 'environmental' are not part of the WHO definition given in the text.
questions.ncert_line: According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, i.e., physical, emotional, behavioural and social.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India's first national-level action plans for reproductive health, called 'family planning', were initiated in which year?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that family planning programmes were initiated in 1951. Option B (1961) is wrong as it is a decade later. Option C (1947) is wrong — that is India's independence year, not when these programmes started. Option D (1971) is also incorrect and does not match the text.
questions.ncert_line: These programmes called 'family planning' were initiated in 1951 and were periodically assed over the past decades.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The lactational amenorrhea method of contraception is based on which of the following facts?

options.A: Intense lactation following parturition prevents ovulation and the menstrual cycle
options.B: Breastfeeding increases the level of progesterone which kills the sperm
options.C: Lactation causes the uterine ling to shed, preventing implantation
options.D: Breastfeeding stimulates ovulation, making fertilisation impossible

questions.explanation: Option A is correct because NCERT clearly states that intense lactation after parturition prevents ovulation and the menstrual cycle, making conception unlikely. Option B is wrong because NCERT does not mention progesterone or sperm killing in this context. Option C is wrong because shedding of uterine ling is unrelated to the mechanism described in NCERT. Option D is completely opposite — NCERT says ovulation does NOT occur during intense lactation, not that it is stimulated.
questions.ncert_line: Lactational amenorrhea (absence of menstruation) method is based on the fact that ovulation and therefore the cycle do not occur during the period of intense lactation following parturition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which days of the menstrual cycle is the fertile period, when chances of fertilisation are very high?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because NCERT explicitly states that ovulation is expected from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle, making it the fertile period with the highest chances of fertilisation. This is mentioned in the context of periodic abstinence as a contraceptive method. Option B (day 1 to 7) represents the menstrual and early follicular phase, not the ovulatory period. Option C (day 18 to 28) is the post-ovulatory/luteal phase where chances of fertilisation are low. Option D (day 5 to 10) is before the expected ovulation window mentioned in NCERT.
questions.ncert_line: Periodic abstinence is one such method in which the couples avoid or abstain from coitus from day 10 to 17 of the menstrual cycle when ovulation could be expected. As chances of fertilisation are very high during this period, it is called the fertile period.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which organ is ammonia converted into urea in ureotelic animals?
questions.explanation: The liver converts ammonia into urea — this is clearly stated in NCERT. Option A (kidney) is wrong because the kidney only filters and excretes urea; it does not convert ammonia. Option B (spleen) is a real organ but has no role in urea formation. Option C (pancreas) is also a real organ involved in digestion and hormone secretion, not urea synthesis.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Ammonia produced by metabolism is converted into urea in the liver of these animals and released into the blood which is filtered and excreted out by the kidneys.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the name of the excretory structure found in most insects including cockroaches?
questions.explanation: Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most insects including cockroaches — directly stated in NCERT. Option A (nephridia) is wrong because nephridia are found in earthworms and other annelids, not insects. Option B (protonephridia) is wrong because these are found in flatworms, rotifers and Amphioxus. Option C (antennal glands) is wrong because these are found in crustaceans like prawns, not insects.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: Malpighian tubules are the excretory structures of most of the insects including cockroaches.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which cells of the Bowman's capsule are arranged to leave minute spaces called filtration slits or slit pores?
questions.explanation: NCERT directly states that podocytes are the epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule that form filtration slits. Option B is wrong because endothelial cells line the glomerular blood vessels, not Bowman's capsule epithelium. Option C is wrong because simple cuboidal brush border epithelium lines the PCT, not Bowman's capsule. Option D is wrong because mesangial cells are associated with glomerular support, not filtration slits, and are not mentioned in this NCERT passage.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class11 — Excretory Products and Elimination
questions.ncert_line: The epithelial cells of Bowman's capsule called podocytes are arranged in an intricate manner so as to leave some minute spaces called filtration slits or slit pores.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell or explant is called:
questions.explanation: Totipotency is the ability of a single cell or explant to develop into a complete plant — directly stated in NCERT. Option B (somatic hybridisation) refers to the fusion of protoplasts from two different plants, not the regeneration ability. Option C (micro-propagation) is the method of producing thousands of plants using tissue culture, not the underlying capacity itself. Option D (organogenesis) refers to the process of differentiation of shoots and roots from a callus, not the capacity of any cell to regenerate a whole plant. The NCERT line cited is accurate, but this concept belongs to Chapter 'Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production' (Class 12, Chapter 9), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare' (Chapter 10).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: This capacity to generate a whole plant from any cell/explant is called totipotency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Bt toxin protein exists as inactive protoxin inside the bacterium. What converts it into an active toxin inside an insect's body?

options.A: The acidic pH of the insect's stomach
options.B: The alkaline pH of the insect's gut
options.C: Digestive enzymes secreted by the insect's midgut
options.D: The high temperature inside the insect's body

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology (Class 12, Chapter 12 - Biotechnology and its Applications), the Bt toxin protein exists as inactive protoxins in the bacterial crystal. Once an insect ingests the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form due to the alkaline pH of the insect's gut, which solubilises the crystals. The active toxin then binds to the epithelial cells of the midgut, creating pores that cause cell swelling and lysis, ultimately killing the insect. Option A is wrong because insects (especially lepidopteran larvae) have an alkaline gut, not acidic. Option C is wrong because NCERT specifically attributes the activation to alkaline pH, not digestive enzymes per se. Option D is wrong because temperature is never mentioned as a factor in this conversion. Note: This topic is covered in Chapter 12 (Biotechnology and its Applications), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare'.
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: once an insect ingest the inactive toxin, it is converted into an active form of toxin due to the alkaline pH of the gut which solubilise the crystals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: To protect tobacco plants from the nematode Meloidogyne incognita, nematode-specific genes were introduced into the host plant using Agrobacterium vectors. What was the result of introducing both sense and anti-sense RNA in the host cells?

options.A: The host plant produced Bt toxin to kill the nematode
options.B: A double-stranded RNA was formed that initiated RNAi and silenced the nematode's specific mRNA
options.C: The host plant's immune system directly destroyed the nematode
options.D: The nematode's DNA was permanently altered, making it non-parasitic

questions.explanation: According to NCERT Biology Class 12, Chapter 12 (Biotechnology and its Applications), nematode-specific genes were introduced into the host plant (tobacco) using Agrobacterium vectors. Both sense and anti-sense RNA sequences of a specific gene were introduced. These complementary RNAs formed a double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) that initiated RNA interference (RNAi), silencing the specific mRNA of the nematode Meloidogyne incognita. This prevented the nematode from surving in the host plant. Option A is wrong because Bt toxin is used against insects (like Lepidoptera, Coleoptera), not nematodes. Option C is wrong because plants do not have an immune system analogous to animals; the protection is mediated through RNAi. Option D is wrong because RNAi silences the nematode's mRNA (post-transcriptional gene silencing), not permanently altering the nematode's DNA. Note: The correct NCERT chapter is 'Biotechnology and its Applications' (Chapter 12), not 'Microbes in Human Welfare' (Chapter 10).
questions.ncert_ref: NCERT Class12 — Microbes in Human Welfare
questions.ncert_line: These two RNA's being complementary to each other formed a double stranded (dsRNA) that initiated RNAi and thus, silenced the specific mRNA of the nematode.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which sociobiologist popularised the term 'Biodiversity' to describe the combined diversity at all levels of biological organisation?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that Edward Wilson, a sociobiologist, popularised the term 'Biodiversity'. Robert May is mentioned in the text but for estimating global species diversity at about 7 million, not for coing the term. Ernst Mayr and Charles Darwin are well-known biologists but are not mentioned in this context in the text.
questions.ncert_line: Biodiversity is the term popularised by the sociobiologist Edward Wilson to describe the combined diversity at all the levels of biological organisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Among all animals recorded on Earth, which group makes up more than 70 per cent of the total animal species?
questions.explanation: NCERT Biology clearly states that insects make up more than 70 per cent of all recorded animal species, making them the most species-rich animal group on Earth. This is mentioned in the context of biodiversity discussions in NCERT Class 12 Biology (Chapter: Biodiversity and Conservation), where it highlights that among all known animal species, insects dominate in terms of numbers. Reptiles, fishes, and mammals, while significant groups, represent far fewer species in comparison to insects.
questions.ncert_line: Among animals, insects are the most species-rich taxonomic group, making up more than 70 per cent of the total.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the scientifically sound estimate of global species diversity made by Robert May?
questions.explanation: NCERT states that Robert May's conservative and scientifically sound estimate places global species diversity at about 7 million. Option A (1.5 million) refers to the number of species already described and named according to IUCN (2004), not the total estimate. Options C (20 million) and D (50 million) are mentioned in the text as extreme estimates, not the scientifically preferred figure.
questions.ncert_line: A more conservative and scientifically sound estimate made by Robert May places the global species diversity at about 7 million.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India covers only 2.4 per cent of the world's land area. What percentage of global species diversity does India contribute?
questions.explanation: The NCERT text clearly states that India's share of global species diversity is 8.1 per cent, which is why it is one of the 12 mega diversity countries. Option B (2.4%) is India's share of world's land area, not species diversity. Option C (4.8%) is a made-up figure not mentioned anywhere in the text. Option D (12%) refers to the number of mega diversity countries, not India's species share.
questions.ncert_line: Although India has only 2.4 per cent of the world's land area, its share of the global species diversity is an impressive 8.1 per cent.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why tropical regions have greater species diversity compared to temperate regions?

options.A: Tropical latitudes have remained relatively undisturbed for millions of years, allowing a long evolutionary time for species diversification.
options.B: Tropical regions experience frequent glaciations, which speeds up speciation.
options.C: Tropical environments are highly seasonal, which promotes more niche specialisation.
options.D: Tropical regions receive less solar energy, reducing competion among species.

questions.explanation: The NCERT text states that tropical latitudes remained undisturbed for millions of years, providing a long evolutionary time for diversification — this is one of the key hypothes explaing greater species diversity in tropical regions. Option B is wrong because it is the temperate regions, not tropical ones, that experienced frequent glaciations. Option C is wrong because the NCERT text states that tropical environments are LESS seasonal (more constant/stable), not highly seasonal.
