questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature dictates that every living organism has one scientifically accepted name, which is composed of two parts: the generic name (genus) and the specific epithet (species).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A: Members of a species are reproductively isolated from the members of other species. R: Species is the basic taxonomic category.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
options.C: A is true but R is false.
options.D: Both A and R are false.

questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, but reproductive isolation is a biological property of a species, while being a basic taxonomic category is a classification concept; one does not explain the other.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A: Botanical gardens are ex-situ conservation strategy of plants. R: National Botanical Garden is situated at Howrah.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
options.C: A is true but R is false.
options.D: Both A and R are false.

questions.explanation: A is correct (botanical gardens are ex-situ conservation). R is false because the National Botanical Garden is situated at Lucknow (NBRI), not Howrah.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Statement-1: Linnaeus classified plants into trees, shrubs and herbs, on the basis of morphological characters. Statement- 2: Aristotle divided animals into group - Anaima and Enaima.

options.C: Both statement-1 and statement-2 are correct
options.D: Both statement-1 and statement-2 are incorrect

questions.explanation: Aristotle classified plants, not Linnaeus. Linnaeus is known for binomial nomenclature. Therefore, statement 1 is wrong and statement 2 is correct.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Primitive bacteria living in salty areas are called as
questions.explanation: Halophiles are archaebacteria that thrive in high saline (salty) conditions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Diatomaceous earth is formed due to which substance?
questions.explanation: The cell walls of diatoms are embedded with silica (silicon dioxide). These indestructible deposits accumulate over time to form diatomaceous earth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Viruses that infect the bacteria are termed as
questions.explanation: Bacteriophages are viruses that specifically infect bacteria. Cyanophages infect cyanobacteria.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following are found in extreme saline conditions?
questions.explanation: Archaebacteria (specifically halophiles) are adapted to survive in extreme saline environments due to unique features like branched chain lipids in their cell membrane.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A root grows in length, which region of the root is responsible for this growth?
questions.explanation: Actual increase in length occurs in the region of elongation due to cell enlargement. The meristematic region produces new cells, but they elongate in the region of elongation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Rhizophora, roots are modified to form
questions.explanation: Rhizophora is a mangrove growing in saline, marshy areas. Its roots are modified into negatively geotropic breathing roots called pneumatophores to obtain oxygen.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: protect Bougainvillea from grazing animals.
questions.explanation: The thorns in Bougainvillea are modified stem structures that provide protection from grazing animals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following plant has a superior ovary?
questions.explanation: China rose has a hypogynous flower with a superior ovary. Peach and Rose are perigynous (ovary is still superior but not as clearly), while Guava is epigynous with an inferior ovary.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Carpels are fused in the flowers of
questions.explanation: Fused carpels is a syncarpous condition. Tomato has a syncarpous gynoecium. Lotus and Rose have apocarpous (free) carpels.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The plants belonging to the family Solanaceae is represented by the floral formula
questions.explanation: The standard floral formula for Solanaceae is actinomorphic (⊕), bisexual (⚥), calyx with 5 sepals (K(5)), corolla with 5 petals (C(5)), androecium with 5 stamens (A5), and a bicarpellary syncarpous superior ovary (G(2)).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The morphological nature of the edible part of conut is
questions.explanation: The edible part of conut is the endosperm, which is both liquid (conut water) and cellular (the white kernel).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many plants among Indigofera, Sesbania, Salvia, Allium, Aloe, mustard, groundnut, radish, gram and turnip have stamens with different lengths in their flowers?
questions.explanation: Stamens of different lengths (didynamous or tetradynamous) are found in Brassicaceae (mustard, radish, turnip) and Lamiaceae (Salvia). This totals 4 plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Perigynous flowers are found in
questions.explanation: In perigynous flowers (like rose and peach), the ovary is superior but surrounded by a cup-shaped thalamus to which other floral parts are attached.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statement is incorrect regarding bryophytes?

options.A: They are dependent on water for sexual reproduction
options.B: The main plant body is diploid
options.C: They usually occur in damp, humid and shaded localities
options.D: They play an important role in plant succession on bare rocks

questions.explanation: The main plant body of bryophytes is the gametophyte, which is haploid (n), not diploid. The sporophyte is dependent on it.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In pteridophytes, spores germinate to give rise to
questions.explanation: In pteridophytes, spores germinate to form an independent, inconspicuous, heart-shaped gametophyte called prothallus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cones bearing microsporophylls are known as
questions.explanation: In gymnosperms, microsporophylls bearing microsporangia are compactly arranged to form male strobili (cones).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: An event unique to angiosperms is
questions.explanation: Double fertilization, involving syngamy and triple fusion resulting in a diploid zygote and triploid endosperm, is a defing characteristic unique to angiosperms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Haplo-diplontic condition is exhibited by
questions.explanation: Both bryophytes and pteridophytes show a haplo-diplontic life cycle with alternating independent haploid gametophyte and diploid sporophyte phases.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Brown algae are quite common in
questions.explanation: While brown algae are predominantly marine, they are most abundant and diverse in cooler, temperate sea waters rather than tropical waters.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Peat is obtained from
questions.explanation: Partially decomposed remains of Sphagnum moss accumulate over time to form peat, which is used as a fuel and soil conditioner.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the mismatch
questions.explanation: Pinus is a monoecious plant. Male and female cones are borne on the same plant. Cycas is dioecious.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Male gametophyte with least number of cells is present in
questions.explanation: In angiosperms like Lilium, the male gametophyte (pollen grain) is highly reduced, consisting of just 2-3 cells at the time of pollination, which is fewer than in gymnosperms (Pinus) or pteridophytes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following cell organelles is non-membrane bound and found in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are non-membrane bound structures composed of RNA and protein and are universally present in all types of cells (both prokaryotes and eukaryotes).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The subunits of ribosomes of a prokaryotic cell are
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S, composed of a large 50S subunit and a small 30S subunit.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The animal cell is different from a plant cell in having
questions.explanation: Centrosomes (containg centrioles) are found in animal cells and are involved in spindle formation, while they are generally absent in plant cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statement is incorrect w.r.t. Golgi apparatus?

options.B: It is composed of flattened sacs called cisternae
options.D: Golgi apparatus has two faces - cis and trans

questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is a membrane-bound organelle, composed of a stack of flattened membrane-bound sacs called cisternae.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The inner membrane of mitochondria forms a number of infoldings called
questions.explanation: The inner mitochondrial membrane folds inward to form finger-like projections called cristae, which increase the surface area for ATP synthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The chromosomes having centromere at terminal end are called
questions.explanation: Telocentric chromosomes have the centromere at the very end, resulting in a single arm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A cell organelle containg hydrolytic enzymes is
questions.explanation: Lysomes are membrane-bound vesicles that contain powerful hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes capable of breaking down a wide variety of biomolecules.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Balbiani rings are sites of
questions.explanation: Balbiani rings are large chromosome puffs found in polytene chromosomes of dipteran insects, representing sites of intense transcriptional activity for RNA and subsequent protein synthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A major site for synthesis of lipids is
questions.explanation: The Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum (SER) lacks ribosomes and is the primary site for the synthesis of lipids, steroids, and for detoxification processes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Nuclear membrane is absent in
questions.explanation: Nostoc is a cyanobacterium, a prokaryotic organism. Prokaryotes lack a true nucleus and a nuclear membrane.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In mitochondria, protons accumulate in the
questions.explanation: During electron transport, protons are pumped from the mitochondrial matrix across the inner membrane into the intermembrane space, creating an electrochemical gradient used for ATP synthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: An elaborate network of filamentous proteinaceous structures present in the cytoplasm which helps in the maintenance of cell shape is called
questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton is a network of protein filaments (microtubules, microfilaments, intermediate filaments) that provides structural support, facilitates cell movement, and aids in intracellular transport.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: DNA replication occurs in
questions.explanation: The S (Synthesis) phase of the cell cycle is specifically dedicated to the replication of DNA, resulting in the duplication of the cell's genetic material.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The phase between the two successive M phase is called as
questions.explanation: Interphase is the longest phase of the cell cycle, occurring between successive mitotic (M) phases, during which the cell grows and prepares for division.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Non-dividing cells enter the
questions.explanation: The G₀ phase is a quiescent stage where cells exit the active cell cycle and remain metabolically active but do not proliferate unless stimulated to re-enter.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The number of chromosomes in G₁ phase is 36, the number of chromosomes in S phase is
questions.explanation: During the S phase, DNA content duplicates (from 2C to 4C), but the number of chromosomes remains the same. Each chromosome now consists of two sister chromatids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The centromere splits during
questions.explanation: The splitting of the centromere, which allows sister chromatids to separate and move to opposite poles, occurs during the anaphase stage of mitosis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If karyokinesis is not followed by cytokinesis, then gives rise to
questions.explanation: Karyokinesis is the division of the nucleus. If the nucleus divides repeatedly without subsequent cytoplasmic division (cytokinesis), it results in a cell containg multiple nuclei, known as a multinucleate or syncytial condition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What will be the total number of mitotic divisions in the formation of 64 daughter cells?
questions.explanation: Starting from a single cell, one mitotic division yields 2 cells. To get 64 cells, the number of divisions is n-1 (where n is the final number). Since 2⁶ = 64, it takes 6 generations, but the number of divisions is 2⁶ - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 divisions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Crossing over occurs during
questions.explanation: Crossing over, the exchange of genetic material between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes, occurs during the pachytene stage of prophase I of meiosis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The homologous chromosomes move towards the opposite poles during
questions.explanation: During anaphase I of meiosis, homologous chromosomes (each consisting of two sister chromatids) separate and move to opposite poles. This is the reductional division that halves the chromosome number.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If there are 30 chromosomes in G₁ phase then what will be number of bivalents in zygotene stage?
questions.explanation: If G₁ has 30 chromosomes, the diploid number (2n) is 30. At zygotene, homologous chromosomes pair up to form bivalents. The number of bivalents is equal to the haploid number (n), which is 15.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which phase of interphase is the most important point in regulation of the cell cycle, during which it must decide whether the cell will start a new cycle or will enter in G₀ phase?
questions.explanation: The G₁ checkpoint (also called the restriction point) is the primary decision point. If conditions are unfavorable, the cell exits the cycle at this point and enters the quiescent G₀ phase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Higher plants differ from animals in having
questions.explanation: Animal cells have astral rays radiating from centrosomes (centrioles) during mitosis, forming an astral spindle. Higher plants lack centrioles and thus have an anastral spindle.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Spireme stage of chromosomes is associated with
questions.explanation: The spireme stage refers to early prophase when chromosomes first become visible as a tangled, thread-like mass within the nucleus.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the meiotic cell division, 56 daughter cells are produced by two successive divisions in which

options.A: First division is equational, second is reductional
options.B: First division is reductional, and second is equational

questions.explanation: Meiosis consists of two successive divisions: Meiosis I is the reductional division (chromosome number halved), and Meiosis II is equational (similar to mitosis).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The recombination nodules which mediate for chromosome recombination appear at intervals on the synaptonemal complex during
questions.explanation: Recombination nodules are protein complexes that facilitate crossing over. They are present on the synaptonemal complex during the pachytene stage, when crossing over actually occurs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Anaphase promoting complex (APC) is a protein degradation machinery necessary for proper mitosis of animal cells. If APC is defective in a human cell, which of the following is expected to occur?

options.D: Recombination of chromosome arms will occur

questions.explanation: APC is responsible for triggering anaphase by targeting securin for degradation, which allows separase to cleave cohesin. Without APC, sister chromatids remain together, and chromosomes fail to segregate.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Arrange the following events of meiosis in correct sequence: (a) Crossing over (b) Synapsis (c) Terminalisation of chiasmata (d) Disappearance of nucleolus
questions.explanation: The correct sequence in prophase I is: Zygotene (synapsis) → Pachytene (crossing over) → Diakinesis (terminalisation of chiasmata + disappearance of nucleolus).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Tissues are

options.A: Groups of cells which are similar in origin and function
options.B: Groups of organs which are similar in origin and function
options.C: Cells which are similar in function but not in origin
options.D: Groups of cells which are not similar in origin and function

questions.explanation: A tissue is defined as a group of cells that are structurally and functionally similar, performing a common function, and sharing a common origin.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The woody axis of flowering plants is produced by
questions.explanation: Secondary growth, which increases the girth (thickness) of stems and roots, is carried out by lateral secondary meristems like vascular cambium and cork cambium, producing wood.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The elongated, thick-walled and tapering cells are
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma cells are characterized by their thick, lignified walls, elongated shape, and tapering ends. They provide mechanical support and are often dead at maturity.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Sclereids are commonly found in

options.A: Young stems and petioles of leaves
options.B: Fruit walls of nuts, pulp of pear

questions.explanation: Sclereids are a type of sclerenchyma cell. They are commonly found in hard parts like the seed coat (testa), fruit walls of nuts, and give the gritty texture to the pulp of pears and guavas.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is true for endarch type of primary xylem?

options.A: Protoxylem lies towards the periphery of the organ
options.B: Metaxylem lies towards the periphery of the organ
options.C: Metaxylem lies towards the pith of the organ
options.D: Protophloem lies towards the pith of the organ

questions.explanation: In endarch xylem (typical of stems), protoxylem is formed first and lies towards the center (pith), while metaxylem is formed later and lies towards the periphery.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Root hairs are the
questions.explanation: Root hairs are extensions of individual epidermal cells in the region of maturation. They are unicellular structures that greatly increase the surface area for water and mineral absorption.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The casparian strips are present on the plant cells of root which are
questions.explanation: Casparian strips are suberin and lignin deposits found on the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells, which are typically barrel-shaped in the root.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vascular bundles surrounded by a sclerenchymatous bundle sheath is a feature of
questions.explanation: In monocot stems (like maize or sugarcane), each vascular bundle is surrounded by a sheath of sclerenchymatous fibers called the bundle sheath for protection and support.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During water stress, the bulliform cells (a) Become turgid (b) Become flaccid (c) Make the leaves curl inwards (d) Make the leaf surface exposed The correct options are
questions.explanation: Bulliform cells (motor cells) are large, empty cells on the upper epidermis of monocot leaves. During water stress, they become flaccid, causing the leaf to fold inward to reduce water loss.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The cambial ring is generally

options.A: More active on the inner side than on the outer
options.B: More active on the outer side than on the inner

questions.explanation: The vascular cambium is more active on its inner side, producing significantly more secondary xylem (wood) cells compared to the secondary phloem cells produced on its outer side.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: An annual ring is formed by

options.A: Two consecutive rings of spring wood
options.B: Two alternate rings of spring wood and autumn wood
options.C: Two consecutive rings of autumn wood
options.D: Two alternate rings of sapwood and heartwood

questions.explanation: In temperate regions, each growing season produces one ring: lighter, wider spring wood (early wood) followed by darker, denser autumn wood (late wood). Together, they form one annual ring.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The feature which is common to both heartwood and sapwood is

options.A: Both are the regions of secondary xylem
options.B: Both are involved in the conduction of water
options.C: Both comprise dead elements with accumulation of aromatic compounds
options.D: Both are located in the central layers of the stem

questions.explanation: Both heartwood and sapwood are parts of the secondary xylem. Sapwood is the outer, functional part, while heartwood is the inner, non-functional, often darker part.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Identify the wrong statement in context of heartwood.

options.A: Organic compounds are deposited in it
options.C: It conducts water and minerals efficiently
options.D: It comprises dead elements with highly lignified walls

questions.explanation: Heartwood is the older, central, non-functional part of the secondary xylem. It is clogged with organic deposits like tannins, resins, and gums (tyloses), making it incapable of conducting water.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Diffusion is a ___process and is not dependent on __.
questions.explanation: Diffusion is a slow, passive physical process. It does not require energy or a living system; it can occur in non-living systems as well.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A cell when immersed in a solution, increases in volume, so the external solution is
questions.explanation: If a cell increases in volume, water has moved into the cell. This occurs when the external solution has a lower solute concentration (hypotonic) than the cell sap, causing endosmosis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Doors made up of wood, swell up in rainy season due to
questions.explanation: Wood contains hydrophilic colloids (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin) that have a high affinity for water. The absorption of water by these solid particles, causing them to swell, is called imbition.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: For a solution at atmospheric pressure, ψw is equivalent to
questions.explanation: The water potential equation is ψw = ψs + ψp. At atmospheric pressure, the pressure potential (ψp) is zero. Therefore, ψw = ψs.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Xylem helps in translocation of

options.D: More than one option is correct

questions.explanation: Xylem primarily transports water and mineral salts from roots to shoots. It also transports some organic compounds like amino acids, amides, and plant hormones (e.g., cytokins, abscisic acid).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The casparian strips of root endodermis is made up of
questions.explanation: Casparian strips are composed of suberin (a waterproof, fatty substance) and sometimes lignin, deposited on the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Translocation of photosynthates occur in the form of
questions.explanation: Sucrose is the primary form in which photosynthates (sugars) are translocated through the phloem. It is a non-reducing, stable, and transport-friendly disaccharide.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the pressure flow hypothesis A) Phloem loading produces a hypertonic condition in the sieve tubes. B) Water potential gradient facilitates the mass movement in the phloem. C) Phloem unloading is a passive process.
questions.explanation: Phloem loading creates high solute concentration (hypertonic) at the source, drawing in water and creating pressure. This pressure drives mass flow. However, phloem unloading at the sink is often an active process.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A few drops of sap were collected by cutting across a plant stem by a suitable method. The sap was tested chemically. Which one of the following test results indicates that it is phloem sap?
questions.explanation: Phloem sap is slightly alkaline (pH 7.8-8.0) due to its high content of organic solutes like sucrose, while xylem sap is more acidic (pH ~5.5-6.5).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A column of water within xylem vessels of tall trees does not break under its weight because of
questions.explanation: Water molecules have a high tensile strength due to strong cohesive forces (hydrogen bonds) between them. This allows the water column to resist breaking under tension.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one gives the most valid and recent explanation for stomatal movements?
questions.explanation: The most widely accepted mechanism is the potassium pump theory. Active influx of K⁺ ions into guard cells lowers their water potential, causing water inflow, increased turgor, and stomatal opening. Efflux of K⁺ leads to closing.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following criteria does not pertain to facilitated transport?
questions.explanation: Facilitated diffusion is a passive process that transports molecules along their concentration gradient (downhill). Uphill transport (against a gradient) requires active transport and energy.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Lenticels are involved in
questions.explanation: Lenticels are lens-shaped openings in the periderm (bark) of woody stems and roots. They permit the direct exchange of gases (O₂ and CO₂) between the internal tissues and the atmosphere.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Guttation is the result of
questions.explanation: Guttation is the exudation of water droplets from the margins and tips of leaves. It occurs when root pressure is high and transpiration is low (e.g., at night), forcing water out through specialized structures called hydathodes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Hydroponics refers to growing plants in
questions.explanation: Hydroponics is the soilless cultivation technique where plants are grown with their roots immersed in a nutrient solution containg all essential mineral elements.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the incorrect statement w.r.t. criteria of essentiality of minerals.

options.A: Element must be necessary for normal growth and reproduction
options.B: Element must be replaceable by another element
options.C: Disorders caused by the absence/deficiency of an element can be corrected only by adding that specific element
options.D: Element must be directly involved in plant metabolism

questions.explanation: One of the key criteria of essentiality is that an element must be indispensable and cannot be replaced by any other element.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not essential for all plants?
questions.explanation: Cobalt (Co) is a beneficial element required for nitrogen fixation in some plants (e.g., legumes), but it is not considered an essential element for all plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A macronutrient is
questions.explanation: Macronutrients are required in large quanties (>10 mmole/kg of dry matter). Magnesium (Mg) is a macronutrient, while manganese (Mn), zinc (Zn), and iron (Fe) are micronutrients.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select the odd one out from the following w.r.t. macronutrient.
questions.explanation: Nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulphur are macronutrients. Iron (Fe) is a micronutrient, required in trace amounts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme recombinase is required at which stage of meiosis?
questions.explanation: Recombinase enzymes catalyze the process of crossing over, which occurs during the pachytene stage of prophase I.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Plants absorb zinc as
questions.explanation: Plants absorb mineral elements in their ionic form. Zinc is absorbed as the divalent cation Zn²⁺.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Death of leaf tissues in plants is caused by deficiency of
questions.explanation: Necrosis (death of leaf tissues) is caused by the deficiency of Ca, Mg, Cu, and K. Ca and Mg are specifically mentioned in the option.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Reaction carried out by N₂ metabolising microbes include I. 2NH₃ + 3O₂ → 2NO₂⁻ + 2H⁺ + 2H₂O II. 2NO₂⁻ + O₂ → 2NO₃⁻ Which of the following statements about these equations is not true?

options.A: Step I is carried out by Nitrosomonas or Nitroccus
options.B: Step II is carried out by Nitrobacter
options.C: Both steps I and II can be called nitrification
options.D: Bacteria carrying out these steps are usually photoautotrophs

questions.explanation: Nitrifying bacteria (Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter) are chemoautotrophs, obtaing energy from oxidizing inorganic nitrogen compounds, not from light.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many ATP are required for the formation of one molecule of NH₃ by the nitrogenase enzyme?
questions.explanation: The nitrogenase reaction for fixing one N₂ molecule requires 16 ATP to produce 2 NH₃ molecules. Therefore, the formation of one molecule of NH₃ requires 8 ATP.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Enzyme nitrogenase is

options.D: Essential to convert NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻

questions.explanation: Nitrogenase is a Mo-Fe protein enzyme complex found only in certain prokaryotes (both free-living and symbiotic). It is highly sensitive to oxygen.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which of the following forms is iron absorbed by plants?
questions.explanation: Plants can absorb iron in both ferric (Fe³⁺) and ferrous (Fe²⁺) forms, though ferrous is more readily available and taken up.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During biological nitrogen fixation, inactivation of nitrogenase by oxygen poisoning is prevented by:
questions.explanation: Leghaemoglobin is an oxygen-scavenging pigment found in root nodules of legumes. It binds to free oxygen, creating an anaerobic micro-environment required for nitrogenase activity.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Deficiency symptoms of nitrogen and potassium are visible first in
questions.explanation: Nitrogen and potassium are mobile elements. Their deficiency symptoms appear first in older, senescent leaves as these elements are translocated to younger, growing parts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which equation represents the process of photosynthesis most adequately?
questions.explanation: The correct and most comprehensive equation is 6CO₂ + 12H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ + 6H₂O, showing that water is both a reactant and a product.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Moll's half leaf experiment was done to show ___ was required for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: In Moll's experiment, part of a leaf was enclosed in a test tube with KOH (which absorbs CO₂). That part tested negative for starch, proving CO₂ is essential.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Photosystem is composed of
questions.explanation: A photosystem consists of a light-harvesting complex (antenna molecules including accessory pigments) and a reaction center (special pair of chlorophyll a molecules).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The oxygen liberated during the photosynthesis comes from
questions.explanation: Using isotopic tracer techniques (¹⁸O), it was proven that the oxygen evolved during photosynthesis comes from the photolysis of water (H₂O), not from carbon dioxide (CO₂).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The CO₂ acceptor in C₃ plants is
questions.explanation: In C₃ plants, the primary CO₂ acceptor is Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (RuBP), a 5-carbon compound. Carboxylation of RuBP yields two molecules of 3-PGA.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme responsible for carboxylation reaction (CO₂ fixation) in C₃ plants is
questions.explanation: The enzyme RuBP carboxylase (part of RuBisCO) catalyzes the carboxylation of RuBP with CO₂, forming two molecules of 3-PGA in C₃ plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: 'Kranz' anatomy of leaves is found in
questions.explanation: Kranz anatomy (wreath-like arrangement) is a characteristic feature of C₄ plants. It involves distinct bundle sheath cells surrounded by radially arranged mesophyll cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Anoxygenic photosynthesis is characteristic of
questions.explanation: Anoxygenic photosynthesis, which does not produce oxygen, is characteristic of certain bacteria like purple sulfur bacteria (e.g., Chromatium) and purple non-sulfur bacteria (e.g., Rhodospirillum), which use H₂S or organic compounds as electron donors instead of water.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A process that makes important difference between C₃ and C₄ plants is
questions.explanation: Photorespiration is a wasteful process that is prominent in C₃ plants but is virtually absent in C₄ plants due to their CO₂ concentration mechanism. This is a key physiological difference.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The correct sequence of cell organelles during photorespiration is
questions.explanation: Photorespiration involves three organelles sequentially. It starts in chloroplasts (phosphoglycolate formation), moves to peroxisomes (glycolate to glycine conversion), and ends in mitochondria (glycine to serine with CO₂ release).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In Kranz anatomy, the bundle sheath cells have

options.A: Thin walls, no intercellular spaces and several chloroplasts
options.B: Thick walls, many intercellular spaces and few chloroplasts
options.C: Thin walls, many intercellular spaces and no chloroplasts
options.D: Thick walls, no intercellular spaces and large number of chloroplasts

questions.explanation: Bundle sheath cells in C₄ plants are characterized by thick walls (to prevent gas diffusion), lack of intercellular spaces, and the presence of large, often agranal chloroplasts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: CAM helps the plants in
questions.explanation: CAM (Crassulacean Acid Metabolism) is an adaptation in xerophytic plants. Stomata open at night to fix CO₂ into organic acids, conserving water by avoiding daytime transpiration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: PGA as the first CO₂ fixation product was discovered in photosynthesis of
questions.explanation: Melvin Calvin and his colleagues used the green alga Chlorella and radioactive ¹⁴C to trace the path of carbon in photosynthesis, discovering that 3-phosphoglyceric acid (PGA) was the first stable product.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Breakdown of complex molecules to yield energy takes place in
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration in eukaryotes involves glycolysis in the cytoplasm and the Krebs cycle and electron transport chain in the mitochondria.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following substances can undergo oxidation and release energy?
questions.explanation: Organic compounds like carbohydrates, fats, and proteins (organic food) as well as intermediary organic acids (like pyruvate, malate) are broken down via oxidation to release energy during respiration.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In EMP pathway, hexose sugar splits into two molecules of triose sugar by the catalytic activity of
questions.explanation: In the EMP pathway (glycolysis), fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (a 6-carbon compound) is split into two 3-carbon molecules (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate and dihydroxyacetone phosphate) by the enzyme aldolase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Reducing power NADH + H⁺ is produced during conversion step of glycolysis.
questions.explanation: The oxidation of glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate to 1,3-bisphoglyceric acid is coupled with the reduction of NAD⁺ to NADH + H⁺. This is the only step in glycolysis where NADH is produced.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme of glycolysis is also called as pacemaker enzyme?
questions.explanation: Phosphofructokinase (PFK) catalyzes the conversion of fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate. It is the key regulatory and rate-limiting (pacemaker) enzyme of glycolysis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: After glycolysis, first step of ethyl alcohol fermentation requires
questions.explanation: The first step in alcoholic fermentation is the decarboxylation of pyruvic acid (3C) to acetaldehyde (2C) and CO₂, catalyzed by the enzyme pyruvate decarboxylase.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Connecting link between glycolysis and TCA cycle is
questions.explanation: The link reaction (or transition reaction) is the oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetyl CoA, connecting glycolysis in the cytoplasm to the TCA (Krebs) cycle in the mitochondrial matrix.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: How many molecules of CO₂ are released during two turns of Krebs cycle?
questions.explanation: One turn of the Krebs cycle releases 2 molecules of CO₂ (one during isocitrate to α-ketoglutarate, and one during α-ketoglutarate to succinyl-CoA). Therefore, two turns release 4 molecules of CO₂.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The complex concerned with oxidative phosphorylation in inner mitochondrial membrane is
questions.explanation: Complex V is ATP synthase, the enzyme complex responsible for oxidative phosphorylation. It uses the proton gradient generated by the electron transport chain (Complexes I-IV) to synthesize ATP.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Total number of ATP produced through ETS only from one molecule of 3-phosphoglycerate in aerobic respiration is
questions.explanation: 3-phosphoglycerate converts to pyruvate (no ATP gain). Pyruvate yields 4 NADH and 1 FADH₂ via link reaction and Krebs cycle. 4 NADH x 3 ATP = 12 ATP + 1 FADH₂ x 2 ATP = 14 ATP from ETS.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The chemiosmotic coupling hypothesis of oxidative phosphorylation proposes that adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is formed because

options.A: There is a change in the permeability of the inner mitochondrial membrane toward adenosine diphosphate (ADP)
options.B: High energy bonds are formed in mitochondrial proteins
options.C: ADP is pumped out of the matrix into the intermembrane space
options.D: A proton gradient forms across the inner membrane

questions.explanation: The chemiosmotic hypothesis (proposed by Peter Mitchell) states that the energy for ATP synthesis comes from an electrochemical proton gradient (proton motive force) across the inner mitochondrial membrane.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The overall goal of glycolysis, Krebs cycle and the electron transport system is the formation of:

options.C: ATP in one large oxidation reaction

questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is a catabolic process. Its primary goal is to break down glucose step by step, capturing the released energy in the small, manageable packets of ATP molecules.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Growth of an organ is defined as

options.D: Infinite and reversible increase in size

questions.explanation: Growth, in a biological context, is defined as a permanent (irreversible) increase in size, mass, or volume of an organ or organism.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Increase in the girth of plant is known as / done by
questions.explanation: Increase in girth or thickness of plant organs like stems and roots is called secondary growth, which is carried out by lateral meristems (vascular cambium and cork cambium).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A Leaf of 20 cm² grows 5 cm² per hour and B Leaf of 25 cm² grows 5 cm² per hour. The relative growth rate of leaf A and B respectively is
questions.explanation: Relative Growth Rate = (Growth per unit time / Initial size) x 100. For A: (5/20) x 100 = 25%. For B: (5/25) x 100 = 20%.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not a function of water in plants?

options.A: It is responsible for cell elongation
options.B: It maintains the turgidity of cells
options.C: It acts as a source of energy
options.D: It provides medium for enzymatic activities

questions.explanation: Water is crucial for cell elongation, maintaing turgor, and acting as a medium for biochemical reactions, but it is not an energy source. Energy comes from the breakdown of organic compounds like ATP.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is formed after dedifferentiation?
questions.explanation: Dedifferentiation is the process by which permanent (differentiated) cells regain the ability to divide. Interfascicular cambium is formed by the dedifferentiation of parenchyma cells in the medullary rays.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The ability of a plant to follow different pathways and produce different structures in response to environment is known as
questions.explanation: Plasticity is the property of a plant to alter its growth form and development in response to varying environmental conditions, leading to different structures (e.g., heterophylly in aquatic plants).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Two synthetic auxins are
questions.explanation: IAA (Indole-3-acetic acid) and IBA (Indole-3-butyric acid) are natural auxins. 2,4-D (2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid) and NAA (Naphthalene acetic acid) are synthetic auxins widely used in agriculture.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Apical dominance is stimulated by auxin whereas it is inhibited by
questions.explanation: Apical dominance, where the main central stem grows more strongly than side stems, is promoted by auxin from the shoot tip. Cytokins are antagonistic and promote the growth of lateral buds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Select a correct match

options.A: GA₃ – Early seed production in conifers.
options.B: Cytokin – Synchronise fruit set in pineapples.

questions.explanation: GA₃ (gibberellic acid) is known to promote early seed production and flowering in conifers. Ethylene synchronizes fruit set in pineapples. Cytokin overcomes senescence (Richmond-Lang effect).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The hormone which can replace long days and low temperature requirement for flowering in some plants is
questions.explanation: Gibberellins have been shown to substitute for the long-day or low-temperature (vernalization) requirement for flowering in some plants, particularly long-day plants under short-day conditions.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The wavelength of light absorbed by Pr form of phytochrome is
questions.explanation: Phytochrome exists in two interconvertible forms: Pr (red-absorbing form) which absorbs red light maximally at 660 nm, and Pfr (far-red-absorbing form) which absorbs far-red light maximally at 730 nm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: An enzyme that can stimulate germination of barley seeds is
questions.explanation: In germinating barley seeds, gibberellin stimulates the aleurone layer to produce and secrete hydrolytic enzymes like α-amylase, which breaks down starch into sugars to fuel the growth of the embryo.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: 2,4-D is an effective
questions.explanation: 2,4-D (2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid) is a synthetic auxin widely used as a selective herbicide (weedicide) to kill broad-leaved weeds without affecting monocot crops like cereals.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If the apical bud has been removed then we observe
questions.explanation: Removal of the apical bud (decapitation) eliminates the source of auxin that maintains apical dominance. This releases the lateral (axillary) buds from inhibition, leading to the growth of more lateral branches.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Process of reproduction which results in production of identical offsprings is
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction is a fast and simple process involving only mitotic divisions, producing offspring that are genetically identical to the parent (clones).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: For which of the following organisms there is no natural death?

options.D: Unicellular organisms reproducing by binary fission

questions.explanation: Unicellular organisms that reproduce by binary fission do not have a natural death as an individual. The parent cell simply divides into two new daughter cells; it does not die but continues its life in the offspring.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Choose odd one w.r.t. vegetative propagule involved in cultivation in following plants
questions.explanation: Bana, ginger, and potato are propagated vegetatively through modified stems (rhizome, rhizome, and tuber respectively). Bryophyllum is propagated vegetatively through adventious buds on its leaves (leaf propagation).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Chara possess
questions.explanation: Chara (a green alga) has multicellular and jacketed sex organs: the male structure is called globule, and the female structure is called nucule.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In fungi homothallic term is used to represent

options.D: More than one option is correct

questions.explanation: In fungi, homothallic refers to a bisexual condition where both male and female reproductive structures are present on the same thallus, allowing for self-fertilization.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Self fertilisation is seen in
questions.explanation: Self-fertilization occurs when male and female gametes from the same individual fuse. Pea flowers are bisexual and typically undergo self-pollination (cleistogamy in some cases), leading to self-fertilization.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Thick walled resistant zygote can be produced in the life cycle of
questions.explanation: Many algae and fungi produce thick-walled, resistant zygotes (zygospores or oospores) that can survive unfavorable conditions and germinate when conditions become favorable again.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following generates new genetic combinations leading to variation?
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction involves the fusion of male and female gametes from two different parents (or at least meiosis and fertilization), leading to new genetic combinations and variations in offspring.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In ginger vegetative propagation occurs through
questions.explanation: Ginger is propagated vegetatively through its modified underground stem called a rhizome. Each node on the rhizome can give rise to a new shoot and roots.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which one of the following is wrong about Chara?

options.A: Upper oogonium and lower round antheridium
options.B: Globule and nucule present on the same plant

questions.explanation: In monoecious species of Chara, the female reproductive structure (nucule/oogonium) is located above the male reproductive structure (globule/antheridium), not the other way around.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Isogamous condition with non-flagellated gametes is found in
questions.explanation: Spirogyra shows isogamous sexual reproduction with non-motile, non-flagellated gametes that are transported by conjugation tubes. Volvox and Fucus are oogamous; Chlamydomonas can be isogamous, anisogamous, or oogamous with flagellated gametes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A typical angiosperm anther is ___ and ___.
questions.explanation: A typical angiosperm anther is bilobed, and each lobe contains two microsporangia (pollen sacs). Therefore, it is described as bilobed and tetrasporangiate (four sporangia).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The most resistant organic material known which makes up the outermost layer of pollen wall is
questions.explanation: Sporopollenin is one of the most resistant organic materials known. It is a complex polymer of carotenoids and carotenoid esters that makes up the exine (outer layer) of pollen grains, protecting them from harsh environmental conditions and even strong acids and alkalis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In 40% angiosperms, the pollen grains are shed at
questions.explanation: In about 60% of angiosperms, pollen is shed at the two-celled stage (vegetative cell + generative cell). In the remaing 40%, the generative cell divides mitotically before anthesis, so pollen is shed at the three-celled stage (vegetative cell + two male gametes).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The largest cell of the mature embryo sac is
questions.explanation: The central cell of the embryo sac is the largest cell. It contains two polar nuclei (which fuse to form the secondary nucleus) and a large vacuole.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not a characteristic feature of insect pollinated flowers?
questions.explanation: Mucilaginous covering on pollen grains is a characteristic feature of hydrophilous (water-pollinated) plants to protect pollen from water. Fragrance, nectar, and bright colors are features of entomophilous (insect-pollinated) flowers.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The central cell after triple fusion becomes the
questions.explanation: After triple fusion (fusion of one male gamete with the two polar nuclei), the central cell becomes the Primary Endosperm Cell (PEC), which is triploid (3n). The PEC then develops into the endosperm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Double endosperm is found in
questions.explanation: Coconut has a unique "double endosperm." It consists of both liquid endosperm (conut water) and cellular endosperm (the white kernel), which is formed from the nuclear endosperm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Adventive polyembryony is common in
questions.explanation: Adventive polyembryony is a type of apomixis where embryos develop directly from somatic cells of the ovule, such as nucellus or integuments. It is common in citrus and mango, leading to multiple embryos in a single seed.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A dioecious flowering plant prevents both
questions.explanation: Dioecious plants have male and female flowers on separate individuals. This prevents autogamy (pollination within the same flower) and geitonogamy (pollination between different flowers on the same plant). Only xenogamy (cross-pollination) is possible.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Functional megaspore in an angiosperm develops into
questions.explanation: The functional megaspore (the one that survives from the tetrad) undergoes three successive mitotic divisions to form the female gametophyte, which in angiosperms is called the embryo sac (typically 7-celled, 8-nucleate).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Attractants and rewards are required for
questions.explanation: Entomophilous (insect-pollinated) flowers attract pollinators by providing rewards like nectar and pollen, and by using attractants such as bright colors, fragrance, and floral patterns.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Flowers which have single ovule in the ovary and are packed into inflorescence are usually pollinated by
questions.explanation: Wind-pollinated (anemophilous) flowers often have a single ovule per ovary and are produced in large numbers in compact inflorescences (e.g., grasses, cereals) to maximize the chances of pollen capture.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Double fertilization is exhibited by
questions.explanation: Double fertilization, involving both syngamy (fusion of one male gamete with egg) and triple fusion (fusion of the other male gamete with polar nuclei), is a defing and unique characteristic of angiosperms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In majority of angiosperms

options.C: Reduction division occurs in the megaspore mother cells
options.D: A small central cell is present in the embryo sac

questions.explanation: Megasporogenesis, the formation of megaspores from the Megaspore Mother Cell (MMC), involves meiosis (reduction division). This is universal in sexually reproducing angiosperms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The ovule of an angiosperm is technically equivalent to
questions.explanation: An ovule is an integumented structure containg the nucellus (sporangial tissue) within which the megaspore mother cell develops. Therefore, it is technically equivalent to a megasporangium.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mark the odd one (w.r.t. true breeding line)

options.B: Shows expression for few generations only

questions.explanation: A true-breeding line is one that has undergone continuous self-pollination and shows stable trait inheritance and expression for several generations without segregation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is not a dominant trait in edible pea?
questions.explanation: In Mendel's experiments with pea plants, yellow seed color was dominant over green seed color. Green seed color is a recessive trait.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In garden pea, starch is synthesised effectively in

options.D: Pure and hybrid round seed plants

questions.explanation: The gene for seed shape (R) codes for starch branching enzyme. Homozygous dominant (RR) plants produce large, effective starch grains, leading to round seeds. Recessive (rr) plants have defective enzyme, accumulating more sugars and less starch, leading to wrinkled seeds due to osmotic water loss.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Heterozygous round and yellow seed pea plants were selfed and total 800 seeds are collected. What is the total number of seeds with first dominant and second recessive traits?
questions.explanation: For a dihybrid cross (RrYy × RrYy), the phenotypic ratio is 9 (Round Yellow):3 (Round Green):3 (Wrinkled Yellow):1 (Wrinkled Green). "First dominant and second recessive" means Round (dominant) and Green (recessive), which is the 3/16 class. (3/16) × 800 = 150 seeds.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Generation of non-parental gene combinations is termed as
questions.explanation: Recombination refers to the generation of new combinations of alles on a chromosome, different from the parental combinations. This occurs primarily through crossing over during meiosis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which of the sex determination both male and female have same number of chromosomes?
questions.explanation: In the XY type of sex determination (e.g., humans, Drosophila), both males (XY) and females (XX) have the same number of chromosomes. In XO type, males have one less chromosome (XO) than females (XX).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Sickle cell-anaemia disorder arises due to

options.A: Duplication of a segment of DNA
options.B: Substitution in a single base of DNA
options.C: Deletion of a segment of DNA
options.D: Duplication in a base pair of RNA

questions.explanation: Sickle cell anemia is caused by a point mutation (single base substitution) in the gene coding for the β-globin chain of hemoglobin. A single nucleotide change (GAG to GTG) results in glutamic acid being replaced by valine.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Phenylketonuria is an inborn error of metabolism that is inherited as
questions.explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a mutation in the gene for the enzyme phenylanine hydroxylase (located on chromosome 12).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following abnormalities is due to autosomal dominant mutation?
questions.explanation: Myotonic dystrophy is an autosomal dominant disorder. Colour blindness and haemophilia are X-linked recessive. Thalassemia is an autosomal recessive disorder.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Physical, psychomotor and mental development is retarted in an individual affected with
questions.explanation: Down's syndrome (Trisomy 21) is characterized by mental retardation, delayed psychomotor development, and distinct physical features (flat face, protruding tongue, etc.).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which of the following disorder gynaecomastia symptom is seen in individuals?
questions.explanation: Gynaecomastia (development of breasts in males) is a characteristic symptom of Klinefelter's syndrome (47, XXY), along with tall stature, small tes, and infertility.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Thalassemia and sickle cell anemia are caused due to a problem in globin molecule synthesis. Select the correct statement.

options.A: Both are due to a qualitative defect in globin chain synthesis
options.B: Both are due to a quantitative defect in globin chain synthesis
options.C: Thalassemia is due to less synthesis of globin molecules
options.D: Sickle cell anemia is due to a quantitative problem of globin molecules

questions.explanation: Thalassemia is a quantitative problem: reduced or absent synthesis of one of the globin chains (α or β). Sickle cell anemia is a qualitative problem: synthesis of a structurally abnormal globin chain (HbS).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Among the following characters, which one was not considered by Mendel in his experiments on pea?
questions.explanation: Mendel studied seven contrasting traits in pea plants: stem height, flower color, flower position, pod shape, pod color, seed shape, and seed color. The nature of trichomes was not one of his chosen characters.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If a colour-blind man marries a woman who is homozygous for normal colour vision, the probability of their son being colour-blind is
questions.explanation: Colour blindness is X-linked recessive. The man (XᶜY) passes his Xᶜ chromosome to all daughters (who become carriers) and his Y chromosome to all sons. The woman (XX) passes an X chromosome to all children. Sons inherit X (normal) from mother and Y from father, so they are all normal.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a testcross involving F₁ dihybrid flies, more parental-type offspring were produced than the recombinant-type offspring. This indicates

options.A: Both of the characters are controlled by more than one gene
options.B: The two genes are located on two different chromosomes
options.C: Chromosomes failed to separate during meiosis
options.D: The two genes are linked and present on the same chromosome

questions.explanation: When two genes are linked (located close together on the same chromosome), they tend to be inherited together. This results in a higher proportion of parental-type offspring and fewer recombinant-type offspring in a test cross.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A tall true breeding garden pea plant is crossed with a dwarf true breeding garden pea plant. When the F₁ plants were selfed the resulting genotypes were in the ratio of
questions.explanation: Cross: TT (tall) × tt (dwarf) → F₁: Tt (tall). F₁ selfing (Tt × Tt) yields genotypic ratio of 1 TT (homozygous tall) : 2 Tt (heterozygous tall) : 1 tt (dwarf). The phenotypic ratio is 3 tall : 1 dwarf.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Haploid content of human DNA contains
questions.explanation: The human haploid genome contains approximately 3.3 × 10⁹ base pairs (bp) of DNA. This represents the total genetic information in a single set of 23 chromosomes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Adjacent nucleotides in a polynucleotide chain are joined by
questions.explanation: Adjacent nucleotides in a DNA or RNA strand are linked by a phosphodiester bond, which forms between the 3' carbon of one sugar molecule and the 5' carbon of the next sugar molecule via a phosphate group.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Length of DNA in E. coli is
questions.explanation: E. coli has about 4.6 × 10⁶ base pairs. With each base pair contributing 0.34 nm (3.4 × 10⁻⁷ mm), the total length of the DNA is 4.6 × 10⁶ × 3.4 × 10⁻⁷ mm = approximately 1.36 mm.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following radioactive isotopes were utilised for labelling protein and DNA in transduction experiment respectively?
questions.explanation: In the Hershey-Chase experiment (bacteriophage experiment), they used ³⁵S to label protein (sulfur is present in amino acids methionine and cysteine but not in DNA) and ³²P to label DNA (phosphorus is present in the phosphate backbone).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: DNA polymerases catalyse polymerisation of / in
questions.explanation: All known DNA polymerases synthesize new DNA strands in the 5′ → 3′ direction by adding nucleotides to the 3′ hydroxyl (OH) group of the growing strand.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Poly A tail is present in
questions.explanation: Polyadenylation (addition of a poly-A tail) is a post-transcriptional modification specific to eukaryotic mRNA. It occurs at the 3' end and is involved in stability, nuclear export, and translation efficiency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Find out the incorrect match.
questions.explanation: UAG is one of the three stop codons (nonse codons), not a sense codon. Sense codons specify an amino acid; stop codons signal termination of protein synthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: One codon codes for only one amino acid, hence the code is
questions.explanation: The genetic code is unambiguous, meaning that a particular codon always codes for the same specific amino acid (e.g., AUG always codes for methionine).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In lac operon, the regulator gene codes for
questions.explanation: The regulator gene (lac I) codes for an active repressor protein. In the absence of lactose, this repressor binds to the operator and prevents transcription. Lactose (inducer) binds to the repressor, inactivating it.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The non-human model organisms sequenced in Human Genome project were
questions.explanation: The Human Genome Project also sequenced the genomes of several model organisms for comparative purposes, including the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster and the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the technique of DNA fingerprinting digestion of DNA is followed by
questions.explanation: After digestion of DNA with restriction enzymes, the resulting fragments are separated by size using gel electrophoresis before being transferred to a membrane (Southern blotting) and hybridized with a probe.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: DNA-dependent RNA polymerase catalyzes transcription on one strand of the DNA which is called the
questions.explanation: The strand of DNA that is used by RNA polymerase to synthesize a complementary RNA strand is called the template strand (or antisense strand). The other strand is the coding strand.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Identify the correct order of organisation of genetic material from largest to smallest
questions.explanation: Genome (complete set of genetic material) > Chromosome (organized structure of DNA and protein) > Gene (segment of DNA coding for a functional product) > Nucleotide (basic building block).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Satellite DNA is important because it

options.A: Code for enzymes need for DNA replication
options.B: Codes for proteins need in cell cycle
options.C: Shows high degree of polymorphism in population and also the same degree of polymorphism in an individual, which is heritable from parents to children
options.D: Does not code for proteins and is same in all members of the population

questions.explanation: Satellite DNA consists of highly repetive sequences that do not code for proteins. They show high polymorphism (variations in repeat number) between individuals, making them ideal for DNA fingerprinting.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Gene regulation governing lactose operon of E.coli that involves the lac I gene product is

options.A: Feedback inhibition because excess of β-galactosidase can switch off transcription
options.B: Positive and inducible because it can be induced by lactose
options.C: Negative and inducible because repressor protein prevents transcription
options.D: Negative and repressible because repressor protein prevents transcription

questions.explanation: The lac operon is under negative control because the product of the lac I gene (repressor) actively prevents transcription. It is inducible because the presence of lactose (inducer) inactivates the repressor, allowing transcription.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: See the given steps that are related to plant breeding: a. Selection and testing of hybrid b. Selection of parents c. Germplasm collection d. Crossing among selected parents e. Testing and release of new cultivars Select the correct order for these steps
questions.explanation: The correct sequence in a plant breeding programme is: Germplasm collection → Selection of parents → Crossing among selected parents → Selection and testing of hybrids → Testing and release of new cultivars.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: India is mainly an agricultural country. Agriculture accounts for approximately ___ of India's GDP.
questions.explanation: As per the information in the text, agriculture accounts for approximately 33% of India's GDP and employs about 62% of the population.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Select the incorrect statement(s) (w.r.t. semi-dwarf varieties of wheat)

options.B: Developed at International Centre for Wheat and Maize Improvement in Mexico
options.C: These varieties were derived from IR-8

questions.explanation: Semi-dwarf wheat varieties (like Sonora-64 and Lerma Rojo-64) were developed by Norman Borlaug in Mexico and were responsible for the Green Revolution in India. IR-8 is a semi-dwarf variety of rice, not wheat.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: In mung bean resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew were introduced by
questions.explanation: According to the text, resistance to yellow mosaic virus and powdery mildew in mung bean was introduced through mutation breeding.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Select the variety developed by mutation breeding
questions.explanation: Sharbati Sonora was developed from the Mexican wheat variety Sonora-64 through gamma ray-induced mutation breeding. The others were developed through hybridization.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Insect resistance in host crop may be due to

options.A: Physiological but not due to morphological character
options.C: Biochemical but not due to morphological character
options.D: Morphological but not due to biochemical character

questions.explanation: Insect resistance in plants can be due to morphological characters (e.g., hairy leaves in cotton), biochemical characters (e.g., production of toxins), or physiological characters (e.g., tolerance to damage).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Smooth leaved and nectarless cotton varieties do not attract
questions.explanation: Smooth leaves and absence of nectar in cotton varieties are morphological features that do not attract bollworms, providing resistance against this insect pest.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Atlas-66 is

options.B: Wheat variety with high protein content
options.C: Brassica variety with resistance against aphids
options.D: Cowpea variety with resistance against bacterial blight

questions.explanation: Atlas-66 is a variety of wheat that has been developed for its high protein content, contributing to biofortification efforts.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: What is meant by single cell protein?

options.B: Unique protein synthesised by liver cell
options.D: Unique protein synthesised by plant body

questions.explanation: Single Cell Protein (SCP) refers to the protein-rich biomass of microorganisms (such as algae, fungi, or bacteria) that can be used as a food or feed supplement.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: An explant is

options.C: Part of the plant that expresses a specific gene
options.D: Part of the plant used in tissue culture

questions.explanation: An explant is any part of a plant (e.g., leaf, stem, root, meristem) taken out and grown in a sterile culture medium under controlled conditions in tissue culture.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: Somatic hybridisation is accomplished by

options.B: Fusion of two protoplast of different varieties of plants

questions.explanation: Somatic hybridization involves the fusion of isolated protoplasts (cells without cell walls) from two different plant varieties or species to form a somatic hybrid cell, which can then be regenerated into a hybrid plant.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: A technique of micropagation is
questions.explanation: Somatic embryogenesis, the development of embryos from somatic (vegetative) cells in culture, is a key technique used in micropagation for large-scale clonal multiplication of plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.chapter: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.topic: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production
questions.question: To obtain virus-free healthy plants from a diseased one by tissue culture technique, which part/parts of the diseased plant will be taken?
questions.explanation: Apical and axillary meristems are typically free of viruses even in infected plants. Culturing these meristems is a standard technique for producing virus-free plants through micropagation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following microbe is made up of protein only?
questions.explanation: Prions are infectious agents composed entirely of protein, with no nucleic acid. Viruses contain protein + nucleic acid, viroids are RNA only, and bacteria are cellular organisms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the beneficial role of LAB in our stomach?

options.A: Causes souring of milk by decreasing nutritional quality
options.D: It produces alkali which coagulate and partially digest the milk proteins

questions.explanation: Lactic Acid Bacteria (LAB) present in our stomach play a beneficial role by creating an acidic environment that checks the growth of disease-causing microbes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The microbe used for making bread is
questions.explanation: Saccharomyces cerevisiae (baker's yeast) is used in bread-making for fermentation, producing carbon dioxide that causes the dough to rise.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following microbe is used for ripening of Swiss cheese?
questions.explanation: Propionibacterium sharmanii is used in the ripening of Swiss cheese. It produces propionic acid and carbon dioxide, which gives Swiss cheese its characteristic flavor and large holes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Production of beverages at industrial scale requires growing microbes in very large containers known as
questions.explanation: Fermentors (or bioreactors) are large, sterile vessels designed for the industrial-scale cultivation of microorganisms to produce products like beverages, antibiotics, and enzymes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The chemicals which are "pro-life" with reference to human beings

options.A: Are regarded as one of the most significant discovery of twenty first century
options.B: Have rarely contributed to human welfare
options.C: Are produced by some plants and all animals
options.D: Can kill or retard the growth of disease-causing microbes

questions.explanation: The term "pro-life" in this context refers to antibiotics, which are chemicals produced by microorganisms that can kill or inhibit the growth of other disease-causing microbes, thereby saving lives.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first antibiotic was discovered accidently by A while working on B.
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming accidentally discovered penicillin in 1928 while working on Staphylocci bacteria. He noticed that a mold (Penicillium notatum) contaminating his culture plates had inhibited bacterial growth.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Match the following (column-I with column-II) Column I (Microbes) | Column II (Organic acid) a. Aspergillus niger | i. Butyric acid b. Clostridium butylicum | ii. Citric acid c. Acetobacter aceti | iii. Lactic acid d. Lactobacillus | iv. Acetic acid
questions.explanation: The correct matches are: Aspergillus niger produces citric acid; Clostridium butylicum produces butyric acid; Acetobacter aceti produces acetic acid; Lactobacillus produces lactic acid.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The product of Monascus pureus has been commercialised as
questions.explanation: Monascus pureus (a yeast) produces statins, which are blood-cholesterol-lowering agents. They work by competively inhibiting the enzyme responsible for cholesterol synthesis.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a sewage treatment plant, primary treatment is

options.A: Physical process which involves sedimentation only
options.B: Physical process which involves both filtration and sedimentation
options.C: Biological process which involves formation of primary sludge and effluent
options.D: Biological process which involves both filtration and sedimentation

questions.explanation: Primary treatment of sewage is a physical process that involves sequential filtration to remove large particles and sedimentation in settling tanks to remove suspended solids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What are flocs?

options.C: Masses of anaerobic bacteria and fungi
options.D: Masses of aerobic bacteria associated with fungal filaments

questions.explanation: Flocs are masses of aerobic bacteria associated with fungal filaments that form during the secondary treatment (aeration) of sewage. They help in consuming organic matter in the effluent.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to activated sludge?

options.A: It is generally released into natural water bodies like rivers and streams
options.B: It is completely pumped back into aeration tank to serve as inoculum
options.C: The major part of the sludge is pumped into large tanks called anaerobic sludge digesters

questions.explanation: A small part of activated sludge is pumped back into the aeration tank as inoculum. The major part is sent to anaerobic sludge digesters for further treatment and biogas production.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the composition of the biogas?

options.B: Methane, carbon dioxide, hydrogen, nitrogen dioxide etc.
options.D: Methane, carbon dioxide, sulphur dioxide etc.

questions.explanation: Biogas is a mixture of gases primarily composed of methane (50-70%), carbon dioxide (30-40%), and small amounts of hydrogen, hydrogen sulfide, and other gases.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The bacterium responsible for biogas production are collectively called
questions.explanation: Methanogens are anaerobic archaea that produce methane as a metabolic byproduct. They are responsible for the generation of biogas during the anaerobic digestion of organic matter.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The effective biocontrol agents of several plant pathogens are species of
questions.explanation: Trichoderma species are free-living fungi that are effective biocontrol agents against several soil-borne plant pathogens. They are commonly found in root ecosystems.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In paddy fields, __ serves as an important biofertiliser.
questions.explanation: Blue-Green Algae (BGA) or cyanobacteria (like Nostoc, Anabaena) are important biofertilizers in paddy fields as they fix atmospheric nitrogen, enriching the soil.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following in sewage treatment removes suspended solids?
questions.explanation: Primary treatment is a physical process involving filtration and sedimentation to remove floating and suspended solids from sewage.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is correctly matched for the product produced by them?
